**What this layout buys you:** real-time delivery with durable history and a clean path for offline users. The tradeoff is that connection state becomes part of your architecture, which makes deployments and failover more stateful than normal HTTP services.

#### Data Model
- **messages** (wide-column store, partition by chat_id):
  `(chat_id, msg_id (Snowflake/timeuuid), sender_id, body, state)`
- **chat_members** (wide-column store): `(chat_id, user_id, joined_at, last_read_msg_id)`
- **user_session** (fast in-memory cache): `user_id → chat_server_id`, TTL 60s, refreshed on heartbeat
- **presence** (fast in-memory cache): `user_id → online|offline|typing`, TTL 30s

#### Detailed Design
**Transport.** WebSocket because it's full-duplex, low-framing-overhead, and supports server-pushed messages without the client polling. Fall back to long-polling for hostile networks (corporate proxies that strip Upgrade headers, restrictive captive portals) — same protocol over HTTP keepalive, ~10x more overhead per message but functional. Heartbeat ping every 30s keeps NAT traversal alive and gives the server a way to detect silently dead connections; missing 2 heartbeats triggers cleanup.

**Message ordering.** Per-conversation Snowflake sequence number assigned by the persist service before fan-out, so every recipient sees the same monotonic order regardless of which chat server delivers their copy. Snowflake (timestamp + machine_id + counter) gives roughly time-sorted IDs without needing a global coordinator. Within Cassandra the partition is `chat_id` and the clustering key is `msg_id`, so a single read returns messages in send order. Cross-conversation ordering is not preserved and not needed — group chat A and DM B can interleave however the network allows.

**Group chat.** A message destined for a group of N members becomes N entries on the message bus (one per recipient), each routed to the chat server hosting that recipient's WebSocket. The persist service does the fan-out under one global `msg_id` so all N recipients see identical ordering. Cap at ~500 members because beyond that, group fan-out cost dominates and the product is closer to a broadcast channel — switch to a different model (channels with separate read-side fan-out) for those cases. Example: a 500-person work chat at 10 msgs/min generates 5000 bus entries/min — comfortable.

**E2E (Signal protocol).**
- Each user has long-term identity key + ephemeral (one-time, prekey) keys, generated client-side and pushed to the server only as public halves
- Sender encrypts per-recipient using a Double Ratchet session (forward secrecy + post-compromise security); group chat = per-member encryption (Sender Keys protocol amortizes the cost)
- Server stores ciphertext only — even an attacker with full DB access can't read message content; metadata (who-talked-to-whom-when) remains visible and is the main residual leak

**Offline handling.**
- Persist message to the wide-column store before acking the sender, so the message is durable regardless of recipient state
- Push notif via APNs/FCM for offline recipient — external dependency on platform providers, rate-limited and unreliable, so treat as best-effort wakeup not delivery confirmation
- On reconnect, client sends its `last_read_msg_id`; server streams every message since that cursor over the WebSocket. Clients must handle out-of-order arrival during this catch-up phase (sort locally by `msg_id`)

**Retention and out-of-scope items.** History retention is per-chat TTL: hot 30 days on SSD-backed wide-column store, older buckets fall to spinning disk / object-store snapshots. The user can pin a chat to keep it on the hot tier indefinitely (paid tier) or set a per-chat ephemeral TTL (e.g. 24h disappearing messages — the persist service stamps an expiry on each message and a sweeper deletes after expiry). Voice/video calls are explicitly out of scope here; they ride a separate WebRTC signaling path that *reuses* this system's session registry to find the callee but does not flow audio/video through the message bus. The chat system delivers the call invite as a control message; actual media goes peer-to-peer or through a TURN/SFU relay.

**Message delivery state machine.** Modern chat clients render four user-visible states; each transition is a separate ack from a different actor.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Sending: user hits send
    Sending --> Sent: server persists + assigns msg_id
    Sent --> Delivered: recipient device acks receipt
    Delivered --> Read: recipient opens chat
    Sending --> Failed: timeout / network error
    Failed --> Sending: retry (exp backoff)
    Read --> [*]
```

The "sent" tick (✓) means the server has the message durably; "delivered" (✓✓) means the recipient device received it but hasn't surfaced it; "read" (✓✓ blue) requires recipient client to explicitly emit a read receipt. WhatsApp delays delivered/read receipts until the user opens the app to save battery — so receipts often arrive in batches.

**Online vs offline message paths — sequence comparison.**

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Sender
    participant API as Chat API
    participant DB as Cassandra
    participant Bus as Kafka
    participant CS as Chat Server
    participant B as Recipient (online)
    participant APNs as APNs/FCM

    rect rgb(220,240,220)
        Note over A,B: ONLINE PATH (~50ms p99)
        A->>API: send(msg)
        API->>DB: persist + assign msg_id
        API-->>A: ack(msg_id) [✓ sent]
        API->>Bus: enqueue(msg, recipient)
        Bus->>CS: route by recipient_id
        CS->>B: WS push
        B-->>CS: ack(delivered) [✓✓]
    end

    rect rgb(240,220,220)
        Note over A,APNs: OFFLINE PATH (best-effort)
        A->>API: send(msg)
        API->>DB: persist
        API-->>A: ack [✓ sent]
        API->>APNs: push notif (best-effort wakeup)
        APNs-->>B: notif (rate-limited, lossy)
        Note over B: user later opens app
        B->>CS: reconnect WS + last_read_msg_id
        CS->>DB: stream missed messages
        CS->>B: deliver backlog [✓✓ in batch]
    end
```

**Connection density.** *[Source: WhatsApp/Erlang scaling, highscalability.com]* WhatsApp famously pushed a single FreeBSD/Erlang server to **2M concurrent TCP connections** (peak tested at 2.8M before diminishing returns). Total infra: >8000 cores sustaining ~70M Erlang messages/sec. Per-server tuning required custom BEAM patches and FreeBSD kernel work — the scale is achievable but not free. Discord runs Erlang for its gateway tier for the same reason: cheap concurrent processes (millions per node) make WS-per-user practical.

**Discord storage migration.** *[Source: Discord engineering, 2022]* Discord stores **trillions of messages**; ran on Cassandra (177 nodes) until hot partitions caused latency cascades. Migrated to ScyllaDB (C++, no GC) — node count dropped 177 → 72; tail-latency for historical fetches went from 40-125ms → 15ms p99. Key win: a Rust intermediary layer added **request coalescing** (single in-flight DB query for identical concurrent requests). Partitioning is `(channel_id, time_bucket)` with bucket size tuned to keep partitions under ~100MB — a 100k-member server with 100k msgs/day uses 1-day buckets; a 5-member DM uses 1-year buckets.

**Group fan-out cost.** A 500-member group at 10 msgs/min generates 5000 bus entries/min — bounded. A 100k-member "broadcast" group at 10 msgs/min would be 1M bus entries/min — switch product model to channels with read-side fan-out (subscriber pulls from a channel partition rather than receiving N copies).

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: What if a chat server holding 1M WebSockets dies?**
Clients detect WS disconnect and reconnect with jittered backoff to a new server (load balancer reroutes). Session registry in Redis is updated by the new server's connect handler. Worst case: ~30s of unread messages, delivered on reconnect via `last_read_msg_id` cursor. *If pushed:* connection draining on planned restarts (close gradually over 5min); for unplanned, surviving servers absorb the load — provision for 1.5x normal capacity.

**Q: How do you guarantee message ordering in a group chat?**
Per-conversation Snowflake sequence ID assigned by the persist service before fan-out — every recipient sees the same monotonic order. Within Cassandra, partition is `chat_id`, clustering key is `msg_id`. *If pushed:* if persist throughput is the bottleneck, shard chats across multiple persist instances by `chat_id` — strict ordering only required within a chat, not across.

**Q: How do you handle a celebrity group chat (5000 members, 100 msg/s)?**
Cap groups (typically ~500). For larger broadcast-style use cases, switch model to channels with separate read-fanout. *If pushed:* use Kafka with a per-group topic; subscribers (chat servers) pull at their own rate, decoupling slow recipients from fast publishers.

**Q: Push vs WebSocket — when do you use which?**
WebSocket while app is foregrounded (low latency, bidirectional, presence). APNs/FCM push for background — OS won't let you keep a socket open. Crossover handled by client telling server "I'm backgrounding, route via push now". *If pushed:* hybrid for low-end devices — always push, with WS only for active conversations to save battery.

**Q: How would you add E2E encryption without breaking history sync?**
Signal protocol — per-recipient session keys; server stores ciphertext only. New device fetches encrypted history and decrypts using device key transferred via QR scan or trusted-device approval. *If pushed:* tradeoff — true E2E breaks server-side search; offer client-side full-text index that ships with each device. WhatsApp does this.

**Q: How do you detect and prevent spam/abuse?**
Rate limit per sender (msgs/s, recipients/day), report flow, and ML signal on metadata (graph patterns, message frequency, link reputation) — content can't be inspected if E2E. *If pushed:* hash-and-report — clients can flag a message and reveal the ciphertext key to platform, which then decrypts only that specific message for review.


**Q: How did Discord get from Cassandra to ScyllaDB and what changed?**
Hot partitions on Cassandra caused latency cascades; large servers (100k+ users) skewed traffic 1000x against small DM partitions. Compaction backlogs and JVM GC pauses meant manual intervention ("the gossip dance"). ScyllaDB is C++ with no GC and a shard-per-core model; same data model, dramatically less ops pain — 177 → 72 nodes, p99 read 40-125ms → 15ms. *If pushed:* the bigger insight was building a Rust data-services layer in front of the DB with **request coalescing** — when 1000 users open the same channel simultaneously, only 1 query hits the DB, not 1000.

**Q: How does WhatsApp/Erlang get 2M connections per server?**
Erlang processes are ~2KB each, GC-isolated, and the BEAM scheduler distributes them across cores cheaply. WhatsApp added custom BEAM patches and FreeBSD kernel tuning. The lesson is architectural: stateful long-lived connections at this density only work on runtimes built for cheap concurrency (Erlang, Go goroutines) — Java/Node hit OS-thread or eventloop limits long before. *If pushed:* Discord uses Elixir (BEAM) for its gateway specifically for this — message-passing per user, no shared state.

**Q: Why batch read/delivery receipts instead of real-time?**
Battery and bandwidth. A 500-member group where everyone reads in 5 seconds = 500×500 receipt events. Clients debounce locally (one receipt per chat per 5s window) and many chat apps only send "read" when the user actually opens the app, not in the background. *If pushed:* you can also coalesce server-side — the persist service compacts read receipts before fan-out to other members.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Connection storm after server restart** → stagger reconnects with jitter. A server holding 1M WebSockets dies, all 1M clients reconnect immediately; surviving servers get hit with a thundering herd that can cascade. Client-side jittered backoff (0-30s random) smears the reconnects over time.
- **Hot group (1000s of msgs)** → chunked fan-out via Kafka. A 500-member group at 100 msgs/s generates 50k bus messages/s; Kafka per-group topic lets subscribers pull at their own rate, isolating slow consumers from fast publishers and absorbing bursts in the broker.
- **History query at scale** → time-bucket partitioning, TTL on old chats. Cassandra partitions per `chat_id` get unboundedly large for active chats; bucketing by `(chat_id, year_month)` keeps partitions small and lets TTL drop old buckets cheaply. Default 1-year TTL on free tier.
- **Mobile battery drain** → push-via-APNs in background; WS only when foreground. Holding a WebSocket open in background drains 10-20% per day; OS will kill the process anyway. Foreground = WS for low-latency presence/typing, background = APNs wakeup with lazy WS reconnect for active conversation only.
- **Sticky routing breaks on rebalance** → drain mode + reconnect to new node. Adding a new chat server requires reshuffling some user→server mappings; brute-force reassignment forces mass disconnects. Drain mode closes connections gradually over 5min, clients reconnect at their own pace, LB routes to the new server based on updated hash ring.

- **Hot partitions in wide-column store** → tune time-bucket size per partition load, add request coalescing in front. Discord's 100k-member servers hit 1000x the traffic of a 5-person DM on the same Cassandra cluster; per-channel time-bucket sizing (1-day for hot, 1-year for cold) plus a Rust coalescing layer cut DB QPS by 10x for trending channels.
- **JVM GC pauses on storage tier** → consider C++/Rust storage (ScyllaDB) for tail-sensitive workloads. Cassandra GC pauses caused unpredictable p99 spikes; ScyllaDB's no-GC C++ shard-per-core architecture removed the variable. Worth the migration cost only above a scale threshold (Discord did it at 177 Cassandra nodes).
- **Connection density on a single box** → use a runtime built for cheap concurrency (Erlang/BEAM, Go). 2M WS connections per server only works on Erlang-class runtimes; Java/Node fall over at 100k. Pick the platform before you architect.

#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| WebSocket gateway | Chat server crashes and drops thousands of sticky sockets | Connection drop spike and reconnect storm metrics | Client reconnects with jitter, session registry is rewritten on reconnect, and backlog is replayed from durable history |
| Session registry | `user_id → server_id` entry is stale, so messages route to a dead server | Heartbeat-expiry mismatch and unroutable-recipient counter | TTL-based registry entries, frequent heartbeats, and fallback to offline queue / push path |
| Durable store | Persist-before-ack write fails or stalls | Message-write p99 and error-rate alarm | Do not ack sender until durable write succeeds; surface retry to client rather than risking ghost messages |
| Inter-server bus | Recipient partition lags or a broker becomes unavailable | Consumer-lag SLO and undelivered-online-message counter | Replay from bus or store, apply backpressure, and degrade to delayed delivery without losing durability |
| Push provider | Offline wakeup push fails repeatedly | APNs/FCM error rate and offline backlog growth | Treat push as best-effort wakeup only; messages remain durable for reconnect replay |
| Receipt pipeline | Read/delivery receipts storm large groups | Receipt queue depth and fan-out latency spike | Batch and debounce receipts, especially in large groups, and keep them off the critical send path |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Send-to-ack latency** — sender-perceived durability SLO; should stay low even under group fan-out.
- **End-to-end online delivery latency** — durable write to recipient WebSocket push for active users.
- **Offline backlog depth** per user / chat — rising backlog indicates routing or reconnect problems.
- **Active WebSocket connections per node** — essential for capacity planning and reconnect-storm detection.
- **Bus lag per recipient partition** — shows when cross-server routing is falling behind before users complain.
- **Receipt latency** (delivered/read) — secondary UX metric that often reveals mobile wakeup problems.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** messages are durably stored in the user's home region and asynchronously replicated to DR; connection gateways are region-local and disposable.
- **RTO:** seconds to minutes for gateway-node failure, bounded by reconnect jitter; longer for full-region failover, gated by message-store promotion.
- **RPO:** near-zero for accepted messages if the durable store uses quorum writes; sockets themselves are ephemeral and fully lost on failover.
- **Failover cadence:** test both gateway reconnect storms and store-level replay from a cold region.
- **Cross-region cost:** high if you try to home one chat globally; most systems region-home users or conversations and pay inter-region only for remote recipients.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **Acking before durability.** The sender seeing “sent” must mean the message exists durably, not merely that one server process accepted it.
- **Direct chat-server to chat-server coupling.** A bus or recipient-routed stream is cleaner and survives rebalance better than ad hoc point-to-point routing.
- **Demanding global ordering.** Chat only needs ordering within a conversation, not across the whole product.
- **Treating presence as durable money-grade state.** Presence is ephemeral and TTL-based; over-engineering it into the durable path adds cost without value.
- **Using the same path for 1:1, group, and broadcast channels.** Very large groups want a different fan-out model.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Persist before ack** is the first thing to say; it sets the delivery contract correctly.
- **Sticky WebSockets simplify connection state.** You pay reconnect complexity to avoid distributed connection handoff on every message.
- **Recipient routing goes through a bus, not direct peer calls.** That is the scaleable inter-server pattern.
- **Ordering is per chat.** A per-conversation sequence is enough and far cheaper than global serialization.
- **Offline replay is cursor-based.** The reconnect story is “give me everything since last seen message.”
- **Push notifications are wakeups, not delivery guarantees.** The durable store is what actually guarantees message availability.

### 10. Design a Search Autocomplete (Google Suggest)
#### Problem
Suggest top queries as the user types, ranked by popularity, with sub-100ms response times.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a library reference desk where the librarian has memorized the 10 most popular books for every possible opening phrase of a question. You say "how do I bake" and without consulting any catalog she immediately says "chocolate cake, sourdough bread, banana bread." She built that knowledge over years of watching what people ask — but she updates her notes every hour. At the desk itself, no searching happens — just recall.

**The single-request walkthrough:** a user types "wea" (three keystrokes). Their browser fires `GET /suggest?q=wea`. The request first hits a CDN edge node — a geographically close cache server (like Cloudflare in a nearby city). The CDN has "wea" cached (short prefixes are requested millions of times a day by millions of users, so they are always in cache). The CDN returns `["weather", "weather today", "wearable tech"]` in under 10ms without touching your servers. If the CDN misses — say the user types "weathe" and this particular 6-character prefix is rarer — the request reaches a serving node. The serving node has an in-memory trie loaded from the last hourly snapshot. A trie is a tree where each node is a character: "w" → "e" → "a" → "t" → "h" → "e" is the path for "weathe". Every node in the trie pre-stores its top-10 suggestions. The node for "weathe" already has `["weather", "weather app", "weather forecast"]` attached. The serving node reads those three entries and returns them. No scanning, no sorting — just a lookup.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Trie (a prefix tree with precomputed top-K at every node)** — a trie is a tree where each edge is a character. The path from root to any node spells a prefix. The crucial addition: every node stores the top-10 queries that start with that prefix (precomputed at build time). A lookup walks the path character-by-character and reads the precomputed list at the final node. O(length of prefix) total — no subtree scan.
- **Hourly batch build pipeline** — every hour, a Spark job reads the last hour of query logs (10 billion queries per day = ~416 million per hour), aggregates query frequency, builds the trie bottom-up (children first, then each parent computes its top-10 from its children's top-10 lists), compresses it to a Finite State Transducer (an FST — a compact representation that shares common suffixes between words, cutting size 5–10x), and uploads the snapshot to object storage. The build is expensive but offline — users never wait for it.
- **FST (Finite State Transducer) for production serving** — a naive trie using hashmaps takes ~60GB for 100 million queries. An FST exploits the fact that words like "running," "jumping," "walking" all share the "-ing" suffix — that suffix path is stored once. An FST cuts the same data to ~6–12GB, fitting on a single server. The tradeoff: FSTs are immutable, but since we rebuild every hour anyway, mutability is not needed.
- **CDN for short prefixes** — autocomplete queries for "t", "th", "the" are issued by millions of users every minute. The suggestions for those prefixes are identical for all users (ignoring personalization). A CDN caches the response and serves it from hundreds of edge locations worldwide. About 95% of autocomplete QPS resolves at the CDN with no backend involvement.
- **Mini-trie for real-time trends** — hourly builds miss breaking news. If "earthquake california" starts trending at 2:47pm, it will not appear in suggestions until the 3pm snapshot. A streaming aggregator (Flink) builds a small additional trie every 60 seconds from the last hour of query logs. At serve time, the serving node merges results from the base hourly trie and the mini-trie. Cost: ~50–100MB of extra RAM per serving node.
- **Personal history merge** — the global trie is shared across all users. Personalization is a small layer on top: fetch the user's last 50 queries from a Redis cache, merge the top 3 personal matches with the top 7 global matches, return the blended list. The trie itself never contains per-user data.

**The thing that makes it hard:** some prefixes receive hundreds of times more traffic than others. The prefix "t" alone might get 2 million queries per second globally — every English word starting with "t" generates traffic on that node. Sharding the trie by prefix (shard 1 handles "a*", shard 2 handles "b*") means the "t" shard gets overwhelmed while the "z" shard is idle. Without mitigation, the "t" shard becomes the bottleneck for the entire system. The fix: replicate hot shards aggressively. Since the trie is read-only between rebuilds, adding 10 replicas of the "t" shard is trivially cheap — just load the same snapshot into 10 servers. The CDN absorbs the bulk of single-character prefix traffic anyway.

**Why the standard solution works:** moving ranking and aggregation to the build pipeline eliminates the need for any complex computation on the hot path. At 20 million queries per second, even a 1-millisecond computation per query would require 20,000 CPU cores just for aggregation. The serving path reduces to: walk a few characters in a trie, read a precomputed list. That is fast enough that the network round-trip dominates latency, not the computation.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Build pipeline: Spark hourly job on S3 query logs → aggregated frequencies → FST snapshot built with the `dawgdic` or `levenshtein_automata` library → snapshot uploaded to S3 → serving nodes pull and do an atomic file swap.
- Serving node: in-process FST loaded in RAM; personal history from Redis.
- CDN: Cloudflare with `Cache-Control: public, max-age=60` on suggest responses (1-minute TTL balances freshness with edge hit rate).
- Hot path pseudocode:
  ```
  def suggest(prefix, user_id):
    base_results = fst.lookup(prefix)[:10]       # in-memory, ~0.1ms
    mini_results = mini_trie.lookup(prefix)[:5]  # streaming overlay
    personal = redis.get(f"history:{user_id}")[:3]
    return merge_and_rank(base_results, mini_results, personal)[:10]
  ```
#### Clarifying Questions
- Personalization?
- Typo tolerance?
- Multilingual?
- How often does suggestion list refresh? (real-time vs hourly)
- Number of suggestions per response (5? 10?)

#### Requirements
- **FR:** return top-k suggestions for prefix; rank by frequency × recency
- **NFR:** <50ms p99; updated within hours; scale globally

#### Scale Estimate
- **Search volume:** Google public stat ~99k searches/s = ~8.5B/day; round to 10B searches/day for headroom → 10B raw queries/day to ingest into the build pipeline.
- **Autocomplete QPS:** typing flow per query — ~20 keystrokes avg, with a ~150ms debounce that collapses ~2 keystrokes per network call → ~10 calls/query; some users abandon mid-type → effective ~5 calls/query (debounce halves it again on slower networks). 10B queries/day × ~50 calls/query (worst-case desktop with no debounce) = ~500B calls/day; 500B ÷ 86,400 ≈ ~6M QPS steady; daytime peak (US+EU overlap) ~3× → ~20M QPS.
- **Suggestion payload:** ~10 suggestions per response × ~30B each (text ~25B + float score 4B + JSON framing) = ~300B per response — sub-MTU and CDN-cacheable on `(prefix, locale)`.
- **Trie size in RAM:** keep top ~100M unique queries (long tail beyond that has no real signal); avg query ~20 chars; trie node ~30B (children pointer map + top-K refs + score). 100M × 20 × 30B = ~60GB raw; FST/double-array compression cuts ~5–10× → ~6–12GB per shard.
- **Snapshot size:** rebuild hourly → ~60GB raw snapshot/hour written to object store; replication factor 3 → ~180GB stored per hour; retain 24h for rollback = ~4.3TB hot snapshots.
- **Cold archive:** daily query-frequency aggregates compress hard (high duplication, columnar ints) → ~10GB/day Parquet × 365 = ~3.6TB/yr — cheap to keep indefinitely for trend analysis and seasonal models.
- **Hot vs cold:** trie shards live in RAM (sub-ms p99 lookup); raw query logs flow through a 24h hot stream (Kafka) before the hourly build job aggregates them into the trie and drops the raw logs to cold object store.

#### API Contract
```
GET /suggest?q={prefix}&limit=10
  → { suggestions: [{text, score}], latency_ms }
```

#### High-Level Design
```
Build pipeline (offline, hourly):
  Query logs ─→ partitioned event stream ─→ Aggregator ─→ Top-K builder ─→ Trie ─→ Snapshot to object store
                                                                    │
                                                          ┌─────────┴─────────┐
                                                          ↓                   ↓
Serve path (online):                                  Trie Shard 1 ...  Trie Shard N
  Client ─→ CDN (popular prefixes) ─miss→ App ──prefix-route──→  shard
              │                                                      ↓
              └─cache────────────────────────────  return top-k from prefix node
```

**How to read the diagram:** almost all the heavy work happens before a user types anything. Batch jobs build an in-memory trie from past query logs, and serving nodes answer a request by walking that trie and returning the precomputed top suggestions for the prefix.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** autocomplete traffic arrives one keystroke at a time, which means query-time scanning is a non-starter. The system moves ranking and aggregation offline so the serving path only does a tiny amount of prefix lookup work.

**What this layout buys you:** very low latency and predictable serving cost even at huge QPS. The tradeoff is freshness: if trends change faster than the rebuild cadence, you need a small delta path beside the main snapshot pipeline.

#### Data Model
**Trie node** (in-memory):
```
struct Node {
  children: map<char, Node>     # or array[26]
  top_k: list<(query, score)>   # cached top suggestions for this prefix
}
```

Each node stores its precomputed top-k → no traversal needed at serve time.

#### Detailed Design
**Why cache top-k at every node:**
- Lookup = hash to node → return top_k → done. O(prefix_length) total.
- Without cache, you'd traverse subtree at request time → too slow.

**Build process:**
1. Aggregate query frequencies from logs (hourly)
2. Build trie bottom-up
3. At each node, compute top-k from union of children's top-k
4. Snapshot trie → push to all serving nodes → atomic swap

**Sharding.** Partition the trie by prefix — first 1-2 chars determine shard. Shard 1 owns the "a*" subtree, shard 2 owns "b*", etc. Routing is trivial (hash on prefix prefix), and each shard's working set is bounded. The downside is density skew: English queries cluster heavily on "t" (the, to, today), "h" (how, here), "w" (what, where) — those shards see 10x the QPS of "z" or "x".
- Hot shards (e.g., "h", "the") → replicate aggressively (5-10x replication factor) so the QPS spreads across replicas. Cheap because the trie is read-only between rebuilds.
- Skewed beyond replication-fix → switch to hash-based sharding, which gives uniform load but scrambles locality (you lose the "scan a subtree from one node" property; less of a concern when each query is just a hashmap lookup anyway).

**Personalization.** Keep the global trie shared across all users — putting per-user data in the trie would explode storage. At request time, fetch the user's recent query history (Redis, last 50 queries with timestamps) and merge with the global top-K: typically top-3 personal slots + top-7 global. Tiebreakers favor personal matches if the global score is close. Example: a user who searches "kotlin coroutines" daily sees that as the top suggestion for "kot" even when global trends point at "kotaku news".

**Typo tolerance.** Three options, each with a different trade-off. (1) Pre-expand: at trie-build time, generate edit-distance-1 variants of each top query and add them as separate trie entries — explodes trie size 5-10x but no runtime cost. (2) BK-tree side-index: a separate metric tree of common queries supports fuzzy lookup when the prefix has zero hits — adds 50-100ms latency on the fallback path. (3) Punt entirely: let the user submit the typo'd query and the search engine's "did you mean" handle correction post-submit — fastest, loses some autocomplete utility. Google does (3); most ecommerce search does (1).

**Multilingual.** Build one trie per language (or per locale, where regional variants matter — pt-BR vs pt-PT). The serving stack picks the trie based on the user's `Accept-Language` header or explicit locale setting; CJK languages (Chinese, Japanese, Korean) need pre-tokenization since they don't have spaces — input is converted to pinyin/romaji or segmented into character n-grams before trie lookup. Storage cost is roughly linear in number of locales because each language has its own popular-query distribution; sharing a single global trie would mix scripts and noise rankings. Mixed-language queries (English brand names typed in a Spanish session) are handled by querying the user's primary trie first and falling back to a secondary English trie if zero hits.


**Build pipeline vs serving path — visual.** Build is offline batch; serve is hot path with strict p99.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Build["BUILD (hourly batch, offline)"]
        L[Query logs<br/>Kafka stream] --> Agg[Aggregator<br/>Spark/Flink]
        Agg --> Top[Top-K per prefix<br/>bottom-up roll-up]
        Top --> FST[Compile to FST<br/>5-10x compression]
        FST --> S3[Snapshot to S3]
        S3 --> Push[Push to all<br/>serving shards]
        Push --> Swap[Atomic swap<br/>old → new trie]
    end
    subgraph Serve["SERVE (online, p99 ≤ 100ms)"]
        U[User keypress] --> CDN{CDN cache?}
        CDN -->|hit ~95%| R1[Return top-K]
        CDN -->|miss| Shard[Route to prefix shard]
        Shard --> Mem[In-RAM trie/FST lookup]
        Mem --> Merge[Merge with personal<br/>history Redis]
        Merge --> R2[Return ranked top-K]
    end
```

The build path tolerates minutes of latency (it runs hourly); the serve path budgets ~100ms total round-trip including network. The CDN absorbs ~95% of QPS because most traffic hits short prefixes ("t", "th", "the") that are stable across users.

**Trie vs FST comparison.** *[Source: Lucene FST docs, real-world autocomplete deployments]*

| Property | Hashmap-trie | FST (Finite State Transducer) |
|---|---|---|
| RAM (10M queries, 12-char avg) | ~60GB | ~6-12GB (5-10x compression) |
| Lookup | O(prefix) hashmap chain | O(prefix) state transitions |
| Mutation | cheap (per-node insert) | expensive (rebuild whole structure) |
| Best for | dev / mutable | prod / immutable hourly snapshot |

FST exploits **shared suffixes** (every word ending "-ing" reuses one trailing path) and shared prefixes (every word starting "anti-" shares a leading path). It's structurally minimal: there is exactly one path per accepted string. This is why Lucene uses FSTs for term dictionaries — the same property that makes them small also makes them ideal for read-only hourly snapshots that get pushed to all serving nodes.

**Real-time mini-trie for trends.** A streaming aggregator (Flink) builds a small trie every 60 seconds from the most recent query stream and serving nodes consult both — base hourly trie + minute-level mini-trie — and merge top-Ks at request time. Cost: each serving node holds an extra ~50-100MB of mini-trie state. Win: breaking news ("earthquake", "election results") shows up in suggestions within a minute instead of an hour.

**Latency budget.** Industry target is ~100ms p99 from keypress to suggestion render. Network alone consumes 30-50ms (one round trip on mobile); the serving stack must finish in ≤50ms. CDN edge serves ~95% of QPS in <10ms. A miss costs ~30-40ms (route, memory lookup, merge with personal history). Anything more and the user has typed the next character before suggestions arrive — feels broken.

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: What if a serving shard goes down?**
Each shard is replicated 3x; LB routes around dead nodes. Trie snapshot in S3 means a new replica can warm up in minutes. Worst case: serve from CDN cache until replacement is up. *If pushed:* during rebuild, fall back to a smaller "head-only" trie (top 1% of queries) that fits on every node — degrades quality but not availability.

**Q: How do you handle real-time trends (breaking news in last 5 minutes)?**
Mini-trie built every minute from a sliding window of recent queries, merged with the hourly base trie at serve time. *If pushed:* tradeoff between freshness and noise — too short a window and one bot can spike a query; mitigate with bot detection + rate limits before counting.

**Q: How would you support typo tolerance?**
Pre-expand each query into common edit-distance-1 variants and add them to the trie. Or use a BK-tree side-index for fallback fuzzy lookups when the prefix has zero hits. *If pushed:* avoid both — let the search engine's "did you mean" handle it after submission. Autocomplete on raw prefix is faster and users mostly self-correct.

**Q: How do you personalize without blowing up the trie?**
Keep the global trie shared. Personalization is a separate per-user query history (Redis), merged at serve time — top-3 personal + top-7 global. *If pushed:* learned re-ranker on top of merged candidates uses user features (location, past queries) — heavier but cleaner than trying to bake personalization into the trie itself.

**Q: How do you prevent suggesting offensive or sensitive queries?**
Blocklist applied at trie-build time — filter out queries matching blocked patterns before they reach any node's top-K. Periodic ML classifier re-runs on the candidate set. *If pushed:* serve-time filter as defense-in-depth; legal/regional variants (EU vs US) maintained as separate trie overlays.

**Q: How would you scale this 10x to 100B queries/day?**
Add more prefix shards and replicate hot ones aggressively (geographically). Most cost is in CDN, not backend — push more prefixes to CDN edge. *If pushed:* swap from RAM trie to FST (5-10x compression) so each shard holds more; trade some lookup latency for memory headroom.


**Q: Why FST over hashmap-trie in production?**
RAM. A hashmap-based trie at 10M queries (12-char avg) is ~60GB; an FST compresses to 6-12GB by sharing suffix paths (every "-ing" path is one). FSTs are immutable, but autocomplete rebuilds hourly anyway so mutability isn't required. Lucene uses FSTs for the same reason. *If pushed:* the trade is build complexity — FST construction is non-trivial vs trivial trie inserts. Use a trie in dev, compile to FST for prod snapshot.

**Q: What's the latency budget end-to-end?**
~100ms p99 from keypress to suggestion render. Network round-trip on mobile is 30-50ms; serving stack must finish in ≤50ms. CDN edge handles ~95% of traffic in <10ms; misses cost ~30-40ms (shard route + RAM lookup + personal-history merge). Beyond 100ms, users have typed the next character — feels broken. *If pushed:* client-side prefetch on the next likely character ("if user typed 'th', request suggestions for 'tha', 'the', 'thi' speculatively") trades bandwidth for perceived latency.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Trie size in RAM** → finite state transducer (FST) compresses 5–10×. A naive char-tree with hashmaps is ~60GB; FST exploits shared suffixes (every word ending in "-ing" shares a path) and compresses to 6-12GB per shard, fitting comfortably on a single box.
- **Hot prefix** → CDN cache + replicate shard. Single-char prefixes ("t", "a") see 100x the QPS of long prefixes; CDN absorbs ~95% and shard replication handles the rest. Most users only type 2-3 characters before clicking a suggestion, so short prefixes dominate.
- **Build time on log explosion** → sample logs (1%) for tail; full for head. A viral event 10x's query volume; the aggregator can't keep up with raw logs. Sampling preserves the head distribution (popular queries still dominate top-K) and only the long tail loses statistical resolution — acceptable because long-tail queries weren't going to make any node's top-10 anyway.
- **Stale suggestions for trends** → near-real-time mini-trie for last hour, merged at serve. Hourly rebuild misses breaking news (e.g., "earthquake" trending in 5 min). A streaming aggregator builds a small trie every minute from the recent-query stream and serving nodes consult both — base trie + mini-trie — and merge top-Ks.

- **Tight latency budget** (~100ms p99 end-to-end) → CDN at edge, FST in RAM, no DB round-trips on hot path. Network alone consumes half the budget on mobile; the serving stack has ~50ms total. Anything that touches a DB (personalization, ranking) must hit RAM or Redis with sub-ms latency.
- **Snapshot push to N shards** → S3 + atomic swap. Pushing a 10GB FST snapshot to hundreds of serving shards via direct copy would saturate egress. Upload once to S3, shards fetch in parallel, atomic file swap (rename) makes the cutover instant per shard.

#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Snapshot distribution | New trie/FST snapshot is corrupt or incomplete on some shards | Checksum mismatch and snapshot-activation failure alert | Verify checksums before swap, keep last-known-good snapshot loaded, and roll back atomically |
| Hot shard | Prefix shard for common prefixes saturates | Per-shard QPS skew and latency spike | Replicate hot prefixes aggressively and let CDN absorb the shortest prefixes |
| Trend overlay | Mini-trie or recent-query overlay stalls | Overlay age and recent-trend miss canary | Serve from the base hourly snapshot only until the streaming overlay recovers |
| Personalization cache | Recent-history lookup is slow or unavailable | Personalization merge latency and cache miss spike | Fall back to global suggestions rather than delaying autocomplete |
| Edge caching | CDN cache stampede on a popular prefix after snapshot roll | Origin QPS spike on short prefixes | Pre-warm top prefixes on rollout and single-flight origin recomputation |
| Policy filter | Suppression rules fail and unsafe suggestions leak into top-K | Blocklist match canary and policy-violation audit | Apply filtering at build time and again at serve time as defense in depth |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Autocomplete latency** — end-to-end keypress to response, with backend p99 comfortably below the network budget.
- **Prefix-shard QPS skew** — shows whether replication strategy for hot prefixes is still adequate.
- **Snapshot build duration and publish lag** — trending freshness depends on not stretching the batch cycle.
- **Mini-trie freshness** — age of the streaming overlay for breaking-news terms.
- **CDN hit rate by prefix length** — short prefixes should overwhelmingly resolve at the edge.
- **Suppressed-suggestion rate** — catches policy regressions and malformed query floods.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** read-only trie shards are replicated globally per locale; they are ideal CDN/origin workloads.
- **RTO:** very fast for serving-node failure because shards are immutable and reloadable from object storage snapshots.
- **RPO:** bounded by the latest successfully published snapshot plus any recent-query overlay lag.
- **Failover cadence:** snapshot rollback drills matter more than classic database failover because the data plane is immutable.
- **Cross-region cost:** mostly snapshot distribution and CDN fill, not transactional replication.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **Scanning the subtree on every keystroke.** The whole point of the design is to move that work to build time.
- **Mutating the trie on the hot path.** Immutable snapshots keep serving fast and operationally simple.
- **Building per-user tries.** Personalization should be a small merge on top of a shared global structure.
- **Ignoring client debounce.** Autocomplete QPS assumptions are nonsense if every keypress becomes a network call.
- **Treating typo tolerance as free.** Fuzzy matching needs a separate budget and often a separate index.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Top-K cached at every prefix node** is the central idea; say it immediately.
- **Snapshot swap beats incremental in-place mutation** for simplicity and predictability.
- **FST is the production memory answer.** Trie in dev, compressed automaton in prod is a strong interview answer.
- **Short prefixes belong at the CDN edge.** Most traffic is massively skewed there.
- **Global plus personal merge** is the personalization story; the trie stays shared.
- **A recent-query overlay handles trends** without making the base serving path mutable.

### 11. Design YouTube
#### Problem
Upload, transcode, store, and stream video globally with adaptive bitrate, search, and recommendations.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a film studio's distribution operation. A director hands in raw footage (upload). A post-production team makes versions for IMAX, standard screens, small TVs, and phones — same film, seven different formats (transcoding). The copies are distributed to thousands of movie theaters in advance (CDN caching). When you buy a ticket, the theater plays the right format from its local copy — not a live stream from the studio in Hollywood. The studio is involved once per film, not once per viewer.

**The single-request walkthrough:** a creator uploads a 20-minute tutorial video (about 2.5GB raw). The upload is chunked — the client splits the file into 5MB pieces and sends them one at a time, getting an acknowledgment for each. If the connection drops, it restarts from the last confirmed chunk. The final chunk triggers the transcoding pipeline. The pipeline first splits the video into 120 six-second segments (6 seconds is the standard "chunk" size for streaming). Those 120 segments are processed in parallel by GPU workers: each segment gets encoded at seven resolutions (240p, 360p, 480p, 720p, 1080p, 1440p, 4K). That is 120 × 7 = 840 encoding jobs running simultaneously. Each job produces a short file called a `.ts` segment. A packaging step assembles the segments into an HLS manifest — a text file that tells the video player "for 1080p, here are the URLs for chunks 1 through 120." The manifest and all chunks land in object storage (like S3). The video status flips to "READY." A viewer opens the video. Their player downloads the manifest, picks a starting resolution based on their connection speed, and starts fetching 6-second chunks from the CDN.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Chunked resumable upload** — the client splits the file and uploads sequentially, tracking which chunks are confirmed. If the upload fails at chunk 47, it resumes from chunk 47 — not the beginning. Necessary because a 2.5GB file over a mobile connection will fail without this.
- **Transcoding pipeline (a DAG of jobs)** — a DAG (directed acyclic graph) is a set of tasks with dependencies: segment-splitting must finish before encoding begins, all encoding jobs for a segment must finish before packaging begins. Workers on GPU servers run encoding jobs in parallel. A GPU (like NVIDIA's NVENC chip) encodes H.264 video roughly 5 times faster than a CPU — critical at 500 hours of video uploaded per minute. The pipeline is orchestrated by a task scheduler (like Temporal) that handles retries, partial failures, and status tracking.
- **HLS (HTTP Live Streaming)** — Apple's streaming protocol. The video is divided into short chunks (6 seconds each) and a plain-text manifest file lists all the chunks at each available bitrate. The video player downloads the manifest once, then fetches chunks one at a time. This is "adaptive bitrate" (ABR): after each chunk, the player measures how fast the download went. If it was slow, the next chunk is requested at a lower bitrate (smaller file, lower quality but no buffering). If fast, higher bitrate. Viewers on shaky mobile connections see smooth playback instead of a spinning buffer indicator.
- **CDN (Content Delivery Network)** — a global network of cache servers close to users (Cloudflare, Akamai, or at YouTube's scale, physical hardware inside ISPs called Google Global Cache). Video chunks are static — they never change after transcoding. So a CDN can cache every chunk and serve it from the nearest city. A viewer in Berlin gets chunks from a CDN server in Frankfurt, not from a Google data center in Virginia. At YouTube scale, ~95% of bytes are served by CDN without ever touching Google's origin servers.
- **Storage tiering** — object storage has cost tiers. Hot tier (S3 Standard): expensive, millisecond access. Warm tier (S3 Infrequent Access): cheaper, still millisecond. Cold tier (Glacier): very cheap, minutes to restore. 80% of YouTube views hit 5% of videos. The other 95% of videos — the long tail nobody watches — can live in Glacier at a fraction of the cost. Lifecycle policies automatically move videos between tiers based on last-access time.
- **Metadata + recommendation services** — a separate concern from media delivery. The metadata service stores title, description, view count, like count. The recommendation service (two-stage: offline candidate generation + online ranking) decides what appears on the home screen. These are logically separate systems that happen to share a user interface.

**The thing that makes it hard:** a Champions League final reaches full-time with a 1–1 scoreline. The winner is decided on penalties. 500 million people all click the highlights video simultaneously 90 seconds after the final whistle. The video was uploaded 10 minutes ago. The CDN has barely cached anything. All 500 million requests are cache misses. They all go to origin. Origin cannot handle 500 million simultaneous requests. This is the cold-cache stampede at catastrophic scale. The fix: predictability and pre-warming. YouTube knows this final is happening and pre-positions chunks at CDN points-of-presence worldwide hours in advance. For unexpected viral events, a "shield cache" layer sits in front of origin — when 1000 CDN edge nodes simultaneously miss the same chunk, they all ask the shield, which issues exactly one request to origin and fans the response to all 1000.

**Why the standard solution works:** the pipeline separation (upload → transcode → serve) is what makes scale possible. Transcoding is expensive — you want to do it once, not once per viewer. Serving is cheap once the chunks exist and the CDN has them. The system spends compute dollars at upload time so it can spend almost nothing per view.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Chunked upload: pre-signed S3 multipart upload URLs handed to the client; client uploads directly to S3, bypassing your servers.
- Transcode pipeline: Kafka job queue, GPU workers running ffmpeg with NVENC, Temporal for DAG orchestration.
- Serving: HLS manifests and TS chunks in S3, fronted by CloudFront (CDN) with `Cache-Control: public, max-age=86400`.
- Hot path for playback (entirely CDN-served):
  ```
  client → GET /manifests/{video_id}/1080p.m3u8  (CDN hit, ~10ms)
  client → GET /chunks/{video_id}/1080p/seg-001.ts (CDN hit, ~15ms)
  client measures bandwidth → requests seg-002 at 720p or 1080p
  repeat every 6 seconds
  ```
#### Clarifying Questions
- Live streaming in scope? (assume on-demand only)
- Resolutions to support? (240p–4K)
- Watermark / DRM?
- Avg video length and upload rate?
- Comments/likes/recos in scope?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** upload, transcode, stream (HLS/DASH), search, comments, recommendations, thumbnails
- **NFR:** global low-latency playback, durable, scalable, reliable transcoding

#### Scale Estimate
- **Upload rate:** YouTube public figure ~500 hours uploaded/min (creator-day data, post-2019). 500 × 60 = 30k hours/hr × 24 = 720k hours/day; 720k ÷ 3600 ≈ ~8.3 hours of video uploaded every second.
- **Avg video length:** weighted ~12 min — Shorts <60s dominate count but not duration, vlogs 5–15 min, podcasts/streams 1–3hr push the mean up. 720k hours × 60 / 12 min/video = ~3.6M videos/day; conservative ~1.5M/day at the 12-min mean accounts for re-uploads + Shorts being a separate pipeline.
- **Upload concurrency:** assume ~10% of 1B+ DAU are creators × ~1 upload/week = ~14M uploads/day; spread over ~6h active US/EU window with chunked resumable streams (5–30 min/upload on mobile) → ~30k in-flight uploads at peak.
- **Raw video size:** consumer phone camera ~2.2 Mbps avg after container/codec pre-processing → 2.2 Mbps × 3600s ÷ 8 ≈ ~1GB/hour. 720k hours × 1GB = **~720TB/day raw ingest**.
- **Variant ladder:** 7 resolutions (240p, 360p, 480p, 720p, 1080p, 1440p, 4K) × ~2 codecs (H.264 universal + AV1 for top-watched titles to save egress); ladder sums to ~5× raw on average after per-rung encoding wins (lower rungs are tiny, top rungs dominate). 720TB × 5 = **~3.6PB/day encoded**.
- **Bitrate × duration math:** 1080p H.264 ~5 Mbps × 3600s ÷ 8 = ~2.25GB/hr; 4K AV1 ~15 Mbps × 3600s ÷ 8 = ~6.75GB/hr — the top two rungs alone account for ~60% of encoded storage even though they serve a smaller share of watches.
- **Replication:** encoded blobs at 3× in hot object store (durability + cross-region) → 3.6PB × 3 = ~10.8PB/day hot, × 365 ≈ **~4 EB/year** before tiering.
- **Hot tier:** last 30 days + popular long-tail (~5% of catalogue) on SSD-backed POPs; Pareto holds — ~80% of watch time hits that ~5%.
- **Cold tier:** ~90% of titles fall to infrequent-access after 30 days (10× cheaper $/GB), glacial archive after 1 year (~50× cheaper). Pays for itself because the long tail is mostly never re-watched.
- **Watch egress:** ~1B+ DAU × ~1 hour/user/day = ~1B hours watched/day; at avg ~720p ABR ≈ ~2.5 Mbps blended × 3600s × 1B ÷ 86,400s = ~80 Tbps avg egress; global event peaks (live World Cup goal) ~6× → ~500 Tbps. CDN absorbs >95%; ISP-embedded caches (Google Global Cache appliances) absorb most of the rest.
- **Chunk size:** HLS/DASH segment ~6s; at 1080p (~5 Mbps) → 6 × 5 ÷ 8 = ~3.75MB ≈ ~3.4MB after container overhead; manifest ~5KB. Chunks are the unit of CDN cache key, cross-user dedup, and ABR switch boundary.

#### API Contract
```
POST /upload/init       → { upload_id, chunked_url }
PUT  /upload/{id}/chunk body: bytes  → { offset_acked }
POST /upload/{id}/finish → { video_id }
GET  /video/{id}         → metadata + manifest URL (HLS .m3u8)
GET  /video/{id}/stream  → HLS chunks (served by CDN)
```

#### High-Level Design
```
UPLOAD:
  Client ──chunked upload──→ Edge Origin ──→ object store (raw)
                                             │
                                             ↓
                                     Transcoding DAG
                                             │
                ┌────────┬──────────┬────────┼──────────┬─────────┐
                ↓        ↓          ↓        ↓          ↓         ↓
              240p     360p       480p     720p      1080p      4K
                └────────┴──────────┴────┬───┴──────────┴─────────┘
                                         ↓
                                   HLS package (manifest + 6s chunks)
                                         ↓
                                   object store (encoded) → CDN

PLAYBACK:
  Client ─→ CDN ──hit──→ HLS chunks (binary)
             │
             └─miss──→ Origin → CDN populate → return

  Client ABR loop: measure bandwidth → request next chunk at appropriate bitrate
```

**How to read the diagram:** uploads, processing, and playback are separate stages. A creator uploads one original file, a media pipeline turns it into many streamable variants, and viewers later fetch those prepared assets from caches and CDNs rather than from the upload path.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** the work needed to make a video playable everywhere is expensive and asynchronous, so it should happen once per upload, not once per view. Playback then becomes a simpler problem of serving the right chunks to the right viewer at the right quality.

**What this layout buys you:** scalable uploads, efficient playback, and the ability to serve the same content across many device and network combinations. The tradeoff is a large transcode pipeline and a delay between upload and full playback readiness.

#### Data Model
- **videos** (transactional store, sharded by video_id):
  `(video_id Snowflake, owner_id, title, desc, duration, status, created_at)`
- **video_variants** (wide-column store): `(video_id, resolution, codec, manifest_url, byte_size)`
- **raw blobs** (cold object store): `raw/{video_id}.mp4`
- **encoded blobs** (hot object store): `enc/{video_id}/{res}/seg-{n}.ts`
- **search index**: title, tags, transcript

#### Detailed Design
**Transcoding pipeline (DAG):**
```
Raw upload
   │
   ↓
Validate (mediainfo)
   │
   ├──→ Split into segments (6s each, parallelizable)
   │
   ├──→ Encode segment × N resolutions (GPU)
   │
   ├──→ Package HLS / DASH (TS / fMP4 chunks + manifest)
   │
   ├──→ Generate thumbnails
   │
   └──→ Update video status to READY → notify uploader
```

Workers pull jobs from Kafka. Use ffmpeg / proprietary encoder. GPU acceleration (NVENC) cuts H.264 encode time ~5x vs CPU; AV1 still expensive enough that it's reserved for top-watched titles where the bandwidth savings repay the GPU cost.

**ABR (adaptive bitrate).** Client buffers 2-3 chunks ahead and measures the per-chunk download time; effective bandwidth = `chunk_bytes / download_time`. The next chunk request picks the highest bitrate variant ≤ a fraction (typically 0.7-0.8x) of measured bandwidth — the safety margin avoids flapping when the network jitters. The manifest exposes all bitrate variants so the switch is just a different URL; no server-side coordination needed. ABR's clear win: a Wi-Fi → cellular handover degrades smoothly from 1080p to 480p instead of stalling for re-buffer. Downside is visible quality fluctuation; player smoothing logic limits how aggressively bitrate jumps to avoid distracting transitions.

**Storage tiering.**

| Tier | Use | Cost | Latency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hot (S3 Standard / SSD) | popular videos | $$$$ | ms |
| Warm (S3 IA) | medium tail | $$ | ms |
| Cold (Glacier) | archival, rarely watched | $ | mins |

Lifecycle policies move objects automatically: 30 days without watch → IA; 1 year → Glacier. Recall the access pattern: 80% of watch time hits 5% of catalog, so 95% of stored bytes can sit in cheap cold tiers. A long-tail video pulled out of Glacier costs minutes of restore latency (acceptable for content nobody's watched in a year — show "preparing video" UI). The economic kicker: hot storage at 3.6PB/day forever is unaffordable; tiering converts most of the cost from $0.023/GB-month down to $0.001/GB-month.

**Recommendation.** Two-stage pipeline. Offline batch (Spark) computes a per-user candidate set nightly: collaborative filtering + watch-history embeddings produce ~500 candidate video IDs per user. Online ranking model (DNN) re-scores those 500 in real-time using freshness features (just-uploaded, just-trending) and serves top 20-50 from a Redis cache with 1h TTL. Two-stage exists because online inference on the full catalog is too expensive but candidate-pool inference is feasible. The cache absorbs the hot read pattern (user hitting home page 5 times an hour).

**Comments and likes.** Treated as a separate sub-system, partitioned by `video_id`. Comments live in a wide-column store keyed `(video_id, comment_id)` for fast in-thread fetches; counts (like count, view count, comment count) are denormalised into a counters service backed by Redis with periodic flush to the canonical store — view increments at YouTube scale (1B hours/day) cannot hit the transactional store directly. Likes use the same counter pattern with a separate per-user `(user_id, video_id) → liked` index for "did I like this" lookups. Hot videos (viral) get write-batched: 1000 increments/sec collapse into one DB write per second via a counter aggregator. Spam/abuse filtering on comments runs async post-write — a comment is visible immediately, then ML-classified within seconds and shadow-banned if flagged.


**Transcoding pipeline DAG (parallelizable per segment + resolution).** The win here is that segments are independent — split a 1-hour video into 600 6-second segments and 600 GPU workers can each handle one, finishing the entire transcode in ~1 minute wall-clock instead of ~600 minutes serial.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Up[Raw upload<br/>S3 ingest] --> V[Validate<br/>mediainfo]
    V --> Split["Split into 6s segments<br/>(600 segments for 1hr video)"]
    Split --> P1[Segment 1]
    Split --> P2[Segment 2]
    Split --> P3[...]
    Split --> PN[Segment N]
    P1 --> E1080[Encode 1080p<br/>NVENC GPU]
    P1 --> E720[Encode 720p]
    P1 --> E480[Encode 480p]
    P1 --> E240[Encode 240p]
    P2 --> E1080
    P2 --> E720
    P2 --> E480
    P2 --> E240
    PN --> E1080
    PN --> E720
    PN --> E480
    PN --> E240
    E1080 --> Pkg[Package HLS/DASH<br/>fMP4 chunks + manifest]
    E720 --> Pkg
    E480 --> Pkg
    E240 --> Pkg
    Pkg --> Thumb[Thumbnails]
    Pkg --> Ready[Status: READY<br/>notify uploader]
    Ready --> CDN[Pre-warm to CDN POPs<br/>if creator > threshold]
```

Orchestrators like Temporal or Cadence track the DAG state, retry failed segments, and surface partial-readiness (mark video READY at 720p before 4K finishes). NVENC GPU encoding cuts H.264 wall-clock ~5x vs CPU; AV1 is ~10-20x more compute than H.264 — reserved for top titles where the bandwidth savings repay the GPU cost.

**Per-title encoding ladder.** *[Source: Netflix tech blog]* Naive approach: same bitrate ladder for every video (e.g. 235/375/560/750/1050/1750/2350/3000 kbps). Netflix switched to **per-title** encoding — analyse video complexity (a talking-head documentary needs far less bitrate than a Marvel action sequence at the same perceptual quality) and pick a custom ladder per title. Wins: ~20% bitrate reduction at equal quality, or equivalently better quality at the same bitrate. Same bytes, less buffering, lower CDN cost. Per-shot encoding (within a title, complexity varies — quiet scene vs action) is the next refinement.

**ABR algorithm in detail.** Player buffers 2-3 chunks ahead and computes effective bandwidth = `chunk_bytes / download_time`. Next request picks the highest variant ≤ 0.7-0.8× measured bandwidth (safety margin avoids flapping under jitter). Modern players use a hybrid algorithm (e.g. BOLA, MPC) combining bandwidth estimation with buffer-occupancy heuristics — high buffer = stay aggressive on quality; low buffer = back off even if bandwidth looks fine. The manifest exposes all variants as separate URLs; switching is just changing which URL the next chunk request hits — no server-side coordination needed.

**ISP-embedded CDN nodes.** *[Source: Netflix Open Connect, Google Global Cache]* For sustained streaming traffic, neither Akamai nor Cloudflare are the bottom layer — Netflix's **Open Connect Appliances** and Google's **Global Cache** ship physical hardware to ISPs. The ISP racks the box in their own datacenter; popular content is pre-positioned on it overnight. Watch traffic never leaves the ISP's network — the bytes already live on hardware inside the eyeball network. This is how a Champions League final 100x spike doesn't melt the public internet: ~80% of bytes serve from hardware physically inside the viewer's ISP.

**HLS vs DASH.** Both are manifest-driven ABR formats over HTTP. HLS (Apple) is the de-facto on iOS/Safari and uses M3U8 playlists + TS or fMP4 segments. DASH is the open standard, MPD manifest + fMP4. Modern stacks ship both manifests pointing at the same fMP4 segments (CMAF) — single byte-for-byte storage, one URL pattern, two manifests for client compatibility. Saves storage and CDN cache duplication.

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: What if the transcoding queue backs up during a viral upload event?**
Autoscale GPU workers based on queue depth; priority lanes for high-creator-tier or pre-scheduled live events. Long videos chunked early so partial readiness lets us serve 720p while 4K is still processing. *If pushed:* progressive availability — mark video READY at the lowest resolution as soon as it's done; higher resolutions populate later via manifest update.

**Q: How do you keep storage costs sane for 3.6PB/day of new content?**
Tier aggressively — popular videos in S3 Standard, long tail in S3 IA after 30 days, Glacier for archive. Aggressive dedup of identical uploads (same hash). *If pushed:* per-title encoding ladder — only encode bitrates clients actually request; for very long-tail content, skip 4K entirely.

**Q: How would you handle a single-event 100x traffic spike (Champions League final)?**
Multi-CDN with pre-purchased capacity; pre-warm chunks to all major POPs hours before. Clients hit edge first, never reach origin. ISP-embedded caches (Google Global Cache / Netflix Open Connect appliances) absorb most of the bytes inside the eyeball network. *If pushed:* tier of mid-tier shield caches in front of origin so a CDN miss in one POP doesn't stampede origin; cap concurrent origin fetches per asset via request coalescing.

**Q: ABR vs progressive download — why ABR?**
ABR adapts to changing bandwidth (mobile networks, congested wifi) per-chunk, so a buffering event becomes a quality drop instead of a stall. Progressive download wastes bandwidth on quality the user can't sustain. *If pushed:* ABR's downside is clients see quality fluctuation; mitigate with smoothing logic in player and per-title ABR ladder tuning.

**Q: How do you secure premium / paid content?**
DRM (Widevine, FairPlay, PlayReady) — encrypted chunks, license server hands out keys after entitlement check. Token-signed CDN URLs with short TTL prevent direct leakage. *If pushed:* watermarking (forensic marks per-user) so leaks can be traced; some platforms add session-bound rotation.

**Q: How would you migrate from H.264 to AV1 in production?**
Dual-encode for new uploads, gradually re-encode top-N most-watched back catalog. Manifest exposes both; client picks supported codec via capability negotiation. *If pushed:* encode AV1 only for high-traffic titles where the bandwidth savings pay for the GPU cost; long-tail stays H.264 forever.


**Q: Why per-title encoding ladder over a fixed ladder?**
A talking-head documentary at 1080p doesn't need the same bitrate as a Marvel film at 1080p — the perceptual complexity differs by 5-10x. A fixed ladder over-provisions bitrate for simple content (waste) and under-provisions for complex (quality drops). Netflix's per-title approach analyses each title and picks a custom ladder, getting ~20% bitrate reduction at equal quality. *If pushed:* per-shot is the next level — within a title, allocate more bits to complex action scenes and fewer to dialogue. Saves bandwidth, reduces buffering, cuts CDN cost.

**Q: How do ISP-embedded caches change the architecture?**
At YouTube/Netflix scale, the public CDN tier (Akamai/Cloudflare) isn't the bottom — they ship hardware (Netflix Open Connect, Google Global Cache) into ISP datacenters. ISP racks the box; popular content pre-positioned overnight; watch traffic never leaves the ISP network. ~80% of bytes serve from inside the eyeball network. *If pushed:* this is why a Champions League final at 100x normal traffic doesn't melt the public internet — the bytes are already physically inside the ISP. Cost shifts from peering to hardware.

**Q: HLS vs DASH — pick one or both?**
Both. HLS is mandatory for iOS/Safari; DASH is the open standard preferred elsewhere. Modern stacks store **fMP4 (CMAF) segments once** and ship both manifests pointing at the same bytes — one storage, two manifests. Avoids the historical waste of storing TS segments for HLS and fMP4 for DASH separately. *If pushed:* HLS now supports fMP4 too, so the segment format gap closed; differences are only in manifest syntax.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Viral video** → CDN absorbs; pre-warm to multiple POPs. A video going from 1k to 10M views/hour overwhelms any single POP; predictive pre-warming (push chunks to all major POPs once a video crosses a trending threshold) eliminates the cold-miss stampede.
- **Transcoding queue depth** → autoscale GPU pool; prioritize by uploader/views forecast. A 10x upload spike (e.g. post-election surge in commentary uploads) backs up the queue; autoscaling adds GPU capacity in minutes, while priority lanes ensure top-creator content doesn't sit behind low-value uploads. Long videos are also chunked early so partial readiness lets us serve 720p while 4K is still encoding.
- **Cross-region replication lag** → eventual consistency on new uploads (accept "video processing"). A video uploaded in California isn't instantly available in Tokyo; rather than block on global replication, the metadata flips region-local first and replicates async — viewers see "processing" until their region's copy lands.
- **Storage costs explode** → aggressive cold tiering of long-tail. 3.6PB/day at hot-tier prices is infeasible; auto-lifecycle moves 90% of bytes to IA/Glacier within 30 days. A long-tail video being pulled from Glacier costs minutes of restore — fine because long-tail = rare access by definition.
- **Live event spike** (Champions League) → multi-CDN, pre-purchased capacity. A single event can draw 100x normal traffic for a 90-minute window; pre-purchased CDN capacity, multi-CDN routing (Akamai + Cloudflare + ISP-embedded caches simultaneously), and DNS-based load steering handle the burst that no single CDN can absorb alone.

- **Bitrate inefficiency on fixed ladders** → per-title encoding analysis. A fixed ladder wastes bandwidth on simple content and under-quality on complex; per-title saves ~20% bitrate at equal quality. At YouTube/Netflix scale that ~20% is petabytes/month of CDN egress savings — the GPU cost of analysis is recovered in days.
- **Storage duplication across streaming formats** → CMAF (fMP4) shared by HLS + DASH. Storing TS for HLS and fMP4 for DASH doubles cold-tier cost; CMAF lets one byte-stream serve both manifests. Halves storage at no quality cost.
- **Public-internet bottleneck on live spikes** → ISP-embedded CDN hardware. Even the largest CDNs are oversubscribed during a Champions League final; Open Connect / Google Global Cache box embedded in ISP racks serves 80% of bytes without crossing peering links.

#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Upload ingest | Resumable upload state is lost mid-file | Offset-mismatch and abandoned-upload alarms | Chunk-level acknowledgements and resumable state keyed by upload ID let clients restart from the last confirmed chunk |
| Transcoding / processing | Segment or variant jobs stall and video never becomes READY | Queue depth, stuck-job age, and per-variant failure alerts | Retry at the segment level, allow partial readiness at lower resolutions, and dead-letter permanently bad inputs |
| Manifest publish | Manifest becomes visible before all required chunks are present | Chunk-404 on startup and manifest-validation canary | Publish manifests only after variant completeness checks and atomic object promotion |
| CDN / origin | Viral content causes cold-miss stampede to origin | Origin egress spike and cache-fill amplification alerts | Pre-warm rising content, use shield caches, and coalesce concurrent origin fetches per asset |
| Recommendation path | Recommender or ranking path degrades and home page empties | Candidate-generation timeout and fallback-feed rate | Fall back to subscriptions / trending / recent content rather than blocking playback |
| Moderation / rights | Unsafe or copyrighted content becomes playable before checks finish | Time-to-moderation and takedown lag metrics | Gate visibility on required moderation stages for risky classes and support instant tombstoning at hydration time |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Upload-to-ready latency** — creator-facing SLO; broken down by ingest, transcode, and packaging stages.
- **Time-to-first-frame** — user-facing playback metric dominated by CDN, manifest, and first-chunk delivery.
- **Transcoding queue depth and oldest-job age** — early warning for processing backlog.
- **CDN hit rate by title class** — hot and long-tail content behave very differently and should be tracked separately.
- **Rebuffer rate / buffering events per hour watched** — the main streaming quality metric.
- **Recommendation freshness** — especially for newly uploaded and newly trending content.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** uploads land in regional ingest, encoded artifacts replicate to multi-region object storage, and CDN handles global edge distribution.
- **RTO:** ingest and stateless APIs fail over quickly; transcode backlog recovery is slower but bounded because the raw upload is durable.
- **RPO:** near-zero for completed uploads if raw bytes are durably persisted before ack; encoded derivatives can be recomputed.
- **Failover cadence:** test cold-region transcode replay and CDN-origin failover, not just API restarts.
- **Cross-region cost:** dominated by encoded media replication and CDN fill, not by metadata.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **Synchronous transcoding on upload.** Upload completion and video readiness are different lifecycle stages.
- **Serving bytes from the API tier or origin directly.** CDN is not optional at video scale.
- **Fixed bitrate ladder for every title.** Per-title ladders save enormous storage and egress.
- **No storage tiering.** The long tail makes hot-only storage economically absurd.
- **Conflating recommendation serving with media delivery.** These are separate scale domains with separate failure modes.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Chunked resumable upload + async transcode** is the clean upload story.
- **ABR over HLS/DASH** is the playback story; the client picks the next chunk bitrate.
- **Per-title encoding ladder** is a strong differentiator beyond the generic “multiple resolutions” answer.
- **CDN and ISP-edge caching are what make the egress numbers believable.**
- **Hot/cold tiering is mandatory.** You cannot keep the whole catalog in expensive hot storage forever.
- **Recommendation and playback are separate systems.** One chooses what to watch; the other serves the bytes.

### 12. Design Google Drive (Dropbox)
#### Problem
File sync and storage across devices with versioning, sharing, and conflict-handling collaboration.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a postal system where every document is sliced into numbered pages before mailing. When you change one page, you only re-send that page, and every post office along the route keeps a copy of every page it has ever received. When your laptop wakes up after a week offline, it asks the post office "what pages changed since I was last here?" and only downloads those — never the whole document.

**The single-request walkthrough:** you edit a 200MB video file, changing 4MB near the end. Your laptop's sync agent splits the file into 4MB chunks and hashes each one (e.g. SHA-256). It sends the hash list to the Metadata Service: "I have chunks A, B, C, D, E, F." The server replies "I already have A, B, C, D, E — just send F." Your client uploads F (4MB) to the Block Service, which stores it by its hash. The Metadata Service records the new version: the file now maps to chunks [A, B, C, D, E, F_new]. It publishes a change event `{ file_id, version: 3 }` to the Notify Service via WebSocket. Your phone wakes up, sees the notification, asks for the chunk list, discovers only F changed, and downloads 4MB. Total bandwidth: 4MB instead of 200MB.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Sync agent (client-side)** — the program running on your laptop or phone. Splits files into fixed 4MB chunks (a "chunk" is just a fixed-size slice of the file bytes), hashes each chunk, compares with the server's hash list, and uploads only what is missing. This is the core bandwidth-saving mechanism.
- **Metadata Service** — stores the logical description of your files: folder structure, permissions, version history, and the ordered list of chunk hashes that make up each file. Does NOT store the actual bytes. Backed by a transactional database sharded by user_id.
- **Block Service** — stores the raw bytes, keyed by the chunk's hash. Two chunks with the same hash (e.g. the same corporate template uploaded by a million users) are stored exactly once — this is called content-addressed storage and is how Dropbox achieves 30-50% dedup across the whole corpus.
- **Server File Journal** — an append-only log of every change in your file namespace (create, modify, delete), each entry stamped with a monotonically increasing ID (JID). When your phone reconnects after a week offline, it fetches changes since its last-seen JID rather than scanning your entire folder tree. This keeps sync fast even if you have 10 million files.
- **Notify Service** — a WebSocket push channel. When a file changes, the Metadata Service publishes an event to the Notify Service, which pushes it to all your other logged-in devices in real time.

**The thing that makes it hard:** two devices edit the same file while offline. Your MacBook edits `report.docx` on Tuesday; your iPad also edits `report.docx` on Tuesday. Wednesday both come online. MacBook uploads version 3 first. iPad then tries to upload its version 3. The server sees a conflict: two branches off version 2 with no common ancestor. There is no safe way to auto-merge binary files like Word documents — merging byte ranges from two divergent edits produces corruption. So the server does the only safe thing: it accepts MacBook's upload as the canonical version 3, and saves iPad's upload as `report (conflict copy from iPad).docx` alongside. Now both files exist, and you resolve the conflict manually by opening both and copying what you want.

**Why the standard solution works:** the hash-list handshake before every upload is what makes the system efficient at scale. Instead of "upload the file and we'll figure it out," the client first asks "which of these chunks do you already have?" and only transfers the missing ones. In a corporate environment, this handshake collapses a 1GB Office installer to ~40MB of actual transfers because 240 of 250 chunks already exist on the server from prior installs. The tradeoff is that the sync engine is complex — it must handle offline edits, network interruptions mid-upload, and the journal cursor falling behind its retention window.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Metadata in Postgres sharded by `user_id`; Block store in S3 with keys = SHA-256 of chunk bytes; Notify Service on Redis pub/sub or WebSocket server.
- The hash-list handshake on every upload:
  ```
  hashes = [sha256(chunk) for chunk in split_4mb(file)]
  missing = POST /file {hashes}  # server replies with hashes it lacks
  for h in missing: PUT /chunk/{h} bytes
  POST /file/commit {file_id, hashes, metadata}
  ```
- On reconnect: `GET /journal?since={last_jid}` → replay operations → upload/download only deltas.
#### Clarifying Questions
- Max file size? Per-user storage cap?
- Real-time collab in docs (Google Docs)? (assume separate problem)
- Selective sync? Offline access?
- Sharing model: per-file ACL, link sharing?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** upload/download, sync across devices, share, versions, conflict resolution, search
- **NFR:** durability (11 9s), efficient sync (delta only), 99.99% availability

#### Scale Estimate
- **Users & raw size:** Google Drive ~3B Workspace-eligible accounts; assume 1B active users with paid/free quota averaging 10GB consumed (free tier 15GB partially used + paid 100GB+ pulls the mean). 1B × 10GB = **~10EB raw user data** before dedup.
- **File-size distribution:** avg ~1MB (geometric mean — most files are <100KB texts/configs/notes; long tail to multi-GB videos and disk images skews the arithmetic mean to ~1MB). Small-file count dominates metadata QPS even though byte volume is dominated by big files.
- **Chunk size:** 4MB fixed (matches Dropbox/Drive published values — large enough to amortise metadata + erasure-coding overhead, small enough to bound retransmits on flaky mobile). Files <4MB stored whole; many small files batched into archive chunks so metadata isn't 1:1 with files.
- **Dedup ratio:** content-defined hashing across corpus catches corporate templates, OS-shipped files, common media → ~30–50% reduction (Dropbox public number is ~30%, internal/enterprise tenants closer to 50%). 10EB × (1 − 0.4) = **~5–7 EB effective at 1× replication**.
- **Replication:** 3× durable on hot tier (cross-AZ object store) + erasure-coded 1.4× overhead on cold (Reed-Solomon 10+4) → 5–7 EB × 3 ≈ **~15–18 EB hot when fully active**; once ~70% of corpus migrates to EC cold → ~7 EB total stored bytes.
- **Daily churn:** ~10% of users sync per day = ~100M syncs/day; avg sync uploads ~100KB net new (most syncs are deletes/renames/metadata) → ~10TB net new bytes/day; with 3× replication → ~30TB/day writes to object store. (Note: pre-dedup ingest is higher, ~30–50TB/day, but dedup absorbs the duplicates.)
