**Photo CDN architecture.** *[Source: Meta engineering / Akamai-style photo CDN]* Instagram uses a multi-tier CDN: edge POPs at user-proximity (city-level) cache hot variants for hours; regional caches (per-country/region) backstop edge misses and absorb invalidations; origin (S3 or Meta's f4 cold storage) serves rare cold reads. The CDN is keyed by full URL including the variant suffix, so `/photo/abc123_medium.jpg` and `/photo/abc123_full.jpg` are separate cache entries — invalidating one variant doesn't invalidate the others, which matters for variant-level lifecycle (full-resolution can tier to cold while thumbnails stay hot). For viral content, pre-warming pushes the medium and small variants to all regional caches as soon as the ranker scores the post into the rising bucket, so the moment users in any region see the post the bytes are already at their nearest edge.

**Cassandra → ScyllaDB migration (2024 patterns).** *[Source: ScyllaDB.com 2024 case studies — Discord, Expedia, Freshworks; not a confirmed Instagram migration]* Note: there is no public Instagram migration case study; the 2024 pattern is broadly applicable. Companies migrating Cassandra → ScyllaDB report 2–10x throughput on the same hardware and 10–95% P99 latency reduction (Discord: 40–125ms → 15ms read p99; Expedia: 80ms → 5ms p99 with 3x throughput, 35% cost reduction; Freshworks: 1s → 50ms p99). The architectural reasons: ScyllaDB is C++ with a shard-per-core thread-per-core model (no shared state, no GC), while Cassandra is JVM with a thread-pool model (shared heap, GC pauses). Migration patterns: dual-write via the ScyllaDB Migrator tool, validate row-level parity in shadow reads, cut over reads first then writes, retire the Cassandra cluster. Risks: ScyllaDB's stricter wire protocol surfaces previously-tolerated client bugs; CL=QUORUM behaviour differs subtly under failure; the migration's value is highest for read-heavy, latency-sensitive workloads (timelines, feeds) and lowest for batch-analytics workloads.

**Sharded counters — the precise math.** *[Source: Discord/Riot/standard pattern]* Splitting a like count across 100 shard keys (`likes:{post}:{0..99}`) means each write picks one shard uniformly at random, capping per-shard write rate at `total_rate / 100`. For a post receiving 50k likes/sec, each shard sees 500 writes/sec — well within a single Cassandra/ScyllaDB row's capacity. Reads sum 100 keys, paying 100x the read fan-out for the displayed count. This is acceptable because reads of the count happen on every post-render but each render only needs the count once; sharded read can be cached at the API tier with 60s TTL to amortise the fan-out. Exact-count operations (advertiser dashboards) read the underlying like-event log instead, which is the source of truth; sharded counters are a fast cache.

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: A Cristiano Ronaldo post gets 5M likes in 10 minutes — how does the counter survive?**
Sharded counter: split the post's like count across N keys (`likes:{post}:{0..N-1}`); writes pick a random shard, reads sum all shards. 5M writes / 100 shards = 50k writes per shard, easily absorbed. *If pushed:* eventual consistency is fine here — users don't notice "1.2M" vs "1.199M". For exact counts (audit, ads), batch reconcile from the like-event log.

**Q: Image processing pipeline backs up — uploads queue grows. What do you do?**
Autoscale workers off queue depth; if still backed up, prioritize: active uploaders' content first, brand new accounts last (anti-spam side effect). User sees "uploading…" state in app until variants are ready. *If pushed:* generate the smallest variant (thumbnail) synchronously on upload so the user sees their post in their grid immediately; other variants fill in async.

**Q: How do you keep stories ephemeral at scale — TTL on billions of objects?**
Store stories in Cassandra with native TTL; expired rows are garbage-collected by compaction. Media in S3 has lifecycle rule: delete after 25h (1h grace). No background sweeper to run. *If pushed:* "Highlights" copies the underlying media to a permanent collection on opt-in to break the TTL — a copy is the right model since stories and highlights have different access patterns.

**Q: A user uploads CSAM / illegal content — how do you catch it before it's seen?**
Hash uploads against a fingerprint database (PhotoDNA-equivalent) inline with transcoding — block instantly on match. ML classifier scores other risk dimensions; high-risk content gets human-review hold before publishing. *If pushed:* high-velocity new accounts get a stricter policy (pre-publish review) until reputation builds; legal-mandated reporting for matched content.

**Q: How do you handle 10x growth in upload volume?**
Image processing is embarrassingly parallel — autoscale workers and S3 doesn't care. The bottleneck is metadata DB; pre-shard posts by user, hot tablets split independently. CDN scales independently. *If pushed:* the metadata write QPS is small per post, but read QPS for hot posts can spike — read replicas + cache (post hydrate cache by post_id, TTL 60s) absorbs it.

**Q: A user blocks someone — how does that propagate to the feed and Explore?**
Block list per user, cached in feed service; merged into feed candidates as a filter at read time. Cheap because block lists are small. Search and Explore consult the same filter. *If pushed:* if blocks are huge (celebrities blocking thousands), use a Bloom filter for fast negative checks, full set for confirms; eventually consistent across services is fine — a blocked post slipping through for a few seconds isn't catastrophic.

**Q: Why are platforms migrating from Cassandra to ScyllaDB in 2024?**
ScyllaDB is C++ with a shard-per-core thread-per-core model — no GC, no shared state, no thread-pool contention. Cassandra is JVM-based with thread-pool execution and GC pauses. On the same hardware, ScyllaDB delivers 2–10x throughput; on read-heavy latency-sensitive workloads (Discord, Expedia, Freshworks) P99 latency drops 50–95%. *If pushed:* the migration tool (ScyllaDB Migrator) supports dual-write + shadow-read validation; main risks are that ScyllaDB's stricter protocol surfaces latent client bugs and CL=QUORUM behaviour differs subtly under failure modes — test extensively before cutting over writes.

**Q: How does multi-tier CDN handle a post going viral globally?**
When the ranker scores a post into the "rising" bucket, a pre-warm job pushes the medium and small variants to all regional caches in the user-proximity tier; edge POPs hydrate from regional caches on first request, so even the first viewer in any city sees a sub-100ms load. Origin only sees one fetch per regional cache, distributing load across ~50 regions instead of bombarding origin. *If pushed:* full-resolution variants stay at origin (rarely viewed, expensive to pre-warm); thumbnail and small variants pre-warm aggressively because they cover 95% of view bandwidth.

**Q: How are sharded counters reconciled against the like-event log?**
The like-event log is source of truth — every like is an append-only event in the wide-column store keyed by `(post_id, user_id, ts)` (also enforces idempotency: a duplicate like from the same user on the same post is a no-op insert). Sharded counters (`likes:{post}:{0..99}`) are a derived cache. A periodic reconciliation job (hourly per shard) sums the event log per post, compares to the sum of sharded counters, and corrects drift via an audit-trailed adjustment. Drift is normally zero because the counter increment is in the same transaction as the event insert; when it isn't (rare cross-region propagation issue), the event log wins. *If pushed:* for advertiser dashboards needing exact counts, query the event log directly via a materialised view that's batch-refreshed.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Hot post** (viral celebrity) — a Cristiano Ronaldo post pulls millions of metadata reads in minutes; the post's row in the wide-column store gets hammered, and feed services hydrating it overwhelm the metadata path. *Mitigation:* the CDN absorbs all media reads (origin sees one fetch per edge POP); the hydrated post payload (metadata + URLs + counter values) is cached at the API tier with a short TTL (60s) so the metadata DB sees one read per cache miss instead of millions. Read-replicas of the metadata DB scale read fan-out further, and per-post rate limits prevent any single post from monopolizing DB resources.
- **Storage explosion** — Instagram accumulates exabytes of media; serving everything from hot tier (S3 Standard) is prohibitively expensive when 95% of media is rarely accessed beyond the first month. *Mitigation:* lifecycle rules tier media by age and access pattern — hot tier (S3 Standard) for first 90 days, warm tier (Standard-IA) for the next 9 months, cold/glacial archive thereafter. Old media still serves from CDN edges if requested; cold-tier reads have higher latency (seconds for Glacier) but apply only to genuinely cold content. Variant-level tiering is possible: thumbnails stay hot indefinitely (used in profile grids) while full-resolution originals tier aggressively.
- **Image processing queue** — upload spikes (a global event drives 10x normal upload rate) cause the processing queue to grow; users see "uploading…" for minutes; new uploaders abandon. *Mitigation:* workers autoscale based on queue depth with a fast scale-up policy (add capacity within 30s); priority lanes route active-creator uploads ahead of brand-new accounts (which are also a spam vector); the smallest variant (thumbnail) can be generated synchronously on the upload edge so the user sees their post in their grid immediately while other variants fill in async.
- **Like/comment write storm on viral posts** — a Ronaldo post getting 5M likes in 10 minutes means 50k writes/sec to one row in the likes counter — single-row write throughput in any wide-column store caps in the low thousands. *Mitigation:* sharded counters split each post's like count across N keys (`likes:{post}:{0..99}`); writes pick a random shard, reads sum all 100 shards. 5M writes / 100 shards = 50k per shard total, easily absorbed; readers tolerate eventual consistency on the displayed count. For exact counts (advertiser dashboards, audit), a batch reconciliation job sums the like-event log — the log is the truth, the counters are a fast cache.
- **Mobile data usage** — serving the full-resolution variant to a mobile client on a 3G network wastes bandwidth, slows load times, and burns user data caps; bad UX for users in emerging markets. *Mitigation:* the API returns CDN URLs for *all* variants in the post payload; the client picks the appropriate one based on viewport size, device pixel density, and connection quality (Wi-Fi vs cellular, signal strength). Adaptive resolution: start with `small` and upgrade to `medium` if the user zooms or the post enters the viewport for >2s. AVIF/WebP variants are served when client headers indicate support, cutting bytes 30-50% over JPEG at equal quality.

#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Variant generation | Async media pipeline fails or stalls, leaving broken post assets | Upload-to-ready lag and variant-generation error rate | Retry at variant granularity, keep post in `processing`, and notify user only when required assets are ready |
| Story TTL | Story rows or media objects outlive 24h unexpectedly | Expired-story canary and TTL drift metrics | Use native row TTL plus object-store lifecycle rules, and copy to highlights explicitly instead of exempting originals |
| CDN invalidation | Wrong or stale variant remains cached after moderation/delete | Tombstone-hit mismatch and media-404 after delete | Short cache TTL for mutable surfaces and canonical tombstone checks before serving metadata |
| Counter shards | Like/comment shard skew or drift appears on viral posts | Shard-write imbalance and reconciliation drift | Increase shard count for hot posts and reconcile from append-only event logs |
| Explore / feed hydrate | Metadata DB slowdown makes media URLs unavailable even though bytes are hot on CDN | Hydration p99 and metadata-cache miss spike | Cache hydrated post cards briefly and serve partial fallbacks where possible |
| Moderation | Unsafe content becomes visible before checks finish | Time-to-moderation and block-after-publish metrics | Gate visibility on required trust-and-safety stages for risky uploads |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Upload-to-ready latency** — creator-facing asset pipeline SLO.
- **Variant-generation failure rate** — catches codec/regression issues early.
- **CDN hit ratio by variant** — especially thumbnail/small vs full-size variants.
- **Story expiry correctness** — fraction of stories still retrievable after TTL.
- **Counter drift against event log** — protects engagement correctness on hot content.
- **Feed media bytes per render** — critical mobile-performance and cost metric.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** media objects replicate broadly behind CDN; metadata is region-homed but globally readable via caches.
- **RTO:** media delivery survives most origin issues via CDN; metadata-region failover is slower but manageable.
- **RPO:** near-zero for post metadata and uploaded media after commit; story expiry is derived and can be recomputed.
- **Failover cadence:** asset-pipeline and metadata failovers both matter; pure API drills are insufficient.
- **Cross-region cost:** dominated by media replication and CDN egress, not by metadata rows.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **Resizing images on demand in the read path.** Precomputed variants are the whole point of the media pipeline.
- **Routing upload bytes through your API tier.** Direct-to-object-store upload keeps APIs from becoming bandwidth bottlenecks.
- **Treating stories as normal permanent posts.** Their lifecycle and storage semantics are different.
- **Exact counters on celebrity posts.** Hot content needs sharded or derived counters.
- **Serving full-resolution media everywhere.** Mobile feeds should bias toward appropriate variants.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Direct upload to object storage plus async variant generation** is the upload story.
- **CDN URLs only in feed payloads** is the read-story simplification.
- **Stories want native TTL semantics** rather than bespoke cleanup jobs.
- **Explore and feed ranking are separate recommendation surfaces.**
- **Sharded counters keep celebrity engagement bursts tractable.**
- **Media dominates the system, not text.** That is the architectural mindset shift for Instagram.

### 28. Design TikTok (Short Video)
#### Problem
Endless personalized short-video feed with sub-second swipe-to-play, upload, and a strong recommendation algorithm.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a jukebox that watches you — every song it plays, it studies whether you fast-forwarded, replayed, or danced. The next song is chosen based on everything it has observed about you and everyone like you. But this jukebox serves 1 billion people simultaneously, and if the next song takes more than a fraction of a second to start, people switch to something else.

**The single-request walkthrough:** you open TikTok and swipe up. Your app sends your `user_id` plus context (time, location, device) to the Feed Service. The Feed Service retrieves your **user embedding** — a 256-dimensional vector (think of it as a point in 256-dimensional space that encodes your viewing history and preferences, pre-computed from all your past interactions). It then runs **ANN retrieval** (approximate nearest neighbor — an algorithm that finds the videos whose embeddings are mathematically closest to yours in that 256-dimensional space, from a pool of 10 billion videos, in under 10 milliseconds). Five retrieval channels run in parallel: the two-tower ANN result, trending videos in your region, recent uploads from creators you follow, diversity candidates (to avoid filter bubbles), and videos using sounds you have engaged with. These ~300 candidates go through a **ranker** — a small neural network that predicts your probability of watching each video to completion, liking it, and sharing it. The top 50 video IDs are returned to your app. Your app starts playing #1 and simultaneously downloads the first chunk of #2 and #3 in the background. When you swipe, #2 plays instantly from local storage.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Two-tower model** — a machine learning architecture with two halves: a "user tower" that encodes a user's history into a 256-dim vector, and a "video tower" that encodes a video's features into the same space. The critical property: the video tower runs offline (once per video, result stored), so at serve time only the cheap user-tower forward pass runs live. You look up your vector, then find nearby video vectors using an index.
- **ANN index (HNSW or IVF-PQ)** — **Approximate Nearest Neighbor** is an algorithm that finds the closest vectors to a query without checking all 10 billion of them. **HNSW** (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) builds a graph where similar vectors are connected; searching it is like navigating a city by always moving toward the destination. **IVF-PQ** (Inverted File with Product Quantization) compresses each vector from 1KB down to ~16 bytes (accepting a small accuracy loss) and clusters them, so you only search the relevant cluster. At 10 billion videos, HNSW would require ~10TB of RAM (infeasible); IVF-PQ uses ~160GB.
- **Online learning** — interaction events (you watched 80% of a video, you skipped this one after 2 seconds, you liked that one) flow back as a stream. A continuous training process updates the **ranker** model every few minutes so a video going viral right now will be boosted in recommendations within minutes, not tomorrow. The retrieval embeddings update more slowly (hourly) to prevent feedback loops where the model's own recommendations distort its training.
- **CDN with HLS** — video is encoded into multiple quality levels (240p, 480p, 720p, 1080p) and chunked into 4-second segments. **HLS** (HTTP Live Streaming) is the protocol that tells the video player which chunks exist at which quality levels. The player downloads the next chunk before it needs it and adjusts quality based on network speed. The CDN (a global network of edge servers) stores these chunks close to viewers.
- **Client preload** — the app downloads the first segment of the next 2–3 videos while playing the current one, using background bandwidth. When you swipe, the video is already on your device and starts in under 100ms. Without preload, every swipe requires a network round-trip plus buffer time.

**The thing that makes it hard — feedback loops in online learning:** if the ranking model updates every few minutes based on what it just recommended, and users naturally watch whatever is recommended, the model's training data becomes a reflection of its own outputs. Imagine the ranker starts slightly over-weighting cat videos. It shows more cat videos. Users watch them (because cat videos are decent). The model sees lots of cat-video engagement. It shows even more. Within hours, everyone's feed is cat videos. This is called a **feedback loop**. The fix: update the retrieval embeddings (which determine what categories of content are even considered) slowly — once per hour from a longer historical window — as a stability anchor. The ranker (which scores videos within a fixed candidate set) can update fast without destabilizing the space of what gets considered.

**Why the standard solution works:** the two-stage retrieval-then-ranking pipeline is necessary because ranking every video for every user is computationally impossible. Running the full ranking neural network on 10 billion videos per request at 1 million requests per second would require more compute than exists. Retrieval narrows 10 billion to ~300 in under 10ms using cheap vector math. Ranking scores 300 candidates in ~30ms using an expensive (but small) neural network. Two cheap operations compose to do what one expensive operation cannot.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Retrieval: Milvus or Pinecone vector database holding 10B video embeddings as IVF-PQ; ANN query for top-1000 candidates in ~5ms. Five retrieval channels in parallel.
- Ranking: TensorFlow Serving or Triton Inference Server; MLP with cross-features scoring ~300 candidates; GPU inference cluster autoscaling on request rate.
- Hot path pseudocode:
  ```
  user_vec = embedding_store.get(user_id)  // pre-computed, 256 floats
  candidates = union(
      ann_index.search(user_vec, k=200),
      trending_cache.get(region, k=50),
      follows_feed.get(user_id, k=30),
      diversity_sampler.get(k=20)
  )
  scores = ranker.predict(user_vec, candidates)  // batch GPU inference
  top50 = sort_by_score(scores)[:50]
  return hydrate(top50)  // add CDN URLs, metadata
  ```
- Interaction events → Kafka → streaming consumer that generates (user_id, video_id, label) training pairs → online ranker update every 5 minutes.
#### Clarifying Questions
- Video duration limits? (15s–10m typically)
- Live in scope?
- Comments/likes/duets?
- Region-specific content (China vs global)?
- Cold-start (new users, no signal)?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** upload video, browse "For You" feed, like/comment/share, follow, search by sound/effect/hashtag
- **NFR:** sub-second video start, smooth scroll/preload, accurate recommendations, scale to 1B users

#### Scale Estimate
- **Userbase:** ~1.5B MAU (TikTok-public 2024 order-of-magnitude); assume DAU ~1B (heavy engagement vs other social apps).
- **Uploads/day:** ~5% of DAU posts → 1B × 0.05 = ~50M; round up for power-creators / Shorts cross-posting → **~100M videos/day**. Avg raw ~10MB (60s @ ~1.3Mbps source) → 100M × 10MB = **~1PB/day raw upload** on object store.
- **Transcoded variants:** ABR ladder = 240p / 480p / 720p / 1080p HLS segments + thumbnail (~50KB) + preview poster (~200KB). Re-encoding to multiple resolutions/codecs (H.264 + H.265) totals ≈ **~2.5× raw** retained. 100M × 25MB = **~2.5PB/day variants**.
- **Storage retention tiering:** hot 30d (recent + actively watched), warm 90d, cold long-tail. ~40% of uploads receive <100 views (long-tail) → tier to cold within 14d. Effective hot tier = ~30d × 1PB raw + ~30d × 2.5PB variants × ~60% hot-retained ≈ **~30PB hot**.
- **Metadata per video:** video_id (8B) + creator_id (8B) + duration (4B) + sound_id (8B) + hashtags[] (~200B) + manifest_url (64B) + thumbnail_url (64B) + moderation_flags (16B) + ts (8B) + envelope ≈ **~2KB**. Daily = 100M × 2KB = **~200GB/day** on wide-column store; RF=3 → ~600GB/day.
- **Interactions:** ~100B feed views/day (1B DAU × ~100 video impressions/day) × ~30 fine-grained events per session (impression / start / 25% / 50% / 75% / complete / like / skip / share / pause) → **~3T interaction events/day**. Each event ~150B (user_id 8B + video_id 8B + event_type 4B + watch_pct 4B + session_id 16B + context features ~100B + ts) → 3T × 150B = **~450TB/day on partitioned event log** (Kafka-tier). 7-day hot retention for online learners → 7 × 450TB ≈ ~3PB hot.
- **User embeddings:** 1B users × 256-dim float32 = 1B × 256 × 4B = 1B × 1KB = **~1TB user-embedding store**; hourly batch refresh.
- **Video embeddings:** eligible pool ~10B videos (full corpus ever uploaded; long-tail kept for retrieval). 10B × 256 × 4B = **~10TB video-embedding store**; ANN-indexed (HNSW/IVF) in vector store.
- **ML training feature lake:** 30d × 3T events × 150B = 90T × 150B = ~13PB raw; columnar Parquet+ZSTD ~5× → **~2.5PB on object store**.
- **Read traffic:** 100B feed views/day ÷ 86,400 ≈ **~1.2M/s avg** → **~5M/s peak** (evenings/holidays). CDN hit-ratio >95% on segments; origin egress ≈ 5% × peak — handled by regional edges.

#### API Contract
```
POST /upload              chunked video upload → { video_id }
GET  /foryou?cursor       → [{video_id, manifest_url, metadata, prefetch_hints}]
POST /interaction         body: { video_id, type: like|share|skip|complete, watch_pct }
GET  /search?q=...        → results across users, sounds, hashtags
```

#### High-Level Design
```
UPLOAD:
  Client → Upload Edge → Object Store (raw) → Transcoding (240p, 480p, 720p) + Thumbnail
                                                  ↓
                                  HLS/MP4 manifests → CDN
                                                  ↓
                                       Metadata DB + Search Indexer
                                                  ↓
                                       Candidate Pool (eligible for recs)

FEED (For You):
  Client → Feed Service:
            1. Recommendation Service returns ~50 candidate video_ids
                   ↑
              Two-tower model retrieves (user embed × video embed)
                   ↑
              Ranking model scores (predicts watch_pct, like, share)
            2. Hydrate metadata + CDN URLs
            3. Client preloads next 2-3 videos
            4. Send watch events back → train ranker
```

**How to read the diagram:** there are two loops running together. One loop uploads, processes, and serves video. The other loop observes watch behavior and feeds those signals into recommendation systems that decide what the next swipe should show.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** in TikTok, ranking quality is part of the playback experience. Serving bytes quickly is necessary but not sufficient; the platform also has to choose the next item well, again and again, at huge scale.

**What this layout buys you:** tight recommendation feedback and highly optimized playback. The tradeoff is that the recommendation loop becomes central infrastructure rather than an optional add-on.

#### Data Model
- **videos** (wide-column store): `(video_id, creator_id, duration, sound_id, hashtags[], manifest_url, thumbnail_url, ts)`
- **interactions** (event stream → wide-column store): `(user_id, video_id, type, watch_pct, ts)`
- **embeddings** (vector store): `user_id → vector`, `video_id → vector`
- **sounds** (transactional store): `(sound_id, original_video_id, usage_count)`
- **hashtags / search** (search index)

#### Detailed Design
**Recommendation (the core):**
```
Two-stage pipeline:

Retrieval (millions → hundreds):
  - Two-tower model: user vector × video vector → cosine similarity
  - Trending in user's region
  - Follows: fresh content from followed creators
  - Diversification: not all from one creator/topic

Ranking (hundreds → ~50):
  - Multi-task neural net: predicts (watch_complete, like, share, comment, follow)
  - Combine into single score
  - Learn online from interaction stream
```

Retrieval uses a two-tower neural network: one tower encodes user features (interaction history, demographic, current session context) into a 256-dim embedding; the other encodes video features (creator, content tags, audio embedding, engagement priors) into the same space. Candidates are fetched by approximate-nearest-neighbor (HNSW or IVF-PQ) lookup against the video embedding store — the user vector becomes the query, top-K nearest videos are returned in milliseconds despite billions of candidates. Multiple retrieval channels run in parallel (collaborative-filter neighbors, trending-in-region, fresh-from-follows, diversity-injection) and their outputs are unioned. Ranking takes those ~hundreds of candidates and runs a multi-task model that predicts watch-completion %, like probability, share, comment, follow — these predictions are combined into a final score (typically a weighted sum tuned by A/B test). Top ~50 are returned to the client.

**Cold start:**

A brand new user has no interaction history, so the user-tower embedding is uninformative. Cold-start uses demographic-bucket defaults — age, region, language, device — keying into a pre-curated pool of "evergreen" content with high completion rates across most user segments. After the user's first ~10 interactions, the embedding has enough signal for normal two-tower retrieval to take over. A faster warm-start uses explicit onboarding (pick 3 interest topics) which seeds the user vector toward those topic clusters. For brand-new videos with no engagement history, the video-tower embedding is initialized from creator history + content features (CV embeddings of the visual track + audio embedding of the soundtrack) so they have plausible neighbors immediately rather than zero-vector cold start.

**Preloading:**

The For You feed response includes the first video plus prefetch hints for the next 2-3 videos. The client begins playing video #1 immediately and concurrently fetches the manifest + first segment for videos #2 and #3 in the background. By the time the user swipes, the next video's first chunk is already buffered locally — playback starts in <100ms instead of waiting for a fresh fetch. Preload adapts to bandwidth: on Wi-Fi, prefetch 1080p first segments aggressively; on slow cellular, prefetch only 480p and only one video ahead to conserve data. Preloaded-but-skipped videos are wasted bandwidth, so prefetch depth is tuned per network class.

**Sound / effects:**

A sound is a first-class entity (`sound_id` table) — every video carries a `sound_id` referring to either the original audio (the creator's recording) or a re-used sound from another video. The "use this sound" feature creates a new video that references an existing `sound_id`; the sound's `usage_count` increments. The sound page is just a query: `videos WHERE sound_id = ? ORDER BY engagement DESC` — surfacing all videos using that sound. This is the structural enabler of meme propagation: a sound becomes viral, every creator riffs on it, and the sound page becomes a discovery surface that compounds usage. Audio fingerprinting lets the upload pipeline detect re-used sounds even when the creator didn't explicitly select one.

**Live, comments/duets, China vs global — scope.** *Live streams* run on a separate ingest path (RTMP/WebRTC into a media server) with low-latency HLS or LL-DASH delivery to viewers; live videos appear in a dedicated Live tab and are eligible for the For-You feed only as recorded clips post-stream. The recommendation pipeline does not run on in-flight live streams (the candidate pool only contains finalised videos with embeddings). *Comments* are stored in a wide-column collection keyed by `video_id` with a separate sharded-counter for the visible comment count; comment ranking is a lightweight per-thread scorer (recency + author popularity + reply count). *Duets and stitches* are first-class derived videos that carry a `parent_video_id` reference; they're rendered as a side-by-side or stitched composite at upload time so the resulting file is a normal video that flows through the standard pipeline, while the parent reference enables "duets of this video" discovery and parent-creator notifications. *China (Douyin) vs global (TikTok)* run as fully separated infrastructures with independent content pools, recommendation models, moderation policies, and identity systems — they are not federated and a Douyin video does not appear on TikTok or vice versa; the design described here covers the global TikTok product. The architectural template is shared (two-stage recommender, online learning, CDN-fronted HLS) but the deployments, model weights, and operational teams are distinct.

**Two-stage retrieval + ranker pipeline.** *[Source: ByteDance / Pinterest / YouTube two-tower papers; Shaped.ai blog]* Every For-You request runs the same two-stage funnel: retrieval narrows ~10B eligible videos to a few hundred candidates via cheap embedding lookup; ranking scores those candidates with an expensive multi-task model and returns the top ~50. Multiple retrieval channels run in parallel and their unioned outputs feed the ranker; the channels exist precisely so the system isn't single-point-dependent on the two-tower model.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  REQ[Feed request user_id] --> CTX[Build context features<br/>session, time, location, device]
  CTX --> UEMB[Fetch user embedding<br/>from embedding store]
  UEMB --> CHAN[Retrieval channels — parallel]
  subgraph RETRIEVAL["Retrieval — 10B → ~hundreds"]
    CHAN --> R1[Two-tower ANN<br/>HNSW or IVF-PQ over video embeddings<br/>top-K cosine neighbors]
    CHAN --> R2[Trending in region<br/>top recent high-engagement videos<br/>cached top-K per region]
    CHAN --> R3[Fresh-from-follows<br/>recent videos from followed creators]
    CHAN --> R4[Diversity injection<br/>random samples from underexplored clusters]
    CHAN --> R5[Sound/hashtag affinity<br/>videos using sounds the user engaged with]
  end
  R1 --> UNION[Union + dedupe]
  R2 --> UNION
  R3 --> UNION
  R4 --> UNION
  R5 --> UNION
  UNION --> SAFE[Safety/eligibility filters<br/>moderation flags, region restrictions, block list]
  SAFE --> RANK[Ranker — multi-task NN<br/>predicts watch_pct, like, share, comment, follow]
  RANK --> SCORE[Combine into single score<br/>weighted sum, A/B-tuned]
  SCORE --> DIV[Diversity/variety re-rank<br/>penalize same creator/topic in window]
  DIV --> TOPK[Top ~50 video_ids]
  TOPK --> HYDRATE[Hydrate manifest URLs + metadata]
  HYDRATE --> RESP[Response to client]
  CLIENT[Client interactions] -.skip/complete/like/share.-> STREAM[Interaction event stream]
  STREAM -.online learning.-> RANK
  STREAM -.hourly batch.-> UEMB
```

Latency budget per request is tight: retrieval must complete in <10ms (ANN lookup + parallel channels merged), ranker scoring on ~200 candidates in <20–40ms, total feed-service P99 under 100ms. The ranker is small enough (a few-layer MLP, sometimes with cross-features) that GPU inference handles ~10k req/s per GPU; retrieval cost is dominated by ANN index lookup which is pure CPU and embarrassingly parallel.

**ANN index trade-offs — HNSW vs IVF-PQ.** *[Source: milvus.io, pingcap.com, ann-benchmarks.com]* Both are approximate-nearest-neighbour indexes for the retrieval embedding lookup; the choice depends on dataset size, recall target, and memory budget.

| Property | HNSW | IVF-PQ |
|---|---|---|
| Structure | Hierarchical small-world graph | Inverted file (Voronoi cells) + product quantization (vector compression) |
| Recall@10 | ~95% at typical settings | 85–93% (tunable, lower than HNSW at equal speed) |
| Query latency | 1–2ms / query @ 95% recall | 0.5–2ms / query @ 90% recall |
| Memory | Full vectors in RAM (~256 floats × 4B = 1KB/vec) | Compressed to ~16B/vec via PQ codes |
| 10B vectors at 256-dim | ~10TB RAM (infeasible single-host) | ~160GB RAM (feasible across small cluster) |
| Index build time | Slow (graph construction) | Fast (clustering + quantization) |
| Update cost | Insert touches graph layers; manageable | Re-cluster periodically; less amenable to streaming updates |
| Best for | <100M vectors, latency-critical, recall-critical | 100M+ vectors, memory-constrained, can tolerate small recall loss |

TikTok-scale (~10B videos in retrieval pool) effectively rules out single-host HNSW on memory grounds; IVF-PQ is the natural fit, with the index sharded across hosts and coarse-quantizer routing the user vector to the relevant shards. Recall loss from PQ is mitigated by retrieving more candidates than needed (e.g. top-1000 from IVF-PQ, then top-200 by exact distance using full vectors fetched from a separate vector store, then ranker on those). For shorter-tail use cases (recent videos only, ~10M vectors) HNSW gives the recall edge.

**Online learning loop.** *[Source: Google YouTube paper, ByteDance recommender talks]* Interaction events (skip, complete, like, share, follow) flow back from clients into a partitioned event stream; a streaming consumer extracts `(user_id, video_id, label)` triples and joins them with the candidate features that were used at request time (logged separately). Mini-batches of these joined examples train the ranker continuously — the model checkpoint refreshes every few minutes, so a video blowing up *right now* gets reflected in scoring within minutes, not the next day. Retrieval embeddings update *slower* (hourly batch) as a deliberate stability anchor: fully online retrieval risks runaway feedback loops where the model's own outputs distort its training distribution. This split — fast online ranker, slower batch retrieval — is standard across modern recommender systems for exactly this reason.

**Two-tower architecture in practice.** *[Source: Shaped.ai two-tower deep dive, Pinterest engineering]* The user tower takes user features (interaction history sequences, recency, demographic, current session context) and emits a 256-dim user embedding. The video tower takes video features (creator, content tags, audio embedding, visual embedding, engagement priors) and emits a 256-dim video embedding in the *same space*. Training objective is contrastive: positive pairs (user × video they engaged with) should have high cosine similarity; in-batch negatives (random other videos in the training batch) should have low cosine similarity. At serve time, only the user tower is run live (cheap, single forward pass); the video tower is run offline over the catalogue and the results are stored in the ANN index. This asymmetry is what makes retrieval sub-ms despite billions of candidates — the expensive side is precomputed.

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: How do you avoid recommendation feedback loops / filter bubbles?**
Inject exploration: ~10% of feed slots come from outside the user's cluster (random trending, diverse-creator candidates). Multi-task ranking optimizes for variety alongside engagement. *If pushed:* tag content topics and enforce diversity quotas in the final mix; track per-user topic-entropy as a metric and alert when it collapses.

**Q: Cold start — brand new user, no signal at all. What do they see?**
Demographic-bucket defaults (age, region, language, device) → pre-curated "evergreen" pool of high-completion content. After ~10 interactions, the user embedding has enough signal for the regular two-tower retrieval. *If pushed:* explicit onboarding (pick 3 interests) gives a faster warm start; for brand-new videos, seed the embedding from creator history + content features (CV/audio).

**Q: How fresh is the ranker — what's the training latency?**
Online learning on the interaction stream — features and partial gradients flow continuously; the ranker checkpoint refreshes every few minutes. Retrieval embeddings update slower (hourly batch). *If pushed:* fully online for retrieval is dangerous (embedding drift can amplify feedback loops); the slower batch on retrieval is a deliberate stability anchor.

**Q: How do you handle a viral video that 100M users want at once?**
The CDN absorbs it almost entirely — origin sees one fetch per edge POP. Pre-warm to all edges as soon as the ranker scores a video into the "rising" bucket. *If pushed:* shard like-counters on hot videos; serve view counts from approximate sketches (HyperLogLog) since exact count on a viral post isn't worth the write contention.

**Q: How do you moderate at 100M uploads/day?**
Multi-stage: CV + audio fingerprint match against a hash database of known-bad content (instant block); ML classifier scores nudity/violence/hate at upload; human reviewers handle the model's uncertain band. *If pushed:* hash lookups on PhotoDNA-equivalent fingerprints catch known CSAM before transcoding completes; high-velocity creators get pre-publish review during reputation-build.

**Q: What if the recommendation service is down?**
Degrade to a "Following" feed (chronological from followed creators) plus a global trending pool. Worse personalization, but the app still works. *If pushed:* keep a per-user candidate cache (last good ~200 video_ids) so even with the recommender fully down, individual users get a personalized fallback.

**Q: HNSW or IVF-PQ for the retrieval index — how do you choose?**
Dataset size and memory budget decide. HNSW gives ~95% recall@10 at 1–2ms/query but stores full vectors in RAM (~1KB per 256-dim vector, so ~10TB for 10B videos — infeasible single-host). IVF-PQ compresses vectors to ~16B via product quantization (160GB for 10B), trading 5–10% recall for 60x memory reduction. At TikTok scale IVF-PQ is the only feasible option; recall loss is mitigated by over-retrieving (top-1000 from IVF-PQ, then re-rank top-200 by exact distance). *If pushed:* hybrid — IVF-PQ for the long-tail catalogue, HNSW for a hot subset (recent + trending) to give recall guarantees on the most-likely-relevant candidates.

**Q: Why update retrieval embeddings hourly batch but ranker continuously?**
Online retrieval is dangerous: the model's own outputs (which videos it recommends) become its training inputs (which videos users watch), creating feedback loops where embeddings drift toward whatever was recommended yesterday. Slower batch updates anchor the embedding space against a longer-term ground truth. The ranker, by contrast, is downstream of retrieval — it scores candidates within a frozen retrieval set, so its updates affect ordering but not which videos exist as candidates. Online ranker captures hour-scale trends without destabilising the embedding space. *If pushed:* this split is standard at YouTube, Pinterest, and ByteDance for the same reason.

**Q: Two-tower model — what are positive and negative training examples?**
Positive pairs are (user, video) where the user engaged meaningfully (watch >50%, like, share). Negatives are sampled in-batch — for a positive (user_A, video_A), use (user_A, video_B) for B in the same batch as negatives, since it's astronomically unlikely user_A also engaged with video_B in the same batch. The contrastive loss pushes positive pairs to high cosine similarity and in-batch negatives to low. *If pushed:* hard-negative mining (use videos the user explicitly skipped as negatives) sharpens the embedding space at higher training cost; in-batch negatives are the cheap pragmatic default.

**Q: How does cold-start handle a brand-new video before it has any interaction signal?**
The video tower is feature-based: it computes the video embedding from creator history, content tags, CV embedding of the visual track, and audio embedding of the soundtrack — no engagement priors needed. So a fresh upload gets a plausible embedding immediately and lands in retrieval candidate pools. The first ~hundreds of views are exploration; once interaction signal accumulates, the embedding is refined by retraining. *If pushed:* explicit exploration budgets (a small percentage of feed slots reserved for fresh content) ensure cold-start videos get the impressions needed to gather signal.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Video start latency** — TikTok's product is "swipe and a video plays instantly"; if start latency exceeds ~300ms, users perceive lag and engagement drops measurably. *Mitigation:* aggressive CDN edge prefetch — as soon as a video is scored high by the ranker, push its first segment to all relevant edge POPs in the user's region before the user requests it. The feed response embeds the manifest URL and signed first-chunk URL so the client can begin playback without a separate manifest round-trip. Client preloads the next 2-3 videos' first chunks in the background so swipes are served from local storage.
- **Recommendation model serving** — every feed request runs ML inference (retrieval ANN + ranker neural net); at 1M feed requests/sec the inference cluster is the cost dominator and any latency spike directly degrades user experience. *Mitigation:* a dedicated online inference cluster with GPU/TPU instances autoscales on request rate; retrieval embeddings are cached in process memory so ANN queries don't hit a remote vector store every time. Per-user candidate cache (last good ~200 video IDs) serves as a degraded fallback during inference outages — users still get personalized content even when the live model is down. Model versions are A/B-routed so a bad model deployment can be rolled back without redeploying.
- **Storage** — 1PB/day raw upload + 2.5PB transcoded variants would cost prohibitive amounts at hot-tier object-store rates if every video stayed hot forever. *Mitigation:* observed access patterns drive aggressive tiering — ~40% of uploads receive <100 views and tier to cold within 14 days; popular content stays in hot tier. Long-tail unwatched videos archive to glacial storage; if surfaced again later (creator becomes popular, retroactive virality), they're rehydrated on demand with the cost paid by the rare access. Variant-level tiering applies — thumbnails and 480p stay hot longer than full-resolution.
- **Transcoding queue** — encoding video is GPU-bound; a global event drives 10x normal upload rate, the queue grows, creators see "processing…" for hours. *Mitigation:* GPU worker pool autoscales on queue depth (with cost guardrails — GPU instances are expensive); priority lanes process active-creator uploads ahead of brand-new accounts; the lowest-quality variant (240p preview) is generated synchronously on the upload edge so the post is feed-eligible immediately while higher-quality variants fill in async. For event-driven spikes, pre-provision GPU capacity ahead of expected events (election, sports finals) rather than relying on reactive autoscaling alone.
- **Spam/abuse** — at 100M uploads/day, manual moderation is impossible; bad actors flood the platform with reposted CSAM, violent content, IP-violating duplicates, or coordinated misinformation. *Mitigation:* multi-stage moderation pipeline: CV + audio fingerprinting against PhotoDNA-equivalent hash databases catches known-bad content before transcoding completes (instant block, no human in loop); ML classifiers score nudity / violence / hate at ingest, with high-confidence matches blocked and uncertain-band content routed to human reviewers; creator-reputation embeddings let high-velocity new accounts hit pre-publish review until reputation builds. Audio fingerprinting catches re-uploads of banned content even when the visual track is altered.

#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Retrieval index | ANN shard or vector-serving node is unavailable | Retrieval timeout and empty-candidate rate | Fall back to cached per-user candidate set or simpler non-personalized channels |
| Ranker serving | Online ranker model is slow or unhealthy | Inference p99 and model-error rate | Serve retrieval-only or following/trending fallback while preserving feed continuity |
| Embedding freshness | User/video embeddings drift or lag behind interaction patterns | Embedding-age metrics and CTR/watch-rate regression | Refresh ranker online quickly, refresh retrieval embeddings on scheduled cadence, and monitor divergence |
| Preload path | Client preloads waste bandwidth or fail on slow networks | Preload hit ratio and wasted-prefetch bytes | Adapt preload depth and quality by network class and device constraints |
| Moderation / policy | Unsafe video clears recommendation eligibility incorrectly | Post-publish takedown rate and moderation-lag alerts | Run safety filters before ranking output is eligible and tombstone content at hydration time |
| Interaction stream | Event pipeline lag starves online learning of fresh labels | Stream lag and model-freshness SLO | Fall back to last-good model checkpoint and keep retrieval/ranking decoupled |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Swipe-to-start latency** — the most product-critical playback metric.
- **Retrieval latency and empty-candidate rate** — tells you whether ANN/search channels are healthy.
- **Ranker inference latency** — main compute-path SLO for the recommendation service.
- **Preload hit rate** — direct measure of how often the next swipe is already buffered.
- **Watch-completion / skip rate** by cohort — best recommendation-quality health indicators.
- **Model freshness** — time since latest online-ranker checkpoint and latest retrieval-embedding refresh.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** feed serving is regional; embeddings and media are replicated globally, but ranking is usually done near the user.
- **RTO:** regional feed-service failover should be fast, with cached candidates providing degraded continuity.
- **RPO:** interaction events can tolerate tiny replay windows; uploaded videos and metadata need durable persistence before eligibility.
- **Failover cadence:** recommendation fallback drills matter as much as raw media-serving failover.
- **Cross-region cost:** large on media/CDN, moderate on embeddings, huge on interaction logs.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **One-stage ranking over the full corpus.** It is computationally impossible at TikTok scale.
- **No client preload.** Swipe-to-play latency is the product; without preload the UX breaks.
- **Nightly-only model updates.** Hour-scale trends are too important for that.
- **Using HNSW blindly for a 10B-video corpus.** Memory economics often force compressed ANN approaches like IVF-PQ.
- **Treating exact view counts as hot-path truth for viral videos.** Approximate or sharded counters are usually sufficient in the product path.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Retrieve then rank** is the first thing to say; it is the core architecture.
- **Client preload of the next 2–3 videos** is a product-defining systems choice, not a UI detail.
- **Online ranker with slower retrieval-embedding refresh** is the stability-vs-freshness trade to surface explicitly.
- **Sounds and hashtags are retrieval channels, not just metadata.**
- **Cold start comes from demographics and content features, not magic.**
- **Have a degraded fallback feed ready.** A recommender outage cannot mean an empty app.

### 29. Design Uber / Lyft (Ride Hailing)
#### Problem
Match riders to nearby drivers in real time, track trips, compute fares, and process payments.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a taxi dispatcher at a busy airport with a radio and a map of pins showing every cab's location, updated every 4 seconds. When a passenger calls, the dispatcher glances at the map, picks the closest available cab, radios it an offer, and waits for acknowledgment. If the driver ignores the radio, the dispatcher calls the next closest. The whole exchange takes 10–15 seconds. Uber does this for millions of passengers simultaneously, each dispatcher only covering their city, each with a map that refreshes every 4 seconds.

**The single-request walkthrough:** a rider in San Francisco requests a UberX at coordinates (37.78, -122.41). The Matching Service runs `GEORADIUS drivers:sf -122.41 37.78 2km` against Redis — a single in-memory command that returns all driver IDs within 2km in about 5ms. There are 47 results. The service filters to `status=available` and `vehicle_type=uberx` — 23 remain. It scores each by estimated arrival time (computed from pre-cached travel-time tiles, not a live routing call) plus driver rating. The top scorer is driver D-449. The service pushes a job offer to D-449's phone via WebSocket: "rider at [pickup], $12 fare, 3 min away." D-449 has 12 seconds to accept. They tap Accept. The Matching Service creates a trip record in Cassandra with state `MATCHED` and notifies both sides. Total time from rider tap to match: under 2 seconds.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Redis GEO (geo index)** — an in-memory data structure that stores geographic coordinates alongside an identifier. Under the hood it encodes each point as a **geohash** (a compact string that approximates the coordinate by subdividing a map into cells — nearby cells share a prefix). `GEORADIUS` returns all entries within a radius in milliseconds because it only needs to scan a small prefix range of the sorted set.
- **H3 hexagonal grid** — Uber's own geographic indexing system. It divides the Earth into hexagonal cells of equal size. Hexagons beat squares because all 6 neighbors of a hexagon are at the same distance from the center — no "diagonal neighbor is farther away" problem that distorts proximity calculations with squares. Uber uses H3 cells for surge pricing zones (each cell has its own multiplier) and for dispatching candidates.
- **Trip state machine (FSM)** — every trip is a record in Cassandra with a `status` field that can only move forward through defined states: `REQUESTED → MATCHED → DRIVER_ENROUTE → ARRIVED → ON_TRIP → COMPLETED → PAID`. Every transition is a **CAS** (compare-and-swap) database write — it only succeeds if the current state matches the expected prior state. If a stale retry tries to move a `COMPLETED` trip back to `ON_TRIP`, the database rejects it. This makes the trip lifecycle immune to duplicate requests and network retries.
- **Surge pricing** — a multiplier (e.g., 1.5×) applied to fares in a geographic cell when demand exceeds supply. A Flink streaming job computes the ratio of ride requests to available drivers per H3 cell every 60 seconds. When the ratio crosses a threshold, it writes the multiplier to a Redis cache keyed by cell ID. The multiplier is locked onto the rider's trip at request time — a price quoted to the rider does not change during the 90 seconds before a driver arrives.
- **Kafka + Cassandra for history** — every driver location ping (every 4 seconds, from 1 million concurrent drivers) writes to Redis for the live index (fast, in-memory, discarded after 10 seconds) and also to a Kafka stream that flows into Cassandra for historical analytics, fraud detection, and route replay. The live path and the history path are completely separate — a Kafka outage does not affect matching.

**The thing that makes it hard — the concert-end surge:** 10,000 people leave Madison Square Garden simultaneously and all open Uber within 60 seconds. The Matching Service for New York City suddenly receives 10,000 ride requests in the same H3 cell. The surge pricing multiplier kicks in (within 60 seconds). But even with surge, 10,000 simultaneous dispatch searches all query the same Redis GEO key `drivers:nyc` in parallel — the Redis node for that key can handle about 200,000 commands per second, so 10,000 simultaneous `GEORADIUS` queries at ~5ms each would take 50 seconds to process serially. The fix: Redis is multi-threaded for reads (concurrent `GEORADIUS` queries on the same key execute in parallel), and the Matching Service per city is a pool of stateless workers that auto-scale. The Redis node sees a spike in CPU but not a serial queue.

**Why the standard solution works:** the key architectural decision is keeping the live driver index in Redis (in-memory, fast reads) completely separate from the trip history in Cassandra (durable, slow writes acceptable). Writing 250,000 location pings per second to Cassandra directly would require a massive cluster and add latency to the match path. Redis absorbs the writes in microseconds; Cassandra absorbs the history writes asynchronously via Kafka, with no impact on matching speed. The tradeoff: a Redis crash loses the current driver positions (up to 10 seconds worth). This is acceptable — drivers send a new ping within 4 seconds, so the index self-heals.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Redis Cluster (sharded by city) for the live geo index; TTL of 10s on each driver entry so dead phones auto-expire. Kafka for location event streaming. Cassandra for trip records and history.
- Matching hot path:
  ```
  candidates = redis.georadius(f"drivers:{city}", lat, lng, radius=2km)
  candidates = [d for d in candidates if d.status == 'available']
  scores = [(driver_id, eta_tile_lookup(driver_loc, pickup_loc) + 0.1*rating) for ...]
  top_driver = min(scores, key=lambda x: x[1])
  websocket.push(top_driver.id, offer_message)
  // on accept:
  cassandra.cas(trip_id, expected_state=REQUESTED, new_state=MATCHED)
  ```
- Surge: Flink job consuming the rider-request Kafka stream, grouping by H3 cell on a 1-minute tumbling window, writing `surge:{cell_id}` to Redis when demand:supply ratio > threshold.
#### Clarifying Questions
- City-by-city or global?
- Surge pricing?
- Pool / shared rides?
- ETA prediction quality?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** rider request → match driver → track location during trip → fare calculation → payment
- **NFR:** sub-2s match in dense areas, accurate ETAs, handle spikes (rush hour, events)

#### Scale Estimate
- **MAU / rides:** ~100M MAU (Uber 2024 public disclosures); ~10M rides/day globally → 10M / 86,400 s ≈ ~120 rides/s avg; 10× rush-hour multiplier (Uber eng posts) → ~1,200 rides/s peak
- **Active drivers / pings:** ~5M weekly active drivers; ~20% on-shift concurrency at peak → ~1M concurrent; ping cadence 4 s → 1M / 4 s = 250k location updates/s
- **Match scan size:** GEORADIUS 2 km in a dense city: density ~5–15 drivers/km² × π × 2² ≈ 60–180, filtered to available/right-vehicle ≈ ~50 candidates
- **Location ping size:** ~100 B = (driver_id 8 B + lat 8 B + lng 8 B + heading 4 B + speed 4 B + status 1 B + ts 8 B + Protobuf framing ~60 B)
- **Location ping volume:** 250k pings/s × 100 B = 25 MB/s on the hot in-memory path; 25 MB/s × 86,400 s = ~2.16 TB/day raw → 5× columnar compression (numeric/timestamp columns) ≈ ~430 GB/day archived
- **Hot geo state:** 1M concurrent drivers × 100 B = 100 MB current-position footprint per city shard, fits trivially in a RAM-resident sorted set
- **Trip record size:** ~2 KB per trip = (polyline ~1 KB at ~50 lat/lng × 16 B + fare ~200 B + surge ~50 B + 6 timestamps × 8 B + ratings ~100 B + IDs ~150 B + framing); 2 KB × 10M trips/day = 20 GB/day; 7-yr retention (US tax/dispute) → 20 GB × 365 × 7 ≈ ~51 TB cold columnar
- **History stream retention:** 25 MB/s × 86,400 s = 2.16 TB/day raw; 24 h hot replay × RF 3 + index/tombstone overhead ≈ ~25 TB on SSD

#### API Contract
```
POST /ride/request    body: { pickup, dropoff, vehicle_type } → { request_id }
GET  /ride/{id}/status  → { state, driver_loc, eta }
POST /driver/location  body: { lat, lng, ts, status }    (every 4s)
WS   /driver/jobs      ← push job offers
```

#### High-Level Design
```
                 ┌──────────────────────────┐
   Drivers ─────→│  Location Ingest (WS)    │
   (loc/4s)      │  → Redis Geo (current)   │
                 │  → Kafka (history)       │
                 └──────────────────────────┘
                              ↑
                 ┌──────────────────────────┐
   Rider req ───→│   Matching Service       │
                 │  1. Geo query "drivers   │
                 │     within 2km, free"    │
                 │  2. Score (ETA, rating,  │
                 │     pool eligibility)    │
                 │  3. Offer to top driver  │
                 │     via WS               │
                 │  4. On accept → trip svc │
                 │     On reject → next     │
                 └──────────────────────────┘
                              ↓
                 ┌──────────────────────────┐
                 │   Trip Service (FSM)     │
                 │   states: requested →    │
                 │   matched → enroute →    │
                 │   on_trip → completed    │
                 └─┬──────────────────────┬─┘
                   ↓                      ↓
              Routing Svc            Pricing Svc
              (Maps API)             (surge multiplier)
                                          ↓
                                     Payment Svc
```

**How to read the diagram:** live location flows in from riders and drivers, a dispatch layer matches them, and a trip state machine tracks what happens next. Search, pricing, and notifications all hang off that moving state.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** the world keeps changing while the system is making decisions. A static database lookup is not enough when both sides are moving and local conditions can flip in seconds.

**What this layout buys you:** responsive matching and a cleaner way to reason about the ride lifecycle. The tradeoff is that geo state, dispatch, and trip transitions all need to be treated as first-class real-time concerns.

#### Data Model
- **drivers_locations** (Redis GEO):
  `GEOADD drivers:{city} lng lat driver_id`, refreshed every 4s, TTL 10s
- **trips** (Cassandra): `(trip_id, rider_id, driver_id, state, pickup, dropoff, fare, ts_*)`
- **trip_history** (Cassandra, partition by user_month)
- **surge** (Redis): `surge:{cell_id} → multiplier`, recomputed per minute

#### Detailed Design
**Matching algorithm:**
```
On rider request at (lat, lng):
  1. Find candidates: GEORADIUS drivers:{city} lng lat 2km
     filter: status=available, vehicle_type matches
  2. Score each: ETA (real road dist) + driver rating + completion rate
  3. Offer to top driver via WS push
  4. Driver accepts within Ns → matched
                rejects → offer next
                no response → next
  5. If no match in 30s → expand radius, surge price
```

**Geo-indexing for drivers.** Redis GEO uses geohash prefixes internally — "find drivers within 2km" is a fast prefix-bounded scan over an in-memory sorted set, returning candidates in milliseconds with no disk hit. Geohash cells distort near the poles and have non-uniform neighbours (a cell's eight neighbours can be different sizes), which matters for driver-distribution math in dense areas. Uber's own H3 (hexagonal hierarchical grid) gives every cell exactly six equal-sized neighbours, making "drivers in this hex and the surrounding ring" a clean uniform query — the canonical fix once geohash skew starts hurting matching quality.

**Surge pricing.** Compute demand:supply ratio per H3 cell on a 1-minute tumbling window from the rider-request stream and the available-driver count from Redis GEO. When the ratio exceeds thresholds, write a multiplier (1.2×, 1.5×, 2×…) into the per-cell surge cache; rider clients fetch and display it before the request commits, so the price is locked at request time. Surge serves two purposes: it rations limited supply (high price = fewer requests) and pulls drivers toward the cell (the higher fare nudges nearby drivers to repo). The 1-minute cadence is a deliberate compromise — faster recompute oscillates as drivers respond.

**ETA prediction.** Routing runs Dijkstra (or A* with geographic heuristics) over the road graph weighted by current edge speeds, fed from a live traffic feed plus historical patterns by `(day_of_week, hour_of_day)`. Per-driver scoring needs an ETA per candidate, so naive scoring of 50 candidates means 50 routing calls — the optimisation is to precompute per-cell ETA tiles (travel time from cell A to cell B at this hour) so candidate scoring is a tile lookup, not a graph search. Live traffic + historical model is what gets ETAs accurate to within ~30s in normal conditions; rush-hour and incident detection are the edge cases that hurt accuracy.

**Trip state machine:**
```
REQUESTED → MATCHED → DRIVER_ENROUTE → ARRIVED → ON_TRIP → COMPLETED
                                                              ↓
                                                          PAYMENT
                                                              ↓
                                                          RATED
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> REQUESTED
    REQUESTED --> MATCHED: driver accepts offer
    REQUESTED --> CANCELLED_BY_RIDER: rider cancels (free <2 min)
    REQUESTED --> NO_DRIVERS: 30s no-match timeout
    MATCHED --> DRIVER_ENROUTE: driver starts moving
    MATCHED --> CANCELLED_BY_DRIVER: driver cancels
    MATCHED --> CANCELLED_BY_RIDER: rider cancels (fee after grace)
    DRIVER_ENROUTE --> ARRIVED: geofence trigger at pickup
    DRIVER_ENROUTE --> CANCELLED_BY_RIDER: rider cancels (fee)
    ARRIVED --> ON_TRIP: rider in-vehicle confirm
    ARRIVED --> NO_SHOW: 5 min wait timeout (rider charged)
    ON_TRIP --> COMPLETED: dropoff geofence
    COMPLETED --> PAYMENT_PENDING
    PAYMENT_PENDING --> PAID: gateway success
    PAYMENT_PENDING --> PAYMENT_RETRY: gateway fail (async retry)
    PAYMENT_RETRY --> PAID
    PAID --> RATED
    RATED --> [*]
    CANCELLED_BY_RIDER --> [*]
    CANCELLED_BY_DRIVER --> [*]
    NO_DRIVERS --> [*]
    NO_SHOW --> [*]
```

The FSM is intentionally unforgiving. Each transition is a single Cassandra row update gated on the prior state — a CAS on `(trip_id, expected_state)` so a stale client retry can't drag a `COMPLETED` trip back to `ON_TRIP`. Cancellation forks (rider, driver, no-show) are terminal states rather than booleans, so refund logic and driver-rating impact each query their own state without inferring intent. *[Source: systemdesign.io rider-matching, Uber engineering]*

**H3 hex grid: cell sizes and the 7× hierarchy.** H3 has 16 resolutions (0 coarsest, 15 finest); each finer level is *exactly* 1/7 the area of its parent because the rotation between levels packs 7 children inside one parent. Resolution 8 is Uber's dispatch workhorse — ~0.74 km² per hex, roughly a city block; resolution 9 (~0.10 km²) is for fine surge zones in dense areas. Hexagons beat squares for two concrete reasons: (1) all six neighbours are equidistant from the centre — there's only *one* "neighbour distance" — so radius-style queries (`kRing(cell, k=2)` returns the cell + 18 neighbours within 2 hops) are uniform; (2) hexagons approximate circles better, so demand kernels and supply smoothing don't suffer corner artefacts. The 12 pentagon cells exist because you can't tile a sphere with hexagons alone — they sit over ocean by design so dispatch never hits them. *[Source: Uber H3 engineering blog]*

**Dispatch matching: ExpressPool batching vs greedy.** Solo Uber's offer-to-top loop is greedy first-come-first-served — fine when the optimisation is per-rider latency. UberPool/ExpressPool flips this: the matcher accumulates pending requests in a 3-5s tumbling window and runs a *batch* assignment problem — Hungarian-algorithm-style bipartite matching of N requests to M drivers, optimising global ETA + detour cost rather than per-rider greed. Batching costs a few seconds of wait but cuts deadhead miles 20-30% and improves shared-ride compatibility (route overlap, seat count). The window is a tunable: longer = better match, worse latency. Solo stays greedy because a 3s wait without a co-rider is just worse UX. *[Source: systemdesign.io find-a-rider]*

**Surge as hysteresis-controlled price levels.** Surge multipliers don't change continuously — they snap to a discrete ladder (1.0×, 1.2×, 1.5×, 1.8×, 2.0×, 2.5×, 3.0×) with hysteresis bands so a hex doesn't oscillate between 1.5× and 1.8× every minute as the demand:supply ratio crosses one threshold. Pricing reads the per-cell multiplier *at request time* and locks it onto the trip — a rider who saw 1.5× pays 1.5× even if surge spikes to 2× during the 90s before the driver arrives. Lock-at-request is the only honest contract; recomputing at fare time would let driver route choice change the price. The 1-minute cadence is a Flink/Samza job over the request stream + live driver-supply count, keyed by H3 cell. *[Source: systemdesign.io surge-pricing]*

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: What happens if the matched driver's app crashes mid-trip?**
GPS goes stale (TTL on Redis GEO entry expires); trip FSM detects no heartbeat for ~30s and flags it. Rider sees "reconnecting"; if it doesn't recover, ops can manually reassign. *If pushed:* persist last-known-good location to Cassandra so a new driver can be routed to the rider's current position, not the pickup; consider a passenger-side "I'm safe" check-in for long stalls.

**Q: How do you prevent a driver from cherry-picking high-fare rides by repeatedly declining?**
Track per-driver acceptance rate; below a threshold their priority in the scoring function drops. Repeated declines = temporary cooldown. *If pushed:* hide destination until accept (the actual real-world fix); enforce a daily decline budget; appeal flow for legitimate refusals (unsafe area).

**Q: How would this scale 10x to 10M concurrent drivers?**
Shard Redis GEO by city and split dense cities by H3 cell ranges. Matching service becomes per-city stateless workers; location ingest fan-outs via Kafka per region. *If pushed:* the bottleneck shifts to the road graph routing service — pre-compute ETA tiles per cell so scoring doesn't hit Maps API per candidate.

**Q: How do you handle a "concert lets out" surge — 10k requests in one cell in 60s?**
Surge multiplier kicks in fast (sub-minute recompute), throttling demand. Matching service autoscales per city; expand search radius dynamically. *If pushed:* pre-warm capacity by predicting events from calendar feeds; nudge nearby drivers toward the cell with a bonus before the spike; queue requests FIFO if all drivers are taken.

**Q: Why offer-to-top instead of broadcast-auction?**
Broadcast causes "already taken" UX and wasted offers across many drivers. Sequential offer = clean state, one driver at a time. *If pushed:* cost is latency on declines; mitigate via short offer TTL (10-15s) and parallelizing the second-best offer if the first is silent past 8s.

**Q: How do you guarantee fare integrity if the rider's phone dies mid-trip?**
Trip state and GPS trail live server-side (Cassandra), driven by the driver's app. Fare is computed from the canonical server-side route, not client-claimed distance. *If pushed:* dispute flow with route replay; ML-flag anomalous routes (driver took the long way) for manual review.

**Q: Why H3 hexagons over geohash squares for the dispatch index?**
Geohash neighbours have two different distances (edge vs corner) and cells distort with latitude — `kRing` queries return non-uniform supply counts in the same nominal radius, biasing scoring. H3 has one neighbour distance, equal-area within a resolution band, and a clean 1-of-7 child hierarchy so a coarse query (`res 7` for surge zones) and a fine query (`res 9` for dispatch) share an indexable prefix. *If pushed:* H3 still has the 12 pentagon edge case at icosahedron vertices; these are placed over open ocean so production dispatch never observes them. *[Source: Uber H3]*

**Q: Solo dispatch is greedy first-come-first-served. Why is Pool batched?**
Solo's optimisation is per-rider latency — a 3s wait with no benefit is worse UX. Pool optimises global routing efficiency: batching N requests over a 3-5s window lets a Hungarian-style assignment match drivers to *combinations* of compatible riders, cutting deadhead miles ~20-30% and improving co-rider route overlap. *If pushed:* the batch window is the tunable knob; longer windows give better matches at higher rider wait. Solo + Pool can share the same matcher service if the dispatch algorithm switches based on `vehicle_type`. *[Source: systemdesign.io find-a-rider]*

**Q: How does surge avoid flapping between multipliers minute-to-minute?**
Multipliers snap to discrete steps (1.0×, 1.2×, 1.5×, 1.8×, 2.0×, 2.5×, 3.0×) with hysteresis bands — a hex must cross a higher threshold to step up but only drops back when demand falls *below* a lower threshold, so steady-state oscillation is dampened. Plus rider-side multipliers are *locked at request time*, so the 90s before driver arrival can't change the price the rider already accepted. *If pushed:* fast oscillation in the surge cache also pulls drivers in then expels them — the supply control loop benefits from the hysteresis as much as the rider-facing UX. *[Source: systemdesign.io surge-pricing]*

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Driver loc storm** (250k pings/s globally) — every active driver writes every 4s and naive Redis writes can saturate a single node. Batch updates client-side (one ping carries last-N positions when cellular drops out) and keep the TTL short (~10s) so dead drivers fall out of the index without an explicit cleanup pass.
- **Hot city / event** (concert lets out: 10k requests in 60s) — Matching Service for that city saturates and surge alone can't ration that fast. Pre-shard by city so the spike is contained; autoscale Matching workers per city; widen radius dynamically so the candidate pool grows even if the local cell is empty.
- **Match latency** (must be <2s in dense cities) — every network hop in the scoring loop hurts. Keep the geo index, surge cache, and pre-computed ETA tiles all in-memory in the same data centre as Matching workers; cross-region calls in the match path are unacceptable.
- **Driver app backgrounding** — iOS/Android suspend the GPS when the app loses focus, so the driver effectively disappears from the index even though they're still driving. Detect via missed-heartbeat (no ping in 15s) and gracefully reassign the rider to another driver rather than waiting for the original to come back.
- **Payment failures** (gateway timeout, declined card) — synchronous payment in the trip-completion path would block the FSM. Retry async with exponential backoff; trip completes regardless and the rider can settle on the next session, so a payment outage doesn't strand drivers waiting for "completed" status.
- **H3 pentagon edge cases** — 12 cells per resolution are pentagons (5 neighbours instead of 6), creating non-uniform `kRing` results if dispatch lands on one. Mitigation is geographic: pentagon centroids sit over open ocean by Uber's deliberate H3 orientation, so urban dispatch never observes them. Test fixtures still need to hit those cells to catch regressions in code that assumes 6 neighbours.
- **Pool batch window starvation** (rider sits in queue 5s with no Pool-eligible match) — fall back to solo dispatch after the window closes; otherwise Pool's batching latency punishes the rider for system inefficiency. The matcher returns a `solo_offer` if no batch assignment is found within the window.
- **Surge feedback loop** (high multiplier pulls drivers in, multiplier drops, drivers leave, multiplier spikes again) — the discrete ladder + hysteresis dampens this, but the deeper fix is asymmetric thresholds: step *up* aggressively (rider sees fair price fast), step *down* slowly (drivers don't get whiplash). Cooling-off periods on per-driver re-positioning bonuses also prevent oscillating fleet movement.

#### Failure Modes

| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Location ingest | Redis GEO node OOM under ping storm | NIC saturation + p99 GEOADD latency alert | Per-city sharding; client-side ping batching; 10s TTL auto-evicts dead drivers |
| Matching | WebSocket offer never acked (driver phone backgrounded) | Per-offer 10-15s timeout | Cascade to next-best driver; mark driver `idle` after missed heartbeat |
| Trip FSM | Stale CAS write attempts (`COMPLETED → ON_TRIP`) | DB rejected-update counter | Gate every transition on prior-state CAS; reject and log; ops alert on repeat |
| Routing | Maps API timeout / rate-limit during scoring | 5xx rate from routing service | Pre-computed cell-to-cell ETA tiles; fall back to haversine ETA on tile miss |
| Pricing | Surge cell stale (recompute job dead) | `surge:{cell}` write age > 2 min | Default to last-known multiplier; alarm; re-elect Flink job on staleness |
| Payment | Gateway 5xx at trip completion | Async retry queue depth + per-PSP error rate | Trip completes anyway; settle on next session; idempotency key prevents double-charge |
| Cross-region | DC outage in primary city region | Health probes from edge | Active-active per-city deployment; failover Matching/Trip workers; Cassandra cross-region replication for trip log |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs

- `match_latency_p99_ms` per city — target < 2,000 ms in dense cities, < 5,000 ms tail.
- `unmatched_request_rate` per city — > 2% triggers capacity / surge investigation.
- `driver_offer_accept_rate` — fleet-wide < 60% suggests scoring or fare regression.
- `geoadd_lag_p99_ms` per city shard — target < 50 ms; spikes mean Redis hot-shard.
- `trip_fsm_stuck_count` (trips in non-terminal state > expected duration) — paged when > 0.5% of in-flight trips.
- `payment_settlement_lag_p99` — target < 30 min from `COMPLETED → PAID`; longer suggests gateway saturation.
- SLO: 99.9% of `POST /ride/request` returning a `MATCHED` event within 30 s in covered cities; error budget burn rate alerted at 2× and 14×.

#### Multi-Region & DR

- **Replication mode:** active-active per-city. Each city shard runs in its closest region; Redis GEO is region-local (rebuilt from Kafka on cold start), trip Cassandra cluster is multi-region with `LOCAL_QUORUM` writes and async replication for cross-region read replicas (history, fraud, analytics).
- **RTO:** ~60 s for Matching/Trip workers (stateless, restart and resume from Kafka offsets); ~5 min for a full city failover (Redis warm-fill from Kafka log replay).
- **RPO:** < 5 s for trip FSM transitions (Cassandra `LOCAL_QUORUM`); ~30 s on cross-region Cassandra replication for analytics replicas.
- **Failover cadence:** quarterly DR game-day per major region; chaos exercises monthly.
- **Cross-region cost:** trips and locations are city-local, so cross-region traffic is mostly Kafka history replication and metadata sync — under 5% of total egress.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns

- *Broadcasting offers to every nearby driver.* Causes "already taken" UX, wasted offers, and contention; offer-to-top sequentially with short TTL is the canonical model.
- *Putting durable history on the match path.* Matching must read/write only the in-memory geo index; Kafka history is async, never blocking.
- *Using geohash naively for dispatch quality.* Cell-size distortion at high latitude and uneven neighbour distances bias scoring; H3 hexagons are the canonical fix once geohash skew bites.
- *Synchronous payment in the trip-completion FSM transition.* Couples trip completion to gateway availability; payment must be async with retry, trip completes regardless.
- *Storing driver location only in Cassandra.* Cassandra writes per ping at 250k/s saturate the cluster; the in-memory hot path + async stream-to-history is the only viable shape.
- *Continuous surge multipliers without hysteresis.* Causes flapping every minute; discrete ladder + asymmetric thresholds is the correct mechanism.

#### Talking Points for the Interview

- **Per-city sharding plus in-memory geo index** is the foundation; Redis GEO + per-city Matching pool + Kafka history is the canonical hot/cold split. State this early.
- **Offer-to-top, not broadcast** — sequential offers with short TTL beat broadcast auctions on UX and on system cost.
- **Trip FSM with CAS-gated transitions** — every state change is one DB row update keyed on prior state, so stale retries can't drag a `COMPLETED` trip backward.
- **H3 over geohash** for dispatch quality — equal-distance neighbours, equal-area cells, clean 1-of-7 child hierarchy. Mention pentagon edge case is sited over open ocean.
- **Surge as discrete-step hysteresis** — multipliers snap to a ladder, locked at request time, asymmetric thresholds (up fast, down slow) prevent flapping.
- **Pool batched, solo greedy** — Hungarian-style bipartite match over a 3-5s window for Pool; greedy first-come for solo. Showing you know both modes signals real operational depth.
- **Fencing the match path against transit history latency** — Kafka backpressure must never block matching; the asymmetric write paths are intentional.

### 30. Design Airbnb (Booking Platform)
#### Problem
Two-sided marketplace: hosts list properties, guests search, book, pay, review — with strict no-double-booking.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a hotel front desk with one twist — each room is managed by an independent owner who sets their own prices and availability, and 100,000 people are browsing the hotel simultaneously.
