The front desk can show the general availability board to all 100,000 browsers instantly (approximate), but when someone actually tries to check in, only one person can get the key — and the front desk must physically lock the calendar for that room during the checkout process.

**The single-request walkthrough:** Alice searches for a 2-bedroom in Paris for June 14–17. The search service queries Elasticsearch with filters: city=Paris, guests≥2, June 14-17 available, price under €200/night. The search index has a pre-computed field "any night booked June 14–17" that was updated when the last booking happened (via a **CDC** feed — Change Data Capture, a system that captures every database write and streams it elsewhere in real-time). The results show 47 listings — all approximately available. Alice picks one and clicks Book. Now the system switches from approximate to exact: the Booking Orchestrator opens a Postgres transaction and runs `SELECT FOR UPDATE` on the calendar rows for (listing_id, June 14), (listing_id, June 15), (listing_id, June 16) — this statement locks those three rows so no concurrent booking can touch them until the transaction completes. It checks `status = available` for all three. They are. It updates all three to `status = booked`. It calls Stripe to authorize Alice's card for the full amount (but not capture yet — the money is just held). It writes the booking record and sends confirmation emails. If Stripe fails, the transaction rolls back and the calendar rows return to `available`. Total booking time: ~1 second.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Per-night calendar rows** — each listing has one database row per date: `(listing_id, date, status, price_override)`. The primary key is `(listing_id, date)`, which means two booking attempts for the same listing and overlapping dates cannot both succeed — the database enforces uniqueness at the row level.
- **SELECT FOR UPDATE (pessimistic locking)** — a database instruction that says "give me these rows AND lock them so nobody else can read or write them until I'm done." This is the only place in the entire system where strict ordering is enforced. Everything else can be eventually consistent; the calendar lock is the one hill worth dying on for correctness.
- **Saga with compensating transactions** — after the calendar lock succeeds, the Stripe authorization is the next step. If Stripe declines the card, the Saga runs a compensation: it updates the calendar rows back to `status = available`. This is the same Saga pattern as in the Payment System design (#23) — multi-step workflow where each step has a defined "undo."
- **Elasticsearch search index** — a search engine database optimized for full-text and filtered queries. The listing's document in the index includes pre-computed availability fields ("any night booked in June?") that are refreshed via the CDC pipeline within about 60 seconds of a booking. This means search results are slightly stale — a listing might appear available when it's actually booked in the last minute. This is intentional: strong consistency on search would require querying every listing's calendar row for every search, which would be 10 million database reads per search query. The booking endpoint re-validates, so the stale result just causes an "availability lost" response to the unlucky second booker.
- **Auth-not-capture (escrow)** — Stripe's card authorization puts a hold on Alice's card but does not actually move money. The money is only transferred ("captured") 24 hours after check-in, when Airbnb confirms the stay happened. This protects both parties: Alice gets a refund if the host cancels, the host knows funds are secured before the guest arrives.
- **Idempotency keys (Orpheus pattern)** — Alice accidentally double-taps "Book." Two requests hit the server. The Booking service stores `(idempotency_key → booking_id)` before starting the Saga. The second request finds the existing key and returns the cached result — same booking ID, same confirmation, zero duplicate charge.

**The thing that makes it hard — two users, one listing, same millisecond:** the Super Bowl is in Las Vegas. A listing near the stadium gets 200 booking attempts within 60 seconds of going live. All 200 requests read the Elasticsearch index and see "available." All 200 reach the Booking Orchestrator. All 200 try to run `SELECT FOR UPDATE` on the calendar rows for the same dates. The database serializes them: the first transaction acquires the lock and proceeds to book. The other 199 block, waiting for the lock. When the first transaction commits, the calendar rows show `status=booked`. The 199 unblock, each reads `status=booked`, each returns "AVAILABILITY_LOST." Only one booking succeeds. This is correct — and it happens at the database level, requiring no application-level coordination logic.

**Why the standard solution works:** the split between an eventually consistent search index (Elasticsearch) and a strongly consistent calendar (Postgres with row-level locks) is the right architecture because these are fundamentally different problems. Search needs to handle 12,000 queries per second across 10 million listings — strong consistency here would require locking millions of rows on every search. The calendar needs to handle 200 concurrent booking attempts on one listing — here you need strict serialization and cannot afford to be wrong. Keeping them separate lets each subsystem use the right consistency model without forcing the other to pay an unnecessary cost.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Calendar: PostgreSQL, table `calendar(listing_id, date, status, price_override)` with PK `(listing_id, date)`. All calendar writes on the primary (no replicas for writes — replica lag here causes double-booking).
- Search: Elasticsearch cluster with listing documents; CDC pipeline (Debezium → Kafka → ES indexer) refreshing availability fields within 60 seconds of a booking.
- Booking Saga pseudocode:
  ```
  write idempotency_key=IN_PROGRESS to Postgres (master only)
  BEGIN;
  SELECT FOR UPDATE FROM calendar WHERE listing_id=X AND date IN (Jun14, Jun15, Jun16);
  if any row has status != 'available': ROLLBACK; return AVAILABILITY_LOST
  UPDATE calendar SET status='booked' WHERE ...;
  stripe.authorize(amount, idempotency_key=booking_id+":auth")
  if stripe fails: ROLLBACK; return PAYMENT_FAILED
  INSERT INTO bookings VALUES (...);
  COMMIT;
  update idempotency_key=DONE; notify host and guest
  ```
#### Clarifying Questions
- Inventory granularity (per-listing per-night)?
- Instant book vs request-to-book?
- Multiple guests, dates, filters?
- Pricing dynamic per host?
- Cancellation policies?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** search by location/dates/filters, view listing, book (instant or request), payment (escrow), reviews, messaging
- **NFR:** strong consistency on calendar; eventually consistent search; 99.99% availability

#### Scale Estimate
- **Listings / bookings / searches:** ~7M listings (Airbnb 2024 public Q-filings, rounded to 10M for headroom); ~2M nightly stays at peak season → ~1M new bookings/day amortised across calendar; search:book = 100:1 industry rule of thumb → 100M searches/day → 100M / 86,400 ≈ ~1,160 search QPS avg, 10× peak ≈ ~12k QPS
- **Payment volume:** 1M bookings/day × 365 = ~365M bookings/yr × avg ~$200 fare ≈ ~$70B GBV/yr (matches Airbnb 2024 disclosed run-rate); "billions/year" reflects payment count not dollars
- **Listing record size:** ~10 KB metadata = (title ~100 B + description ~2 KB + amenities array ~500 B + policies ~500 B + lat/lng + host_id + reviews summary + booking rules ~6 KB framing); media: ~30 photos × 200 KB thumbnail (full-res cached separately on CDN) = ~6 MB
- **Listings storage:** 10M × 10 KB = ~100 GB hot in Postgres-class store; 10M × 6 MB = ~60 TB object store; CDN tier-1 cache hits ≈ ~95%
- **Calendar storage:** 10M listings × 365 days × ~50 B/row (listing_id 8 B + date 4 B + status 1 B + price_override 4 B + framing/index overhead ~33 B) = 10M × 365 × 50 ≈ ~180 GB hot; 5-yr analytics retention → ~900 GB cold
- **Booking record size:** ~2 KB per booking = (parties ~100 B + dates 16 B + fare breakdown ~300 B + payment ref ~200 B + state history ~500 B + idempotency/audit ~900 B); 2 KB × 1M/day × 365 × 7 yr ≈ ~5 TB cold archive
- **Search-index footprint:** 10M docs × ~5 KB (denormalised: filters + availability summary + ranking features) = ~50 GB raw; ES replication × 3 = ~150 GB across cluster
- **Daily booking ingest:** 1M × 2 KB = 2 GB/day raw; CDC events ≈ 1M / 86,400 ≈ ~12 events/s avg; 10× peak (weekend booking surge) ≈ ~120 events/s feeding search re-index

#### API Contract
```
GET  /search           params: location, checkin, checkout, guests, filters
                       → [{listing_id, name, price, photos[], avail}]
GET  /listing/{id}     → details + calendar
POST /booking          body: { listing_id, dates, guests, payment_method, idempotency_key }
                       → { booking_id, status }
POST /review           body: { booking_id, rating, text }
```

#### High-Level Design
```
                                ┌──────────────────────┐
       Search ──────────────→  │   Search Service     │ → Search index
                                │   (geo + filters)    │   (denormalized)
                                └──────────────────────┘            ↑
                                                                    │ CDC
                                ┌──────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
       Browse listing ───────→ │  Listing Service     │ → │ Listings DB     │
                                └──────────────────────┘    │ (transactional) │
                                                            └──────────────────┘
                                ┌──────────────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐
       Book ────────────────→ │  Booking Orchestrator│ → │ Calendar Service  │
                                │  (Saga)              │    │ (per-listing,    │
                                └────┬─────────────────┘    │  per-night row)  │
                                     │                      └──────────────────┘
                                     ↓
                              ┌──────────────┐
                              │ Payment Svc  │ (escrow: hold until check-in)
                              └──────────────┘
                                     ↓
                              ┌──────────────┐
                              │ Notify (host │
                              │  + guest)    │
                              └──────────────┘
```

**How to read the diagram:** there is a discovery side and a reservation side. Search helps users find listings they might like, but booking flows through a tighter inventory path that deals with actual night-by-night availability and holds.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** users browse broadly, but bookings must be exact. Separating those concerns lets search stay fast and expressive while keeping reservation correctness concentrated in a smaller, more transactional path.

**What this layout buys you:** better user-facing search without weakening booking integrity. The tradeoff is that search results can only ever be an approximation of inventory until the booking path confirms them.

#### Data Model
- **listings** (transactional store): `(listing_id, host_id, title, address, lat, lng, amenities[], policies)`
- **calendar** (transactional store, sharded by listing_id):
  `(listing_id, date, status: available|booked|blocked, price_override?)` PK = `(listing_id, date)`
- **bookings**: `(booking_id, guest, listing_id, checkin, checkout, total, state)`
- **search_index**: denormalized listing doc with current price + availability summary
- **reviews**: `(review_id, booking_id, rating, text, ts)`

#### Detailed Design
**Booking flow (Saga):**
```
1. Validate dates not in past, listing exists
2. Calendar Service: lock dates (SQL txn with row-level lock)
   - For each date in range: SELECT FOR UPDATE; check status=available
   - UPDATE status=booked
   - On any conflict → abort, return AVAILABILITY_LOST
3. Payment Service: charge guest (or hold via Stripe)
   - On fail → compensate calendar (release dates)
4. Booking persisted; notifications sent
5. Funds in escrow until 24h after check-in
```

**Instant book vs request-to-book.** Per-listing flag set by the host. *Instant book*: the Saga above runs end-to-end on `POST /booking` — calendar locks immediately, payment auth fires, host sees a confirmed reservation. *Request-to-book*: the calendar row is *soft-held* (status=`pending`, TTL 24h) and the payment is auth'd but not finalised; host has 24h to accept or decline. Accept flips pending→booked and resumes the Saga. Decline / timeout releases the soft-hold and voids the auth. The same `(listing_id, date)` PK guarantees no double-booking in either mode — a request-to-book pending hold blocks instant-book attempts on the same date until it resolves. Most hosts default to instant-book; request-to-book is opt-in for stricter screening.

**Search.** ES query combines a geo bounding box (from the user's map viewport) with a date-availability filter and arbitrary attribute filters (price, amenities, guest count). Availability needs care — checking each night against the calendar at search time is too slow, so the search doc stores a pre-computed boolean "any night booked in `[checkin, checkout]`" that's refreshed via CDC; this gets you 99% of cases with one document hit. For correctness, the booking endpoint re-validates the calendar at commit, so a stale search result just produces an `AVAILABILITY_LOST` response. `(city, date_range)` queries cache for 5min in front of ES, which absorbs most repeat traffic on hot weekends. Personalisation re-ranks the top 200 results by the user's prior click and book history.

**Pricing.** Smart Pricing is an ML model trained on (booking, no-booking, cancellation) outcomes against (date, location, demand, comparable listings) features — it produces a suggested price per night. Hosts always have the final say; the suggestion lands as a default they can override per-night via the calendar UI, so each calendar row carries an optional `price_override`. Service fees (~14% guest, ~3% host) are added at checkout, not stored on the listing. The override-per-night model is what lets hosts charge weekend premiums and event-week multipliers without ML.

**Reviews.** Both guest and host submit a review within 14 days of checkout; neither sees the other's content while writing. They're released simultaneously the moment the second one submits, or at the 14-day deadline if only one submits. This is the structural fix for retaliatory review trading — without dual-blind release, a bad guest knows their host already wrote something nice and freely retaliates. Reviews feed the ranker as both a quality signal (high-rated listings rank higher) and a trust signal (verified-stay reviews weighted heavier than older ones).

**Booking + review FSM.**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SEARCHED
    SEARCHED --> REQUESTED: guest hits Book
    REQUESTED --> CALENDAR_LOCKED: SELECT FOR UPDATE on date rows
    CALENDAR_LOCKED --> AVAILABILITY_LOST: another booker won the row
    CALENDAR_LOCKED --> AUTH_PENDING: lock acquired
    AUTH_PENDING --> AUTHORIZED: Stripe hold succeeds
    AUTH_PENDING --> AUTH_FAILED: card declined → release calendar
    AUTHORIZED --> CONFIRMED: notify host + guest
    CONFIRMED --> RE_AUTH: ~5d before check-in if >7d window
    RE_AUTH --> CONFIRMED: re-auth succeeds
    RE_AUTH --> CANCELLED_PAYMENT: re-auth fails → notify guest
    CONFIRMED --> CHECKED_IN: stay starts
    CONFIRMED --> CANCELLED: guest/host cancellation per policy
    CHECKED_IN --> CAPTURED: 24h after check-in → funds to host escrow
    CAPTURED --> AWAITING_REVIEWS
    AWAITING_REVIEWS --> REVIEWS_LOCKED: one party submitted, holding for the other
    REVIEWS_LOCKED --> REVIEWS_PUBLISHED: both submit OR 14d deadline
    AWAITING_REVIEWS --> REVIEWS_PUBLISHED: both submit simultaneously
    REVIEWS_PUBLISHED --> [*]
    AVAILABILITY_LOST --> [*]
    AUTH_FAILED --> [*]
    CANCELLED --> [*]
    CANCELLED_PAYMENT --> [*]
```

The FSM bakes in three cross-system contracts: the calendar lock is the *only* point of strict ordering (everything downstream is a Saga step with explicit compensation), the payment is *authorised* not *captured* until 24h after check-in (the escrow), and reviews live in a `REVIEWS_LOCKED` quarantine state until both parties commit or the 14-day timer fires — neither side sees the other's content while writing. *[Source: systemdesign.io hotel-reservation, Airbnb engineering]*

**Idempotency on the booking commit (Orpheus pattern).** Every `POST /booking` carries a client-generated idempotency key; the booking service stores `(idempotency_key → booking_id)` in a sharded transactional store before the Saga starts. A retry with the same key returns the cached prior outcome instead of re-running the Saga, so a flaky network can't double-charge. The same pattern wraps every downstream RPC (Stripe auth, calendar lock) using a *deterministic* sub-key derived from `booking_id` + step name, so replay of any individual step is safe even mid-Saga. Master-DB-only writes for idempotency state — replica lag here causes duplicate payments. *[Source: Airbnb engineering — Orpheus]*

**Pricing as a function of host policy + ML suggestion.** Smart Pricing is a regression model trained on `(booking | no-booking | cancellation)` outcomes against `(date, location, demand index, comparable listings)`. The model emits a *suggested* price per night; hosts can accept the suggestion as a default or override per-night via the calendar UI. A calendar row therefore carries `(status, price_override?, smart_price_at_write_time)` — the second column is the audit trail for "did this listing trend up because of Smart Pricing or host action?" Service fees (~14% guest, ~3% host) are added at checkout, never written into the listing or calendar.

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: How do you prevent double-booking when two guests hit "Book" within milliseconds?**
Calendar Service uses `SELECT FOR UPDATE` per `(listing, date)` row inside a transaction; second txn blocks then sees `status=booked` and aborts with `AVAILABILITY_LOST`. *If pushed:* under heavy contention, optimistic concurrency with a `version` column avoids lock waits — retry on conflict; PK on `(listing_id, date)` is the source of truth.

**Q: A guest cancels 2 hours before check-in. What happens to the host?**
Cancellation policy on the listing dictates payout — strict policies still pay the host a portion. Calendar dates are released back to available, but Airbnb may suppress them from search if it's last-minute. *If pushed:* offer the host a backup-guest feature; for extenuating circumstances (hurricane), batch flow refunds guest in full and Airbnb absorbs host comp.

**Q: How does search stay fresh when a calendar update happens?**
CDC pipeline (Debezium → Kafka) ships Postgres calendar changes to ES with sub-minute lag. Search is optimistic; the booking endpoint is the source of truth — a stale ES hit just produces one extra `AVAILABILITY_LOST` response. *If pushed:* for hot listings, write-through invalidate the ES doc on booking commit; cache popular `(city, date-range)` queries 5min.

**Q: What if Stripe's payment auth expires before check-in (>7 days out)?**
Re-auth job runs ~5 days after the original auth, refreshing the hold. If re-auth fails (card cancelled, insufficient funds), guest gets notified to update payment or the booking cancels. *If pushed:* alternative is capture-early-refund-on-cancel — simpler but worse for guests; Airbnb trades complexity for guest UX.

**Q: How do you stop hosts and guests from gaming the dual-review system?**
Reviews are mutually hidden until both submit or 14d pass; neither side sees the other's content while writing. Edit windows are short. *If pushed:* ML-detect retaliation patterns (host floods that guest's profile after a bad review); ban brigading; weight reviews by reviewer reputation.

**Q: 50k searches and 200 booking attempts on one Super Bowl listing in an hour — bottleneck?**
The single calendar row becomes the hot spot — every booking contends for the same lock. Queue FIFO behind the row, return remaining-slots-N hint to clients. *If pushed:* short-circuit at the API layer once first booking commits (broadcast invalidation); for true mega-events, switch to a lottery flow rather than first-come-first-served.

**Q: A user mashes the Book button five times before the spinner clears. How do you guarantee one booking?**
Client generates one idempotency key per checkout session and includes it on every retry. Booking service writes `(idem_key → booking_id)` before the Saga starts and returns the cached `booking_id` on duplicate keys. The Saga's downstream calls (Stripe auth, calendar lock) carry their own deterministic sub-keys derived from `booking_id` + step name, so even mid-Saga retries don't double-execute. *If pushed:* idempotency table is master-only — replica lag here is what causes duplicate payments; Airbnb's Orpheus framework hit five-nines consistency on this exact pattern. *[Source: Airbnb engineering — Orpheus]*

**Q: How does Airbnb differ from a hotel system at the data-model level?**
Three twists: (1) `calendar.price_override` is per-night because hosts dynamically price weekends/events; hotels rate-card per room class. (2) Payment is `authorised`-not-captured until 24h post-check-in (escrow); hotels capture on arrival. (3) Reviews are dual-blind released — neither side sees the other's content until both submit or 14d pass; hotels are typically one-way (guest reviews property only). *If pushed:* the data model also carries host-set cancellation policies (Strict / Moderate / Flexible) per listing, which a hotel system doesn't model — payout on cancellation depends on policy and pre-arrival timing.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Hot listing on event date** (Super Bowl: 200 booking attempts on one calendar row) — every booker contends for the same `SELECT FOR UPDATE`, serialising into a queue. Queue them FIFO with a remaining-slots hint to clients so the UI can short-circuit obvious losers; for true mega-events, switch the listing to a lottery flow rather than first-come-first-served.
- **Search latency / freshness** — search must stay sub-second across 100M searches/day, but the calendar updates constantly. CDC ships calendar mutations into the search index with sub-minute lag; popular `(city, date_range)` queries cache for 5min in front of ES so repeat traffic on hot weekends doesn't hit the cluster.
- **Payment hold expiry** (Stripe auth expires after 7d, check-in might be 30d out) — a single auth at booking won't cover a long lead time. Re-auth job runs ~5d after the original auth, refreshing the hold; if re-auth fails (cancelled card), notify the guest to update payment and offer the booking back to the host.
- **Cancellation refund storm** (event cancelled, 10k bookings refund at once) — running 10k synchronous refunds via Stripe is rate-limited and slow. Batch them through an async refund queue with retries and per-payment-method quotas; users see "refund in progress" not "refunded" until the gateway acknowledges.
- **Calendar consistency under failure** (Saga compensation: payment fails after dates locked) — without compensation, the calendar leaks unrecoverable holds. Bookings carry an idempotency key; the Saga's compensating action releases the calendar rows and is itself idempotent, so retry-on-crash converges to a clean state.
- **Trust-and-safety adversarial review behaviour** (host or guest gaming the dual-review window) — pure dual-blind release stops the simplest retaliation, but coordinated manipulation (host floods bad-guest's profile after release, brigading rings) still happens. ML detects retaliation patterns post-release; reviews are weighted by reviewer reputation and verified-stay status; appeal flow lets victims surface gaming.
- **Smart Pricing audit confusion** (host claims "I never set $400/night, the platform did") — calendar row stores `price_override` plus a snapshot of the Smart Pricing suggestion at write time, so any disputed night reconstructs whether the host accepted a suggestion or made an explicit override. Service fees are computed at checkout, never persisted on the listing.

#### Failure Modes

| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Calendar lock | `SELECT FOR UPDATE` deadlock under contention | Postgres deadlock log + retry counter | Optimistic concurrency on `version` column; serialise per-listing booking via in-process queue |
| Saga | Payment auth fails after dates locked | Saga compensation queue depth | Idempotent compensating action releases calendar rows; retry on crash converges |
| Search index | CDC stream stalled (Debezium dies) | Kafka consumer lag > 60 s | Pager; failover Debezium task; booking validates calendar at commit so stale search hit is harmless |
| Payment hold | Stripe auth expires before 30-day check-in | Re-auth job failure rate | ~5-day re-auth job; on failure notify guest + offer back to host |
| Reviews | Retaliation gaming after dual-blind release | ML anomaly detection on per-host review patterns | Reviewer-reputation weighting; appeal flow; brigade detection |
| Listings DB | Master failover during write storm | Replica lag + write 5xx rate | Multi-AZ Postgres with synchronous replica; idempotency key shields duplicate POSTs |
| Cancellation | Refund storm hits Stripe rate limit | Refund queue depth + 429s from PSP | Async refund queue with per-payment-method quotas; users see "in progress" |
| Cross-region | Region outage during peak weekend | Health probes from edge | Active-active multi-region; sharded calendar can fail over by listing-shard |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs

- `booking_saga_success_rate` — target > 99.5% per region; drops trigger Saga-step error breakdown.
- `calendar_lock_wait_p99_ms` — target < 200 ms; spikes mean hot-listing contention.
- `cdc_lag_seconds` (Debezium → ES) — target < 60 s; > 5 min pages.
- `availability_lost_rate` — booking attempts hitting `AVAILABILITY_LOST` after search; > 2% suggests stale-index drift.
- `payment_reauth_failure_rate` — target < 1%; long-lead bookings at risk.
- `review_publish_lag` — time from second-review-submitted to publication; should be near-zero.
- SLO: 99.99% calendar consistency (no double-booking) measured via daily reconciliation against payment ledger.

#### Multi-Region & DR

- **Replication mode:** active-active per-region with calendar sharded by `listing_id`; each shard has a primary region (sync replica + async cross-region replicas). Search index replicated per region; CDC fans out from each shard's primary.
- **RTO:** ~2 min for stateless services (Booking Orchestrator, Search); ~10 min for a calendar-shard failover (promote sync replica, redirect writes).
- **RPO:** zero on calendar (sync replica + idempotency); ~30 s on search index; ~5 min on analytics replicas.
- **Failover cadence:** monthly per-shard chaos test; quarterly full-region DR.
- **Cross-region cost:** modest — calendar/listing data is per-region for the listing's home; cross-region traffic is search query routing for travellers and CDC replication.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns

- *Treating availability as eventually consistent at the booking commit.* Search can be stale, but the booking endpoint must `SELECT FOR UPDATE` against the source of truth — anything else allows double-booking.
- *Capturing payment at booking instead of authorising.* Breaks the escrow contract that protects guests; the gateway's 7-day auth window plus re-auth is the correct mechanism.
- *Releasing reviews as soon as one party submits.* Hands a retaliation cudgel to whoever submits second; dual-blind release with a 14-day deadline is the structural fix.
- *Synchronous notification fan-out in the Saga path.* Slow notify provider stalls the booking commit; notify is async off the persist event.
- *Skipping idempotency keys on `POST /booking`.* Mash-the-button retries double-charge; key per checkout session is the canonical fix (Airbnb's Orpheus pattern).
- *Replicating calendar across regions for low-latency reads.* Cross-region async replication on a row that needs `SELECT FOR UPDATE` lets two regions grant the same night; pin each listing to one home region.

#### Talking Points for the Interview

- **Calendar row-per-night with pessimistic lock** is the only place strict ordering exists; everything else is Saga + compensation. This is the core insight.
- **Escrow via auth-not-capture** with re-auth at ~5 days — protects guests, gives the platform leverage on disputes; Stripe's 7-day window is the cost.
- **Dual-blind reviews** — the structural fix to retaliation; mention the 14-day deadline and the publish-when-both-submit semantics.
- **CDC from Postgres to ES** for search freshness, with the booking commit as source of truth — `AVAILABILITY_LOST` on stale search hits is the canonical contract.
- **Idempotency keys (Orpheus)** on `POST /booking` and on every Saga step — master-only writes for the idempotency table because replica lag here is what causes duplicate payments.
- **Strong consistency on the calendar, eventual everywhere else** — the only hill worth dying on for correctness.

### 31. Design Netflix (Video Streaming)
#### Problem
Stream long-form video on demand to hundreds of millions of users globally with adaptive bitrate, low buffering, and personalized recommendations.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a library where instead of checking out the whole book and carrying it home, you read it one page at a time from a photocopied page that's already been placed at the library branch closest to your house — before you ever asked for it. Netflix is that library, operating at planetary scale. The hard problem is not storing movies; it's making sure the right pages are already at the branch near you before Friday night.

**The single-request walkthrough:** you hit Play on Stranger Things Season 5. Your Netflix app sends `POST /play` to Netflix's cloud servers (running on AWS). The server authenticates you, checks your region's licensing rights, and asks the **manifest service** for a list of available quality levels and their CDN URLs. It also calls the **DRM license server** (Digital Rights Management — a system that encrypts video chunks so they can only be decoded on an authorized device) to issue a short-lived decryption token tied to your device. Your app receives a **HLS manifest** (HLS = HTTP Live Streaming, a standard that describes a video as a collection of 4-second segments at multiple quality levels, stored in a `.m3u8` text file). The app picks a starting quality based on your current network speed, fetches the first segment from the CDN URL in the manifest, and starts playing. Every ~30 seconds it sends a heartbeat to Netflix with your current position — this is how "Continue Watching" knows where you stopped. The app monitors your download speed for each segment: if it takes 0.3 seconds to download a 4-second segment, your buffer is growing — you can upgrade quality. If it takes 3 seconds, downgrade immediately.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Open Connect Appliances (OCA)** — Netflix's own physical servers, installed for free inside ISP data centers (internet service providers like Comcast, BT, Telstra). Each appliance is a server with hundreds of terabytes of SSD storage holding the most popular Netflix content for that ISP's customers. When a Comcast subscriber in Chicago plays a Netflix show, the video bytes come from a Comcast data center in Chicago — never from AWS. This is the entire economic story: Netflix carries ~15% of global internet traffic; paying Amazon or Akamai CDN egress fees for that volume would cost billions. Free ISP appliances eliminate that cost.
- **Pre-positioning** — between 2 and 6 AM local time, Netflix's popularity-prediction model calculates which shows each appliance should hold and pushes the relevant segments to it overnight. When Friday night arrives and millions of subscribers start Stranger Things simultaneously, those bytes are already at their nearest ISP — zero traffic goes to AWS. This is a cache-warming strategy: predict what will be popular, stage it before demand spikes, serve from the edge.
- **ABR (Adaptive Bitrate Streaming)** — the video is encoded into multiple quality levels (360p, 480p, 720p, 1080p, 4K HDR) and each level is sliced into 4-second chunks. The player decides which quality chunk to fetch next based on how fast its buffer is filling. Buffer filling fast (your connection is strong) → fetch the next chunk at higher quality. Buffer draining (connection degraded) → immediately switch to lower quality for the next chunk. This is why Netflix rarely buffers — it constantly adjusts to your current network conditions.
- **Content-aware encoding** — instead of encoding every movie at the same set of bitrates, Netflix analyzes each title's complexity (fast action scenes need more bits than talking heads; animation has flat colors that compress very efficiently) and chooses a custom encoding ladder per title. A simple animation at 1080p might look perfect at 2Mbps; an action movie needs 6Mbps for the same quality. This saves ~20% total bandwidth at the same perceived quality.
- **Control/data plane split** — the "control plane" (auth, manifest, DRM token) runs on AWS and is tiny — a few hundred bytes per play session. The "data plane" (actual video bytes) runs entirely through OCAs inside ISPs. These two paths never mix: making AWS serve video bytes would destroy the economics and routing.

**The thing that makes it hard — the surprise hit:** a show that nobody predicted becomes viral on Saturday morning. The popularity model did not flag it as important, so the appliances near you do not have it pre-positioned. Every subscriber who clicks Play causes their local OCA to fall back to fetching from a peer OCA or, worst case, from origin (AWS S3). At Netflix's scale this means hundreds of gigabits per second of surprise traffic flowing through paid CDN or AWS egress — costing hundreds of thousands of dollars in hours. Netflix monitors **origin fill rate per OCA** (what fraction of chunks this appliance had to fetch from origin rather than serving locally) as a paged metric. A sudden spike means the model missed something; engineers can push an emergency pre-position job to get the content to the appliances before the spike gets worse.

**Why the standard solution works:** the OCA program's economics make everything else possible. Without it, Netflix would need to contract with commercial CDN providers (Akamai, Cloudflare) who charge per byte of egress. At 32 terabits per second of peak traffic, that bill would be astronomical. The ISP partnership is mutually beneficial: ISPs reduce the load on their upstream transit links (expensive) by hosting Netflix locally; Netflix eliminates egress costs. The engineering choice to build and maintain physical hardware inside thousands of ISPs worldwide is justified by the savings.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Origin: AWS S3 holding the master encoded catalog (~1.5PB for 20,000 titles × ~75GB per title).
- OCAs: Linux servers running open-source nginx as a cache, controlled by Netflix's centralized pre-positioning orchestration system that knows each appliance's disk inventory and pushes diffs nightly.
- Playback hot path:
  ```
  POST /play → auth + entitlement check
  manifest_service.get(title_id, user_region) → HLS manifest with OCA URLs
  drm.issue_token(device_id, content_key_id, geo=user_region)
  return {manifest_url, drm_token}
  // client then:
  GET {oca_url}/stranger_things_s5_ep1_1080p_chunk_001.ts
  // OCA: cache hit → serve from SSD; cache miss → fetch from peer OCA or origin, cache, serve
  ```
- ABR buffer-based heuristic: switch up when buffer > 20 seconds ahead; switch down immediately when buffer < 5 seconds; never oscillate more than 2 quality levels in 60 seconds.
#### Clarifying Questions
- DRM required?
- Live in scope? (assume VOD only)
- Offline downloads?
- Multi-device profiles?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** browse catalog, play video, pause/resume, recommendations, search, profiles
- **NFR:** sub-second start, no rebuffering, global low latency, durable catalog

#### Scale Estimate
- **Subscribers / streaming hours:** ~280M global subscribers (Netflix 2024 public, rounded ~250M for older estimates); ~1B hours/week (public number) → 1B / 7 ≈ ~143M hours/day; assume 250M and double-counting downward → ~200M hours/day used here
- **Catalog size:** ~17k–20k titles (public estimates); each in ~10 ABR rungs × 4 codecs × ~5 audio = ~200 files per title → multi-PB (see footprint below)
- **Peak bandwidth share:** Netflix ~15% of global internet traffic per Sandvine reports; Sandvine global peak ~hundreds of Tbps → tens of Tbps for Netflix
- **Per-title encoded size:** 2 h × 60 min × ~10 MB/min at 1080p H.264 ≈ ~6 GB (rounded ~5 GB) per rung; 10 rungs × 4 codecs ≈ 40 video files; 1080p baseline (~5 GB) × half rungs lower bitrate avg → full ladder ≈ ~50–100 GB; pick midpoint ~75 GB
- **Catalog footprint:** 20k titles × 75 GB avg encoded ladder = 1.5M GB ≈ ~1.5 PB master library on origin object store
- **Open Connect cache footprint:** popular ~10% of catalog (~2k titles) × ~20 GB hot rungs only (drop AV1+top 4K) = 40 TB per appliance; large Storage Appliances ship with 100s of TB SSD, smaller Flash/PoP boxes ~20 TB
- **Per-stream bitrate:** 720p ≈ 3 Mbps, 1080p ≈ 6 Mbps, 4K HDR ≈ 15 Mbps; viewing mix weighted ~50% mobile/SD + ~40% 1080p + ~10% 4K → weighted avg ≈ ~4 Mbps
- **Aggregate bandwidth:** 200M hours/day / 24 h ≈ ~8M concurrent avg (peaks higher; Stranger Things drops hit 80M+ concurrent); 8M × 4 Mbps = ~32 Tbps; >95% absorbed by Open Connect inside ISP → AWS-paid transit egress sub-linear
- **Playback heartbeat volume:** 8M concurrent × 1 heartbeat / 30 s × ~200 B = 8M × 200 / 30 ≈ ~53 MB/s ≈ ~50 MB/s into Cassandra; 50 MB/s × 86,400 s ≈ ~4.3 TB/day raw

#### API Contract
```
GET  /catalog?row=trending|continue|recommended  → titles
GET  /title/{id}      → metadata + manifest URLs
POST /play            → { manifest, license_token (DRM) }
POST /heartbeat       body: { title_id, position_sec, profile_id }
```

#### High-Level Design
```
                     ┌───────────────────────────────┐
                     │   Control Plane (cloud)       │
                     │  - User / billing             │
                     │  - Catalog metadata           │
                     │  - Recommendations            │
                     │  - Playback session start     │
                     └──────────────┬────────────────┘
                                    │ start session →
                                    │ returns manifest + token + CDN URLs
                                    ↓
   Client ←─── streaming chunks ─── ┌───────────────────────┐
                                    │  Open Connect (CDN)   │
                                    │  - Custom appliances  │
                                    │    co-located in ISPs │
                                    │  - Pre-positioned     │
                                    │    popular content    │
                                    │    (push during off-  │
                                    │    peak)              │
                                    └───────────────────────┘
```

**How to read the diagram:** ingestion and playback are separate. Content is prepared offline into many encodings and chunks, then manifests and CDNs make playback mostly a data-delivery problem rather than an on-demand transcoding problem.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** viewers care about startup time and buffering, not about how elegant the encoding pipeline is. Doing the heavy media work in advance leaves the runtime path focused on choosing and delivering the right chunks quickly.

**What this layout buys you:** predictable playback and efficient global distribution. The tradeoff is large storage overhead for many variants and a substantial offline media-processing pipeline.

#### Data Model
- **titles** (wide-column store): `(title_id, title, year, genres, cast, ratings, ...)`
- **encodings** (object store + index): `(title_id, resolution, codec, bitrate, lang, drm_key) → manifest_url`
- **playback_history** (wide-column store): `(profile_id, title_id, position_sec, completed, ts)`
- **recommendations** (precomputed per user, in fast in-memory cache): `profile_id → [title_id, score]`

#### Detailed Design
**Open Connect CDN (the secret sauce):**
- Netflix-owned cache appliances installed inside ISPs (free for the ISP)
- Pre-positioned: predict popular content per region; push to edges during off-peak (3am)
- Reduces transit cost massively; most streams served from same ISP

**Encoding pipeline (offline).** Each title gets encoded into a per-title ABR ladder (typically 8–12 rungs from 240p to 4K HDR), four codecs (H.264 for compatibility, HEVC, AV1, VP9 for efficiency), audio per language (5.1 surround on supported titles), and subtitle tracks per language. The ladder is content-aware — animation has flat colour fields and benefits from a lower-bitrate top rung than live action with grain and motion. Encoding runs as a fleet of GPU jobs against the master file uploaded once; output is millions of small chunked files written to the origin object store, then later pre-positioned to OCAs.

**ABR (adaptive bitrate).** Client measures throughput per chunk and picks the next chunk's bitrate from the manifest accordingly. Modern players use buffer-based ABR (BBA): switch up only when the buffer is healthy (>20s ahead), switch down aggressively when it dips, because oscillation looks worse than a stable lower quality. Chunks are 4–10s — short enough to react to network changes, long enough to amortise the HTTP overhead. Per-chunk decisions (rather than per-session) is what handles wifi contention and cellular handoff smoothly.

**Recommendations.** Offline batch jobs train matrix factorisation and deep learning models on viewing history (watch %, completed, abandoned), producing per-user scores against the catalog. Output is precomputed rows ("Trending", "Because You Watched X", "New Releases", "Top 10 in Country") served from an in-memory cache keyed by `profile_id`. Even the artwork shown for each title is personalised — same title, different thumbnail per user, A/B tested heavily because thumbnail click-through is a measurable lift.

**Offline downloads.** Mobile clients can download a title for offline playback by fetching the encoded chunks from the same OCAs they'd stream from, plus a *long-lived* DRM license bound to the device that the player stores alongside the chunks. The license has a separate expiry (typically 30 days from download or 48h after first play, whichever comes first) and is non-renewable while offline — once it expires, the chunks are unplayable until the device reconnects and re-licenses. Region rights are baked into the license at issue time, so a downloaded title plays anywhere geographically until expiry, but a region-rights revocation invalidates re-licensing. Encoded ladder for downloads is restricted to the lower rungs (480p / 720p) to keep file sizes manageable; 4K downloads are usually disabled.

**DRM.** Widevine (Chrome/Android), FairPlay (Apple), PlayReady (Windows/Xbox) — three ecosystems, one encryption scheme (CENC) at the chunk level. License server issues a short-lived token bound to the device's DRM provisioning; without the token, the encrypted chunks are useless. Tokens are cacheable per-device for the session, so a viral launch doesn't melt the license server. Geo enforcement happens at token issuance — manifest endpoint serves a country-scoped manifest, license server gates on user's verified geo, so a leaked manifest still won't decrypt outside the licensed region.

**Viewing-session pipeline (manifest → chunk).**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client / TV / Mobile] -->|POST /play| API[Playback API<br/>control plane / AWS]
    API -->|auth + entitlement| Geo[Geo + Catalog Service]
    Geo -->|country-scoped catalog| API
    API -->|issue manifest| Manifest[Manifest Service]
    Manifest -->|nearest-OCA URLs<br/>+ ABR rungs| Client
    API -->|short-lived token| DRM[License Server<br/>Widevine / FairPlay / PlayReady]
    DRM -->|CENC content key| Client
    Client -->|GET /chunk?br=1080p| OCA1[Open Connect<br/>inside ISP]
    OCA1 -->|miss: <5%| Peer[Peer OCA / IX fill]
    Peer -->|miss| Origin[(S3 Origin<br/>master encodes)]
    OCA1 -->|chunk bytes| Client
    Client -->|heartbeat /30s| Telemetry[Playback Telemetry<br/>Kafka / Mantis]
    Telemetry -->|resume position| HistoryDB[(Cassandra<br/>playback_history)]
    Telemetry -->|QoE signals| ABRTuner[ABR Heuristics]
    Prepos[Pre-positioning Job<br/>02:00-06:00 local] -->|push predicted-popular| OCA1
    Prepos -->|popularity model| Recs[Recommendations]
    Recs -.->|per-region forecast| Prepos
```

The arrows show why control- and data-plane separation is the entire economic story: every byte the client sees as video flows from an OCA inside its ISP, never AWS. The control plane handles auth, manifest issuance, and the DRM token — small, infrequent, AWS-egress-acceptable. Telemetry is the only return path back into the control plane, and it's tiny (heartbeats every 30s, ~200 B). *[Source: Netflix Open Connect, Netflix tech blog]*

**Open Connect appliance hierarchy and fill sources.** OCAs ship in two general classes — large *Storage Appliances* (100s of TB SSD, hold ~all of the popular catalogue, deployed at major ISP cores) and smaller *Global* / *Flash* appliances (tens of TB, edge PoPs). Fill source priority for a chunk miss is: peer OCA on the same network → IXP fill from a peer OCA at an internet exchange → OCA at a Netflix backbone PoP → S3 origin (last resort). Origin-fill rate per OCA is a paged metric — a healthy OCA serves >95% from local cache and pulls fills from its peer set, never origin during peak. Settlement-free interconnect (free peering between Netflix and the ISP) is the deal that makes the program work for both sides; the ISP saves transit cost on its outbound peering, Netflix saves CDN egress. *[Source: openconnect.netflix.com]*

**Pre-positioning timing and popularity forecast.** Fill jobs run between 02:00-06:00 local time per appliance — the network's quiet window. A regional popularity model (per-country, per-region, per-show new-season-drop calendar) generates a ranked list of chunks each OCA should hold; the job pushes deltas, not full catalogue, so a single appliance only fetches the new-since-yesterday content overnight. Surprise hits trending mid-day can live-fill from origin or peer, but live-fill rate is alerted because uncontrolled it blows the egress budget. New-season global drops at Friday 00:00 UTC are pre-positioned hours earlier per region. *[Source: Netflix Open Connect distribution]*

**Per-title encode ladder optimisation.** A flat 10-rung ABR ladder (240p → 4K) wastes bits on simple content (animation, news) where the top rung looks identical at half the bitrate, and starves complex content (action, sports) where even the top rung shows compression artefacts. Per-title encoding analyses each title's complexity (motion vectors, scene cuts, grain) and picks a *content-aware* ladder — the same perceptual quality target hits different bitrates per title. Reported savings are ~20% bytes at the same VMAF (perceptual quality score) over the legacy fixed ladder. The cost is encoder fleet CPU — encoding a title now means analysis pass + encode pass × N rungs × M codecs (H.264, HEVC, AV1, VP9). *[Source: Netflix tech blog — per-title encoding]*

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: An Open Connect appliance inside a major ISP fails. What happens?**
Client manifest lists multiple edges; player retries the next-closest appliance, possibly one in a peer ISP or Netflix's IX presence. Brief quality drop while ABR adjusts. *If pushed:* if all nearby OCAs fail, fall back to AWS-hosted CloudFront — transit cost spikes but service stays up. Control-plane health checks reroute manifests within seconds.

**Q: How do you predict which content to pre-position per region?**
Recommendation system already produces per-region popularity forecasts; combine with release calendar (new season ships globally Friday 00:00 UTC). Push to OCAs during 2-6am local. *If pushed:* for surprise hits trending mid-day, live-fill from origin to OCAs works but is expensive — alerting on origin-fill rate guards against runaway transit cost.

**Q: A new title launches and 50M people press play in the first hour. Bottleneck?**
If pre-positioning ran cleanly, each OCA serves its local ISP independently — no global hot spot. Real risk is OCAs that missed the prefetch falling back to origin/IX fill, blowing transit budget. License server is a secondary bottleneck (every play needs a DRM token). *If pushed:* alert on origin-fill rate per OCA at launch; license servers are stateless and horizontally scaled, tokens cacheable per-device for the session.

**Q: Why ABR per chunk instead of picking quality on session start?**
Network conditions change mid-stream (wifi contention, cellular handoff). Picking once means buffering or wasted bandwidth. *If pushed:* clients use buffer-based ABR (BBA) — switch up only when buffer is healthy, switch down aggressively when it dips; throughput-based ABR oscillates.

**Q: How do you handle live events (sports) given this is VOD-architected?**
Different pipeline: low-latency HLS/DASH (2-5s glass-to-glass) with origin packagers feeding edges in real-time, no pre-positioning possible. *If pushed:* live can't use Open Connect's cache-warming model; relies on conventional CDN topology (multi-tier edges, anycast); player tolerates higher rebuffer rate during peaks.

**Q: How does region-locked content (rights expire in UK at midnight) get enforced?**
Catalog metadata service serves a per-country view; player requests a manifest scoped to user's geo (verified server-side, not client-trusted). At expiry, manifest endpoint returns 403 for that title in UK. *If pushed:* VPN evasion mitigated via IP reputation lists; the license server gates DRM token issuance based on geo, so a leaked manifest still won't decrypt.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Hit show launch** (Stranger Things S5: 50M plays in the first hour) — if pre-positioning ran cleanly, each OCA serves its local ISP independently and there's no hot spot. The actual risk is OCAs that *missed* the prefetch falling back to origin/IX fill, which blows transit budget. Push to all OCAs in advance; multi-CDN (CloudFront, Akamai) as paid backup for OCAs that fail.
- **ISP congestion** (last-mile saturated) — Open Connect physically inside the ISP avoids the upstream transit link entirely; that's the mitigation. Without it, peak-hour video would compete with everything else over the ISP's transit and quality would collapse.
- **Buffering on slow networks** — clients drop to lower ABR rungs aggressively when buffer dips, and Netflix tunes per-network heuristics (cellular vs wifi vs fiber) so each environment picks the right starting bitrate. The cost is a brief quality dip, not a stall.
- **Region rights changes** (UK rights expire at midnight) — manifest endpoint must respect the new geo rules immediately. Catalog metadata service refreshes per-region views fast; the license server gates DRM token issuance on geo, so even cached manifests stop being playable.
- **Chaos / regional AWS outage** — control plane is in AWS, so a regional outage could kill new sessions even though Open Connect keeps streaming. Chaos Monkey forces engineers to test failover continuously; multiple regions stand active-active so a regional loss is a degraded mode, not an outage.
- **Origin-fill rate alarm** (an OCA falls back to S3 origin for a non-trivial share of chunks) — every origin fill pays AWS egress and breaks the no-transit economics. Healthy OCAs serve >95% from local cache and pull misses from peer/IXP fills; sustained origin-fill on one appliance is a paged alert pointing at either a missed prefetch or a popularity-model regression.
- **Per-title encode CPU** (encoder fleet runs analysis-pass + encode-pass × ~10 rungs × 4 codecs per title) — one title can cost thousands of GPU/CPU hours. Mitigation is amortisation: encode once, serve forever, and the ~20% bandwidth savings vs a fixed ladder pays back in days at Netflix's 32 Tbps peak.
- **Mantis / playback telemetry hot path** (8M concurrent × 1 heartbeat/30s = ~270k events/s) — the playback-quality feedback loop is what tunes ABR heuristics in production. Backpressure from Mantis must never block the player; heartbeats are fire-and-forget UDP-style with a small in-memory buffer per client and best-effort delivery.

#### Failure Modes

| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open Connect | OCA dies inside major ISP | Per-OCA health probe + chunk-error rate from clients | Manifest lists multiple edges; player retries next-closest; cloud CDN (CloudFront) backstop |
| Origin fill | OCA falls back to S3 origin during peak | `origin_fill_rate` alarm | Pre-position better; peer/IXP fill priority; alert on sustained fill > 5% |
| Pre-position | Popularity model misses surprise hit | Live-fill rate spike | Live-fill from origin until next overnight push; capacity headroom in egress budget |
| License server | Widevine/FairPlay 5xx during launch | License-issue error rate | Per-device token caching for the session; horizontal scale; multi-region license servers |
| Control plane | AWS regional outage | Health-probe failures across services | Active-active multi-region; existing OCAs keep streaming for already-started sessions |
| ABR loop | TWCC misreads bandwidth (cellular handoff) | Client rebuffer rate | Per-network heuristics; conservative initial bitrate; aggressive switch-down |
| DRM enforcement | Region rights expire mid-stream | Manifest endpoint returns 403 | License server gates token issuance on geo; cached manifests stop being playable |
| Encoding pipeline | Per-title encoder fleet falls behind on launch slate | Encode backlog by `release_at` | Priority queue by ship date; codec/rung subset for delayed titles |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs

- `play_start_p99_ms` — target < 1,000 ms (manifest + first chunk).
- `rebuffer_ratio` — target < 0.5% of playback time; per-network breakdown for tuning.
- `origin_fill_rate_per_oca` — target < 5% during peak; > 10% pages.
- `oca_cache_hit_rate` — target > 95% inside ISP.
- `license_issue_p99_ms` — target < 200 ms.
- `pre_position_completion` — target 99%+ of predicted-popular bytes resident on each OCA by the regional morning rush.
- SLO: 99.95% playback start success globally; per-title VMAF (perceptual quality) target maintained across the ABR ladder.

#### Multi-Region & DR

- **Replication mode:** control plane active-active across multiple AWS regions; data plane (OCAs) is global by design — every appliance is independently servable. Catalog metadata + recommendations are eventually consistent across regions.
- **RTO:** ~30 s for control-plane regional failover (DNS / anycast); zero for already-streaming sessions (OCA continues serving).
- **RPO:** ~minutes for catalog metadata; zero for already-streamed bytes (the OCA cache is the canonical edge).
- **Failover cadence:** Chaos Monkey/Gorilla/Kong continuously kill instances/zones/regions in production; explicit DR exercises layer onto baseline chaos.
- **Cross-region cost:** dominated by origin → OCA pre-position bandwidth (rare per chunk, paid overnight); control-plane cross-region traffic is small.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns

- *Treating Netflix as "just a CDN problem".* The control/data plane split and Open Connect's ISP-co-located model are the actual economic story; without them the bytes don't pencil.
- *Letting bytes flow through the control plane.* Manifest+token+CDN-URLs from control plane; chunks always from OCA — coupling them ruins both economics and scalability.
- *Live-filling from origin as the default.* Live-fill is the bug; pre-position is the feature. Origin-fill rate must be a paged alert.
- *One ABR ladder for all titles.* Per-title content-aware encoding saves ~20% bytes at the same VMAF — material at Netflix scale.
- *Picking ABR rung at session start.* Network changes mid-stream (wifi contention, cellular handoff); per-chunk decisions are non-negotiable.
- *Synchronous DRM license issue per chunk.* Per-session token cache is essential; otherwise a viral launch melts the license server.

#### Talking Points for the Interview

- **Open Connect is the entire economic story.** Netflix is ~15% of internet traffic — paying CDN egress for that is impossible. Lead with this.
- **Control/data plane separation** — lead with it, then explain the consequences (license server, manifest service, telemetry are all small-traffic, and bytes never traverse the cloud).
- **Pre-position over live-fill** — prediction-driven cache warming during ISP off-peak; live-fill is alerted because it breaks the no-transit economics.
- **Per-title ABR ladder** with content-aware encoding — animation gets a different ladder than action; ~20% bandwidth savings at the same perceived quality.
- **Per-chunk ABR via TWCC** with buffer-based heuristics — switch up only when buffer is healthy, switch down aggressively; don't oscillate.
- **DRM gates geo at token issuance** — cached manifests can't decrypt outside licensed regions, so leaks don't matter.
- **Chaos engineering as production hygiene** — Chaos Monkey/Gorilla/Kong continuously kill things so failover is exercised constantly, not just at game-day.

### 32. Design Slack (Workplace Messaging)
#### Problem
Team messaging with channels, threads, DMs, search, presence, integrations — within isolated workspaces.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a building with many floors, where each floor is a company (a "workspace"). Within each floor there are rooms (channels). The PA system on each floor lets anyone in that room hear new messages instantly. But there's also a filing cabinet for every room — old messages go in chronologically, and there's a searchable index card system so you can find "that pricing email from March." The building manager insists each floor's records are stored separately and no one on floor 3 can accidentally reach into floor 7's filing cabinet.

**The single-request walkthrough:** a user on the Salesforce workspace types a message in #general and hits Send. Their client sends the message over a WebSocket — a persistent two-way connection (unlike a normal web request, it stays open so the server can push messages back instantly) — to the Message Server (MS Server) for the Salesforce workspace. The MS Server publishes the message once to a per-channel topic on the internal pub/sub bus. Every other MS Server that has at least one connected Salesforce user subscribed to #general receives the message from the bus and pushes it down those users' open WebSocket connections. Simultaneously, the Persist Service writes the message to a wide-column database (a type of database that stores rows keyed by channel and sorted by time — Cassandra is the canonical choice). The Search Indexer reads from that same persist stream and adds the message to Salesforce's dedicated Elasticsearch index. Total time from Send to delivery: under 200ms in the same region.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **MS Server (Message Server)** — a pool of long-running servers, each holding thousands of open WebSocket connections for users in one workspace. "Sticky" routing means reconnects land on the same pool so presence and channel lists stay warm in memory.
- **Per-channel pub/sub topics** — the bus that lets MS Servers talk to each other without a direct connection mesh. One MS Server publishes once per message; every other MS Server with a subscriber on that channel consumes once and pushes locally. This keeps fan-out cost proportional to how many MS Servers have subscribers, not how many users are in the channel — critical when a 100k-seat workspace's #general has 80k members who are mostly idle.
- **Persist Service + wide-column store** — durably writes every message to Cassandra, partitioned by channel_id and sorted by message timestamp. This is the source of truth, not the WebSocket path. If the bus crashes, messages are not lost.
- **Search indexer + per-workspace Elasticsearch index** — asynchronously reads the persist stream and indexes new messages within a few seconds. Each workspace gets its own index so a Salesforce query cannot accidentally return Shopify messages, and Elasticsearch's free-plan retention pruning can be applied per workspace without touching others.
- **Slack Connect bridge** — handles shared channels between two different companies. Rather than copying the message log into both workspaces (which caused ordering bugs), there is one "home shard" for the channel and all reads/writes from the visiting company route to it. The extra network hop on writes from the visiting side is the accepted cost.

**The thing that makes it hard:** imagine #general in a 100k-person company. A single message is sent. Naive delivery would mean writing to 100k recipients. The pub/sub design avoids that — but what about threads? A "reply to a message" naively requires scanning the entire channel partition for rows matching a `thread_parent_ts` column. On a busy channel this scans millions of rows for every thread view. The fix: a second Cassandra table, partitioned by `(channel_id, thread_parent_ts)` with message timestamp as the sort key. Loading a thread becomes a single partition range read — fast regardless of channel size.

**Why the standard solution works:** the two-table pattern (channel timeline + thread index) is the canonical Cassandra pattern for this problem. You write every reply twice — once to each table — but that's a small cost: a write to two tables in one Cassandra cluster costs roughly the same as writing to one, whereas the alternative (scanning millions of rows per thread view) degrades linearly as the channel grows. Cassandra is chosen because it handles high write throughput gracefully by appending to sorted partitions, and it scales horizontally by adding nodes without resharding all data.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- One Cassandra cluster per workspace shard (or shared cluster with workspace-prefixed keyspaces for smaller customers). Two tables: `messages (channel_id, message_ts, ...)` and `thread_replies (channel_id, thread_parent_ts, message_ts, ...)`.
- Redis or a lightweight pub/sub service for the per-channel fan-out topics. Kafka sits in front of the persist path (Kafkagate pattern) so a viral message → 100k link unfurls does not OOM the worker queue.
- On every WebSocket message receive: publish to channel topic → await persist ack → return ack to sender. Never ack before persist.
  ```
  receive(msg):
    publish(topic=channel_id, msg)        # fan-out to online users
    cassandra.write(messages, thread_replies)
    elasticsearch.index_async(msg)
    return ack(msg.id)
  ```
#### Clarifying Questions
- Workspace isolation strict?
- Threading depth?
- File uploads?
- Integrations / bots / app marketplace?
- E2E encryption?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** channels (public/private), DMs, threads, mentions, file uploads, search, presence, integrations
- **NFR:** real-time delivery (<200ms), per-workspace data isolation, fast search, rich client (web/mobile/desktop)

#### Scale Estimate
- **DAU / workspaces:** ~30M+ DAU (Slack last-public 2019 + organic growth); ~500k+ workspaces (public); avg ~60 DAU/workspace; large workspaces (Salesforce-class) hit 10M+ msgs/day
- **Concurrent WS:** during business-hours overlap ~33% of DAU online → ~10M concurrent WebSocket connections globally
- **Message size:** ~500 B avg = (text ~200 B + msg_id/ts/user/channel ids ~80 B + reactions ~50 B + attachment refs ~50 B + JSON framing ~120 B); rich blocks (Block Kit) bump to ~2 KB
- **Daily message volume:** ~30M DAU × ~150 msgs/day/active user (Slack disclosed avg) ≈ ~4.5B–5B msgs/day × 500 B ≈ ~2.5 TB/day raw → Cassandra-class with RF 3 = ~7.5 TB/day stored
- **Annual message storage:** 2.5 TB/day × 365 ≈ ~900 TB/yr raw; free-plan 90-day retention trims ~40% of historical ingest → ~550 TB/yr effective hot
- **File uploads:** ~30M DAU × ~1.5 files/user/day ≈ ~50M files/day; 500 KB avg (mix of pasted images, docs, screenshots) → 50M × 500 KB ≈ ~25 TB/day on object store
- **Search-index per large workspace:** 10M msgs/day × 5 yr × 500 B ≈ 10M × 1825 × 500 B ≈ ~9 TB indexed; tier-1 enterprise customers get dedicated ES clusters for query isolation
- **WebSocket fan-out:** 10M concurrent users × ~5 channels avg active subscription = 50M topic subscriptions; per-channel pub/sub fan-out keeps each msg cost at O(MS-servers-with-subscribers) ≪ O(workspace_users)
- **Mention/notification stream:** ~5% of messages mention someone (industry heuristic) → 5B × 5% = ~250M mentions/day → 250M / 86,400 ≈ ~2.9k/s avg; 10× business-hours peak → ~30k/s on Notif dispatcher

#### API Contract
```
WebSocket (RTM):
  →  { type: send_message, channel, text, thread_ts? }
  ←  { type: message, ... }
  ←  { type: presence_change, user, presence }

REST:
  GET /channels/{ws_id}                  → list
  GET /history/{channel_id}?cursor       → messages
  POST /upload                           → file_id
  GET /search?q=...&workspace=...        → results
```

#### High-Level Design
```
   Client                                Client
     │                                     │
     │ WebSocket                  WebSocket │
     ↓                                     ↓
  ┌──────────┐                       ┌──────────┐
  │ MS Server│                       │ MS Server│   (per-workspace
  │ (workspace│                       │  sticky) │    sharding)
  │  sticky) │                       │          │
  └────┬─────┘                       └────┬─────┘
       │                                  │
       └────→ Pub/Sub bus per workspace ←──┘
              (channel topics)
                    ↓
       ┌──────────────────────────────┐
       │ Persist Service              │ → Wide-column store (channel partition)
       │                              │ → Search indexer
       │                              │ → Notif Service (push if offline)
       └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**How to read the diagram:** the architecture has a live side and a history side. WebSocket connections make channels and DMs feel instant, while durable storage and indexing make those same messages searchable and recoverable later.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** enterprise messaging is not just about delivery. Teams expect presence, threads, history, permissions, and search to work together. That pushes the design beyond a simple chat server.

**What this layout buys you:** responsive collaboration plus long-term retrieval. The tradeoff is that tenancy and search become core architectural concerns instead of optional features.

#### Data Model
- **workspaces** (transactional meta store): `(ws_id, name, plan, primary_shard)`
- **channels** (wide-column store per-workspace): `(channel_id, type, members[], created_at)`
- **messages** (wide-column store, partition by channel_id):
  `(channel_id, message_ts (ulid), user_id, text, thread_parent_ts?, edits, reactions)`
- **search index** (per workspace): all messages tokenized
- **files** (object store + signed URLs per workspace)

#### Detailed Design
**Real-time delivery.** Each client opens one WebSocket to its workspace's MS Server pool, sticky-routed by workspace ID so reconnects land on the same pool (warm presence + cached channel list). Posting is fanned via per-channel pub/sub: the originating MS Server publishes once to `topic:{channel_id}`, and every other MS Server in the workspace that has at least one subscribed client consumes once and pushes to those clients locally. This keeps fan-out cost at O(MS-servers-with-subscribers), not O(channel-members) and not O(workspace-clients) — a critical scaling property for a 100k-seat workspace where most users aren't in any given channel.

**Threads.** A reply is a normal message with `thread_parent_ts` set to the parent's `message_ts`. *Threading is single-level by design* — a "reply to a reply" still attaches to the same `thread_parent_ts`, so the conversation flattens to parent + ordered replies (no recursive tree). This is a product choice to avoid Reddit-style nesting, and it keeps the data model trivial: one column, not a materialised path. The trick is read patterns: loading a thread is "give me all messages with `thread_parent_ts = X`" — naive, this scans the entire channel partition. The fix is a denormalised second table partitioned by `(channel_id, thread_parent_ts)` with `message_ts` as the clustering key, written alongside the main `messages` table; loading a thread is then one partition range read. Thread participants (anyone who replied or was mentioned) get pushed updates via a thread-specific pub/sub topic, distinct from the parent channel's topic, so a noisy thread doesn't pollute every channel member.

**E2E encryption (Enterprise Key Management).** Slack does *not* offer strict end-to-end encryption — server-side search, link unfurls, integrations, and DLP all need plaintext. Instead, paying enterprise customers get *Enterprise Key Management* (EKM): each workspace's messages and files are encrypted at rest with a customer-controlled key in their own AWS KMS, and the customer can revoke the key to make their workspace unreadable on demand. Trade-off is explicit: no E2E (Slack still decrypts server-side for indexing), but the customer holds the off-switch. True E2E would break server-side search and the bot ecosystem — a product trade-off Slack consciously declined.

**Notifications.** Mentions (`@user`) pierce do-not-disturb because they're high-signal; DMs always notify; channel messages respect per-channel-per-user preferences (all / mentions only / nothing). Notif Service consumes the persist stream (not the WebSocket fan-out path), so notifications are delivered regardless of whether the user is online — APNs/FCM is the path of record. Dedupe via `(user_id, message_ts)` so a reconnect doesn't double-notify.

**Search.** Each workspace has its own dedicated ES index, sized by message volume. New messages index asynchronously off the persist stream — search is eventually consistent (a few seconds lag), which is fine because the user just posted it and can see it in their channel. Filters (`from:user`, `in:channel`, `before:date`) compile to ES query DSL on indexed fields. Per-workspace indices give natural ACL (no cross-tenant leak risk in query construction), perf isolation (one big workspace can't slow another's search), and per-plan retention (free-tier indices auto-prune past 90 days).

**Integrations / bots.** Incoming webhooks let external systems POST a payload to a per-workspace URL; outgoing webhooks fire to external services on matching events. The App framework runs apps against an OAuth-scoped token; Slack hosts a limited bot-server fleet and forwards channel events to the app's webhook endpoint. Per-app rate limits at the API gateway protect the workspace's bus — a runaway bot can't fan-out faster than its bucket allows.

**Slack Connect (cross-workspace shared channels).** Slack abandoned the obvious "replicate the channel into both shards" model — it caused inconsistent message ordering across the two copies and double-write integrity bugs. Instead, a shared channel has *one* authoritative shard (the channel's *home* workspace), and reads/writes from the other side route to that home shard. A `shared_channels` bridge table on each participating workspace stores `(channel_id, home_shard, local_overrides)` — each side can override the channel name and topic locally, but the message log is single-source. Cross-workspace user identity required extending the edge cache (Flannel) to expose external user objects only when the viewer shares a channel with that user, so the identity boundary is per-channel rather than per-workspace. *[Source: Slack engineering — How Slack built shared channels]*

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant UA as User A (Workspace X)
    participant MSX as MS Server (Shard X)
    participant HomeShard as Channel Home Shard (X)
    participant Bridge as shared_channels bridge
    participant ShardY as Shard Y (Workspace Y)
    participant MSY as MS Server (Shard Y)
    participant UB as User B (Workspace Y)

    UA->>MSX: send_message in #shared
    MSX->>HomeShard: persist message (canonical)
    HomeShard-->>MSX: ack + message_ts
    MSX-->>UA: ack
    HomeShard->>Bridge: publish on shared-channel topic
    Bridge->>ShardY: forward (channel_id, msg, ts)
    ShardY->>MSY: pubsub fan-out on channel
    MSY-->>UB: WebSocket push
    Note over Bridge,ShardY: Identity lookup:<br/>Flannel resolves User A's<br/>profile for Workspace Y via<br/>per-channel ACL
    UB->>MSY: react / reply
    MSY->>HomeShard: write to canonical log<br/>(cross-shard hop)
    HomeShard->>Bridge: publish update
    Bridge->>MSX: forward to A's shard
    MSX-->>UA: reaction visible
```

The expensive part is the cross-shard hop on writes from the *visiting* workspace — a User B reaction crosses Shard Y → Home Shard X. Slack accepts this latency because the alternative (replicating the message log on both shards) creates ordering bugs that are unfixable without a global consensus on every message. The bridge layer carries channel events, not user state; user identity is resolved on-demand via Flannel scoped per-channel-membership. *[Source: Slack engineering]*

**Job queue: Kafka + Redis (post-2016 architecture).** Slack runs ~1.4 B background jobs/day at 33k peak — push notifications, link unfurls, calendar reminders, billing — all fed off the persist stream. The original Redis-only queue had two bugs: client app servers held N×M connections (every client to every Redis), and adding workers raised polling pressure on already-stressed Redis. The fix layered Kafka in front: PHP/Hack web servers POST jobs to *Kafkagate* (Go), which writes them to a 32-partition Kafka topic with replication factor 3 and 2-day retention. *JQRelay* (Go) pulls from Kafka and pushes to the Redis worker queues; Consul-based locks let JQRelay self-elect per partition. Result: enqueue rate decoupled from execute capacity, durable storage instead of lossy in-memory, and Redis stops being a single point of memory pressure. *[Source: Slack engineering — scaling Slack's job queue]*

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: Half a workspace's WebSocket pool gets killed by a deploy. What happens?**
Clients see disconnect, reconnect with exponential backoff + jitter to a healthy MS server. Drain-mode deploys move connections off N% of pool at a time, never all at once. *If pushed:* without jitter you get a thundering herd reconnect; mitigate with token-bucket admission on new servers and replay-from-cursor (last-seen ts) so clients don't refetch entire history.

**Q: A workspace grows from 1k to 100k seats overnight (Salesforce buys a startup). What breaks?**
That workspace's shard saturates — DB write throughput, ES index size, MS server pool all grow linearly with members and message volume. *If pushed:* split the workspace across multiple shards keyed by channel range; introduces cross-shard mention search but it's tractable. Or vertically scale the shard hardware as a stopgap.

**Q: How do you ensure mention notifications aren't lost if the user is offline?**
Persist Service writes the message to Cassandra, then publishes; Notif Service consumes the same stream and delivers via APNs/FCM regardless of online state. WebSocket path is additive, not authoritative. *If pushed:* deduplicate via `(user_id, message_ts)` so reconnect doesn't double-notify; mobile push has platform-side retry on failure.

**Q: How does threading not destroy Cassandra partitions?**
Maintain a second table partitioned by `(channel_id, thread_parent_ts)` with `message_ts` as the clustering key — loading a thread is then a single-partition range read, not a filter on the whole channel partition. The main `messages` table stays partitioned by `channel_id` for the channel timeline. *If pushed:* writes go to both tables (denormalized); very long threads (1000+ replies) still fit in one partition fine; secondary indexes on the channel partition would work but degrade under write load — denormalized table is the canonical Cassandra fix.

**Q: A bot in a popular workspace posts 1000 messages/minute. Defenses?**
Per-bot rate limits at the API layer (token bucket per workspace+app); workspace admins can throttle or revoke OAuth scopes. *If pushed:* fan-out cost capped via pub/sub decoupling; backpressure on the bot's webhook with 429s; alerting on per-app message volume anomalies.

**Q: How is data isolation enforced between workspaces in shared infrastructure?**
Every query is scoped by `workspace_id` at the application layer; cross-workspace queries forbidden by code review and integration tests. ES indices are physically separate per workspace. *If pushed:* compliance customers (healthcare, gov) get dedicated shards; encryption keys are per-workspace via KMS, so even at storage one workspace's data is unreadable to another's keys.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Hot channel** (#general at 10am, 100k members all online) — naive per-recipient delivery would be O(members) writes per message. Per-channel pub/sub topic flips this to O(MS-servers-with-subscribers) — each MS Server consumes the message once and locally pushes to its connected subscribers. Cost stays bounded regardless of how many members the channel has.
- **Workspace shard hot** (Salesforce-sized customer: 100k seats, 10M msgs/day) — the workspace's single shard saturates DB writes, ES index size, and MS Server pool capacity. Split that one workspace across multiple sub-shards keyed by channel range; cross-shard mention search for that workspace becomes a fan-out query, but everyone else is unaffected.
- **Message edit/delete** — original message is already cached on every viewer's client and indexed in ES. Edits/deletes propagate as separate events on the same channel topic; clients reconcile by `message_ts` (replace local copy), and the search index updates via the same persist stream.
- **Search cardinality per workspace** — a 5-year history at 10M msgs/day is multi-TB per ES index. Per-workspace sizing keeps any one index manageable; free-plan retention caps (90 days) trim ~40% of ingest at index time. Tier-1 enterprise customers run on dedicated search clusters so their query patterns don't contend with anyone else's.
- **WebSocket connection storm on deploy** (10M concurrent connections all reconnecting at once) — without jitter, the surviving servers melt. Drain-mode deploys move connections off N% of the pool at a time, never all at once; clients use exponential backoff + jitter on reconnect; new servers do token-bucket admission so they don't accept faster than their warmup allows. Replay-from-cursor (last-seen `message_ts`) on reconnect avoids refetching entire history.
- **Slack Connect cross-shard hop on visitor writes** — User B reacting from a non-home shard pays an extra hop to write into the channel's home shard.
