**Q: 10,000 workers all trying to acquire the same lock. Bottleneck?**
ZK ephemeral sequential nodes give you a fair queue — each waiter watches only the znode immediately before theirs (not all of them), so wake-up traffic is O(1) per release. *If pushed:* even so, contention this high suggests redesign — shard the lock by sub-key, use optimistic concurrency (CAS on a version), or change the workflow to a single-leader pull from a queue rather than N workers fighting for one resource.

**Q: How do you handle re-entrancy (same holder wants to acquire its own lock again)?**
Store `(owner_id, refcount)` instead of just `owner_id`; acquire bumps refcount if same owner, release decrements. *If pushed:* re-entrancy across process restarts is unsafe (different instance, same logical owner) — bind owner_id to a session/lease ID that dies with the process; ZK ephemeral nodes do this naturally via session.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Lock contention** (10k workers fighting for one lock) — naive Redis SETNX has no fairness; everyone polls and the lucky winner gets it next, leading to wasted retries and unbounded wait times. ZK ephemeral sequential nodes give a fair FIFO queue — each waiter watches only the znode immediately before its own, so wake-up traffic per release is O(1), not O(waiters). Even so, contention this high is a smell — shard the lock by sub-key, or move to a single-leader-pulls-from-queue pattern.
- **Lock service outage** — has to fail one way: fail-closed (no acquires granted, work stops, correctness preserved) or fail-open (acquires granted on minority side, work continues, correctness sacrificed). Always fail-closed for correctness locks; for "best-effort" locks (rate-limit dedupe), fail-open + idempotent work + reconciliation is acceptable.
- **Long GC pause holding lock** (30s pause with 20s TTL) — lock expires while holder thinks it's still active; second process acquires; both write. This *cannot* be fixed at the lock service. Fencing tokens at the resource reject the late writer's request. Without fencing, no distributed lock implementation is safe.
- **Clock skew (Redlock)** — Redlock measures TTL via each node's wall clock. A forward NTP step on a majority of nodes expires locks early, letting a second client acquire while the first thinks it still holds — two holders. ZK/etcd use logical clocks via consensus, immune to wall-clock skew; use them when correctness matters.
- **Hot lock** (every request needs the same lock) — turns the lock service into a serialisation bottleneck regardless of implementation. Re-design: shard by sub-key (lock per user, not global), use optimistic concurrency (CAS on a version column, retry on conflict), or change the workflow entirely so a single leader pulls work from a queue rather than N workers fighting.
- **Missing fencing tokens at the resource** — every distributed lock implementation, no matter how careful, can grant two holders under sufficiently bad conditions (GC pause, partition, clock skew). The *only* defence is the resource itself maintaining `max_seen_token` and rejecting writes from lower tokens. Without it, the lock is theatre — it can prevent contention in the happy path but cannot prevent corruption in the failure path. *[Source: Kleppmann]*
- **Redlock failover monotonicity** — Redlock has no consensus log, so a sequence of acquires across different majorities of N nodes can produce non-monotonic "tokens" (whatever proxy you bolt on). Even *with* fencing at the resource, the resource may see token 34 then later token 33 from a new acquirer because the new majority disagreed with the old one. This is what Kleppmann means when he says "Redlock cannot supply correct fencing tokens." Use ZK/etcd whose Raft/ZAB log gives you a single global sequence. *[Source: Kleppmann; antirez]*
- **etcd lease keep-alive storm** (100k long-running holders all renewing at TTL/3 cadence) — keep-alive RPCs hit the leader's consensus log, so a flood of renewals at the same instant saturates Raft. Mitigation: jitter renewal timing across holders (each picks a random offset within `[TTL/4, TTL/2]`); lease grouping (one keep-alive renews many leases) on the etcd v3 client.

#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coordination quorum | etcd/ZooKeeper loses majority; acquires stop succeeding | Quorum-health alert; leader-election churn; request error spike | Fail closed for correctness locks, preserve existing holders until lease expiry, and restore service from majority rather than letting the minority side grant locks |
| Holder process | Lock holder crashes mid-critical-section and never releases | Missing heartbeat / keep-alive; lease expires | TTL or session expiry auto-releases the lock; downstream resource still requires fencing tokens so any late side effects from the crashed holder are rejected |
| Resource fence check | Protected resource does not enforce `max_seen_token` | Duplicate-write synthetic; stale-token write accepted in canary test | Treat fencing enforcement as part of the resource contract, not an optional client behaviour; reject writes with lower token and page if a stale-token write is ever accepted |
| Redis failover | Primary dies before async replication, losing a granted lock | Failover event; duplicate-holder alarm from resource ledger | Use Redis only for best-effort locks, or move correctness-critical locks to ZK/etcd; absorb duplicates through fencing or idempotent work semantics |
| Network partition | Client can still talk to the resource but loses the lock service session | Session timeout; keep-alive failures; minority-side acquire errors | Session-bound locks expire automatically; clients must stop work on keep-alive failure; resource rejects stale fence token if the client continues anyway |
| Renewal path | Long-running holder misses keep-alive due to GC pause or event-loop stall | Renewal-latency SLO breach; lease remaining time falls below safety floor | Renew at TTL/3 or earlier with jitter, surface "lease remaining" metrics, and use generous TTL margins relative to worst-case pause time |
| Wait queue | Contended lock waiters pile up and watchers misfire | Wait-queue depth alert; excessive wakeups per release | Use predecessor-only watches (ZK style) to keep release fan-out O(1); for extreme contention redesign away from a single global lock |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Acquire latency** (request start → lock granted or denied) — SLO p99 depends on lock class, but should stay in the low tens of milliseconds for uncontended correctness locks.
- **Quorum write success ratio** on the coordination service — primary safety signal for etcd/ZooKeeper-based locks; persistent dips mean the lock service itself is the incident.
- **Lease-renewal lateness** (actual renew time vs planned renew deadline) — catches GC pauses, overloaded runtimes, and network jitter before holders start losing locks.
- **Wait-queue depth per lock key** — identifies hot locks and fairness problems; one key going pathological is usually a design smell, not just a capacity issue.
- **Fenced-write rejection count** at the protected resource — this should be non-zero occasionally in chaos tests and near-zero in steady state; a sudden spike means holders are pausing or renewing badly.
- **Duplicate-holder canary results** — synthetic workloads intentionally pause a holder past TTL and verify the stale token is rejected; this is the proof that the end-to-end design is actually safe.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** keep correctness-critical lock services region-local. Each region runs its own etcd/ZooKeeper quorum; the protected resource is usually region-homed to the same place. Global resources route lock acquisition to the resource's home region.
- **RTO:** ~seconds to tens of seconds for in-region leader or node failover inside the coordination quorum. Cross-region failover for the lock service itself is usually a manual relocation of the resource home, not an automatic active-active flip.
- **RPO:** ~0s for committed acquires within a healthy quorum because grants are majority-committed. Cross-region DR is about rebuilding service placement, not replaying "best effort" lock state as a business record.
- **Failover cadence:** automatic inside a region; cross-region exercised sparingly because correctness locks should not normally span continents on the hot path.
- **Cross-region cost:** low for the lock service itself if kept region-local; very high if you try to run a single global quorum because every acquire pays WAN latency and every replication event crosses regions.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **Equating "I acquired the lock" with "I can safely write."** The safe write only exists once the resource enforces fencing tokens; everything else is just advisory mutual exclusion.
- **Using Redis SETNX for money-moving or correctness-critical work.** Redis single-instance locks are fast, not durable enough under failover. If two holders corrupt data, Redis is the wrong primitive.
- **Skipping the paused-holder scenario.** If you cannot explain the GC-pause / expired-TTL / late-writer case, the design is incomplete. Interviewers use this as the standard safety test.
- **Stretching one lock quorum across regions.** The latency is terrible and the failure modes get worse. Region-local quorums plus resource homing are the pragmatic answer.
- **Relying on wall-clock time for correctness.** Wall-clock TTL reasoning is exactly where Redlock criticism lands; logical monotonic sequencing from a consensus log is the safer mental model.
- **Using one global lock for throughput problems.** If every request needs the same lock, the architecture is already serialised; shard, redesign, or use optimistic concurrency instead.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Fencing tokens are the real answer.** Say this early. The lock service reduces contention; the fencing token at the resource prevents corruption.
- **ZooKeeper/etcd for correctness, Redis for best-effort.** This trade is crisp and interviewers expect you to name it rather than hand-wave over "a distributed cache."
- **The paused-holder / GC scenario is the canonical failure test.** Walk through it proactively and show how stale writes get fenced out.
- **Fairness under contention matters.** Ephemeral sequential nodes with predecessor watches give you a clean FIFO queue and O(1) wake-up fan-out.
- **Auto-release via TTL or session expiry is mandatory.** Otherwise crashed holders wedge the system forever.
- **If the lock is hot, redesign the workload.** The best distributed lock answer is often "this should be partitioned or converted into queue ownership instead."

### 36. Design a Distributed Job Scheduler / Cron
#### Problem
Schedule and reliably execute jobs across a fleet of workers — one-shot, recurring (cron), with retries, observability, and exactly-once semantics where required.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a cooking timer on a restaurant kitchen wall, with a team of chefs. The timer board tracks every dish that needs to cook and when to pull it. When a timer fires, a notecard gets placed on the chef's station ("pull the salmon in oven 3 at 7:15pm — table 12"). The chef picks up the notecard, does the work, and marks it done. If a chef drops the card and forgets, the card goes back in the queue and another chef picks it up. The key insight: the timer board (scheduler) and the kitchen work (workers) are completely separate, and each notecard is a physical artifact you can track and retry independently.

**The single-request walkthrough:** an analytics platform has a cron job defined as `0 2 * * *` (run at 2am daily), which triggers a report-generation function. At 2:00:00am, the Scheduler Service (a single elected leader running on a 3-node coordination cluster) wakes up, queries the jobs database for all entries where `next_run_at <= NOW()`, finds this job, and creates a run record: `run_id=abc123, job_id=report-daily, scheduled_at=2am, attempt=1`. It pushes this record to a durable queue (Kafka or SQS — a log that persists messages to disk and delivers them to consumers reliably, even if a consumer crashes). A worker from the worker pool pulls the record. Before touching anything, the worker calls `SETNX lease:abc123 → worker-07` with a 300-second TTL in Redis — "SETNX" means "set if not exists," an atomic Redis operation that succeeds for only one caller. Worker-07 wins the lease, runs the report generation (writes output to S3), marks the run `SUCCEEDED` in the status store, and releases the lease. Total end-to-end: job fires within ~30 seconds of 2am, runs for ~120 seconds, status shows `SUCCEEDED` with a link to the S3 output.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Jobs store (transactional database)** — stores job definitions: cron expression, payload (what to run and with what arguments), retry policy, DAG dependencies. This is the source of truth for "what should run." Separate from run history.
- **Scheduler Service (leader-elected)** — a process that polls the jobs store every ~30 seconds for due work and enqueues run records. It runs as a single elected leader (using a distributed lock so two schedulers never both enqueue the same job) with standbys ready to take over on failure. It does NOT execute jobs — only decides when they are due.
- **Durable job queue (Kafka or SQS)** — the handoff between scheduler and workers. Durable means messages survive crashes: if all workers go down at 2am, the run records sit in the queue and workers drain them when they recover. The queue decouples scheduling throughput from execution throughput.
- **Worker pool** — a fleet of stateless processes that pull run records, acquire leases, execute, and report status. Each worker is replaceable — if one crashes, another picks up the lease when it expires.
- **Lease (Redis SETNX with TTL)** — the mechanism that prevents two workers from executing the same run simultaneously. The `(job_id, scheduled_at)` pair is the dedup key. Whoever wins SETNX runs the job; everyone else who accidentally receives the same run record from the queue (queues can redeliver) sees the existing lease and skips.
- **Status/history store** — an append-only log of every run record: attempt number, start time, end time, status, error snippet. Used for debugging, alerting, and DAG dependency resolution.

**The thing that makes it hard:** every company with any scheduled jobs ends up scheduling a huge number of them at round times — midnight, 1am, 2am, the top of every hour. Imagine 2 million jobs all configured as `0 * * * *` (run at the top of every hour). At 03:00:00, the scheduler tries to enqueue 2 million run records in the same 30-second window. The durable queue gets a burst of 2 million messages at once, the worker pool tries to start 2 million jobs simultaneously, and the downstream systems those jobs write to (databases, S3, email APIs) all get hit simultaneously. This is the "thundering herd at the top of the hour" problem, and it can take down healthy systems.

**Why the standard solution works:** add `±jitter_seconds` to every job's scheduled time at creation — a random offset sampled from a window (e.g. ±60 seconds for hourly jobs). The 2 million jobs that were all at 03:00:00 now fire spread across 02:59:00 to 03:01:00. The queue gets ~33k messages/second for 120 seconds instead of 2 million at once. Combined with per-job idempotency (the job checks a ledger row before doing work, so a retry after a crash doesn't double-send an email or double-charge a card), at-least-once delivery becomes safe at scale. "Exactly once" is not realistically achievable end-to-end when the job communicates with external systems (Stripe, SendGrid) — instead, you achieve "idempotent at-least-once," which is practically equivalent.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Postgres for the jobs store (small — 10M jobs × 1KB = ~10GB). Kafka for the durable queue (24h retention, handles replay if workers fall behind). Redis for leases (300k concurrent leases × 200B = 60MB — trivially small). Wide-column store (Cassandra) for run history if run volume is high.
- Core scheduler tick pseudocode:
  ```
  every 30s (on elected leader only):
    runs = db.query("SELECT * FROM jobs WHERE next_run_at <= NOW()")
    for job in runs:
      queue.push({ run_id: uuid(), job_id: job.id, scheduled_at: job.next_run_at, attempt: 1 })
      db.update(job, next_run_at = compute_next(job.cron, now()))
  ```
- Workers: pull → `SETNX lease:{job_id}:{scheduled_at}` → execute → write status → release. Heartbeat every 30s to extend lease while running. On crash: lease TTL expires, next worker re-runs.
#### Clarifying Questions
- One-shot or recurring? Both?
- Exactly-once or at-least-once OK?
- Job failure handling (retry, DLQ, alert)?
- DAG dependencies between jobs?
- Latency tolerance vs scheduled time?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** schedule one-shot at time T; recurring (cron expr); retries with backoff; DAGs; cancel; status
- **NFR:** scale to millions of jobs, no single point of failure, no double-execution (when configured), durable

#### Scale Estimate
- **Active scheduled jobs:** hyperscaler-internal cron (Google's reportedly 10M+) is the upper bound; mid-sized SaaS scheduler ~100k. Use 10M as platform-tier default — covers Airflow + business cron + ML pipelines for a Fortune-500 fleet. Mix: ~70% recurring (cron lines, daily/hourly ETL), ~30% one-shot (delayed user actions, scheduled emails) inferred from public Airflow / Temporal usage breakdowns.
- **Run rate:** assume avg recurring job fires ~5×/day (mix of hourly + daily + weekly). 10M jobs × 5 fires = 50M runs/day → 50M ÷ 86400s ≈ 580/s steady. Round to ~2k/s steady to account for ad-hoc one-shots. Peak at top-of-hour (every `0 * * * *` lines up): assume 30% of recurring jobs are hourly → 0.3 × 7M ≈ 2.1M jobs all firing in the first 60s → ~35k/s burst; jitter-smoothed to ~10k/s peak sustained over a few minutes.
- **DAG depth:** public Airflow examples show data-platform DAGs at 50-300 tasks; deepest production DAGs (chained backfills, multi-stage ML training) ~1k tasks. Use "100s of tasks" with p99 ~1k.
- **Job record size:** `job_id` (16B UUID) + cron expr (~32B) + payload pointer (~64B URL) + retry policy (~32B JSON) + DAG parent list (~10 IDs × 16B = 160B) + state/timestamps (~100B) + headers/labels (~600B padding) → ~1KB. 10M jobs × 1KB = 10GB metadata; RF=3 → ~30GB on the transactional store.
- **Job-runs append log:** each run ~500B (run_id 16B + job_id 16B + scheduled_at 8B + started_at 8B + finished_at 8B + status 4B + attempt 4B + error snippet ~200B + output URL ~100B + worker_id 16B + padding ~120B). 10M jobs × 5 fires/day = 50M runs/day × 500B = 25GB/day raw; RF=3 → ~75GB/day.
- **Hot retention** (90d for debug dashboards — covers a quarter of incident review): 50M × 90 × 500B = ~2.25TB on the wide-column store (RF=1 hot copy; replicas archived); SSD-backed for sub-100ms history queries.
- **Cold archive** (1yr+ for compliance / audit): columnar Parquet on object store, dictionary encoding on `status`/`worker_id` → ~5-10x compression. 25GB/day ÷ 7x avg ≈ 3.5GB/day; with field pruning (drop output URL on archive) → ~250GB/yr compressed; tier to glacial after 180d.
- **Queue throughput:** 10k jobs/s peak × ~1KB envelope (job_id 16B + payload pointer ~100B + scheduled_at 8B + attempt 4B + DAG context ~200B + Kafka/RabbitMQ headers ~700B) = ~10MB/s sustained; 24h retention buffer (stuck consumer can drain) = 10MB/s × 86400 ≈ ~864GB on the durable queue cluster.
- **Lease cache footprint:** in-flight runs = peak × avg exec = 10k/s × 30s = ~300k concurrent leases. Per entry: key `lease:{job_id}:{scheduled_at}` (~40B) + worker_id (16B) + TTL metadata (~20B) + Redis overhead (~120B) ≈ ~200B. 300k × 200B = ~60MB; fits in a single Redis node.

#### API Contract
```
POST /jobs           body: { cron|run_at, payload, max_retries, dependencies? }
                     → { job_id }
GET  /jobs/{id}      → status, history, next_run
DELETE /jobs/{id}
POST /jobs/{id}/run  (manual trigger)
```

#### High-Level Design
```
                         ┌────────────────────────┐
                         │  Scheduler Service     │
                         │                        │
                         │  - reads jobs store    │
                         │  - ticks every N sec   │
                         │  - finds due jobs      │
                         │  - puts on queue       │
                         │                        │
                         │  HA via leader election│
                         │  (coordination service)│
                         └─────────┬──────────────┘
                                   ↓
                          Durable Job Queue
                                   ↓
                         ┌────────────────────────┐
                         │  Worker Pool           │
                         │  - pull job            │
                         │  - acquire job lock    │
                         │  - execute             │
                         │  - update status       │
                         │  - commit / retry      │
                         └────────────────────────┘
                                   ↓
                         Status / History Store
                                   ↓
                         Notifications / Alerts on failure
```

**How to read the diagram:** the scheduler is responsible for deciding when a run should happen, but workers are responsible for actually doing the work. A durable queue sits in the middle so the system can survive crashes and scale execution separately from timing.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** mixing scheduling and execution makes failure handling much harder. By materializing each due run as its own record, the system gets a clean place to attach leases, retries, and state transitions.

**What this layout buys you:** better durability, cleaner retries, and clearer ownership of execution state. The tradeoff is that jobs must be written with at-least-once behavior in mind.

#### Data Model
- **jobs** (transactional store):
  `(job_id, schedule (cron or run_at), payload, max_retries, dag_parent[], state)`
- **job_runs** (append-only, wide-column store):
  `(job_id, run_id, scheduled_at, started_at, finished_at, status, attempt, error)`
- **leases** (fast in-memory cache): `job_run_id → worker_id`, TTL = job timeout

#### Detailed Design
**Scheduler tick:**
```
Every 30s:
  jobs_due = SELECT * FROM jobs WHERE next_run_at <= NOW() AND state='active'
  for each:
    enqueue job_run record
    UPDATE jobs SET next_run_at = compute_next(cron, NOW())
```

**Avoiding double-execution.** Every scheduled run gets a unique `(job_id, scheduled_at)` tuple — that tuple is the de-dup key end-to-end. Before executing, a worker does `SETNX lease:{tuple} → worker_id` with a TTL equal to the job's max execution timeout. Whichever worker wins the SETNX runs the job; any other worker that pulled the same envelope from the queue (e.g. after a queue redelivery) sees the existing lease and skips. The lease TTL is critical: if the worker crashes mid-execution, the TTL expires and another worker can claim and re-run, which is why jobs must be idempotent. Concrete example: a "send invoice email" job at 09:00 lands twice on the queue; worker A claims the lease, sends, releases; worker B sees the lease was held and the run is finished, drops the envelope.

**At-least-once vs exactly-once.** Default is at-least-once: simpler infra, worker can crash and a retry will run the same logic again. Jobs are expected to be idempotent — usually via a per-`(job_id, scheduled_at)` ledger row in the job's own DB consulted before side effects. Exactly-once requires a distributed transaction across queue ack + lease + side effect + status update, which in practice means a transactional outbox in the job's DB plus careful queue acknowledgement protocols. The cost is throughput and operational complexity. Concrete example: a payment-disbursement job uses an idempotency key in Stripe; even if the worker crashes after Stripe charged, the retry hits Stripe's idempotent endpoint and gets the same charge_id back.

**DAG support (Airflow-style).** Each task declares its parent task IDs; the scheduler emits a task only after every parent has reached `COMPLETED` for the same DAG-run instance. Implementation: when a task finishes, the worker writes its status and publishes a `task_completed` event; a DAG reconciler subscribes, looks up which downstream tasks are now satisfied, and enqueues them. Failure modes are policy: `fail_dependents` (skip everything downstream), `retry_with_backoff` (retry the failing parent N times before failing), or `manual_override` (operator marks parent as success). Concrete example: an ETL DAG with extract → transform → load; if `transform` fails after 3 retries, the `load` step is marked SKIPPED and an alert fires; the operator can mark `transform` success once they've fixed the data manually.

**Cron scheduling.** Parse cron expression once per job, store `next_run_at`; on each scheduler tick, compute next from current `last_run_at` using a tz-aware library (handles DST, leap seconds). Catch-up policy is per-job: `catch_up=skip` (jump to the next future fire and forget missed) for things like "send daily summary," `catch_up=run_all` for things like billing aggregation that must process every period. Compute `next_run_at` immediately after enqueueing (not after the worker finishes) so a long-running job doesn't block subsequent schedules. Concrete example: a job set to `*/5 * * * *` whose worker takes 8 minutes — with skip policy, you get one run every 8 minutes; with run-all, the queue stacks until throughput catches up.

**Latency tolerance vs scheduled time.** Default budget is `tick_interval + queue_drain_time` — with a 30s tick on a healthy queue, jobs fire within ~30-60s of their scheduled second. That covers the bulk of cron workloads (nightly ETLs, hourly reports). Sub-second precision is not promised by a polling-tick architecture; jobs that genuinely need it (timed market-data snapshots, second-precision timers) route through an event-driven delay-queue path (Redis ZSET on `next_run_at` woken by the imminent fire), trading scheduler simplicity for tighter precision. Per-job SLO: `actual_fire_at - scheduled_at < 60s` for tick-driven, `< 1s` for delay-queue-driven; the tick-lag alert (now − last successful tick > 2× tick interval) catches lagging schedulers before users see late jobs.

**Long-running jobs.** Worker heartbeats — extends the lease TTL by a fixed delta every N seconds (e.g. extend by 60s every 30s). If the worker process dies, OS kills it, or pod is evicted, heartbeats stop and the lease naturally expires; the run becomes claimable by a replacement worker. The job itself should checkpoint progress (rows processed, intermediate result IDs) so a retry can resume rather than restart from scratch. Concrete example: a 4-hour data backfill job heartbeats every 30s with a 90s TTL; if the worker dies at the 2-hour mark, within 90s another worker picks up and reads the checkpoint to resume from row 1.5M instead of row 0.

**Job-run state machine.** A run progresses through a small set of well-defined states; every transition is durable so a crashed scheduler/worker can pick up without ambiguity. `QUEUED` (scheduler emitted run, sitting on durable queue) → `LEASED` (worker won SETNX, lease key in Redis with TTL = job timeout) → `RUNNING` (worker started side-effects, heartbeats renewing lease) → terminal `SUCCEEDED` / `FAILED` / `TIMED_OUT`. Failures within the retry budget loop back to `QUEUED` with `attempt+1` and exponential backoff; failures past budget land in `DEAD_LETTER` and page on-call. Lease expiry mid-`RUNNING` (worker died) transitions to `ORPHANED` and is reclaimed by the next puller. *[Source: Temporal docs, Airbnb Dynein blog]*

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> QUEUED: scheduler tick emits run
    QUEUED --> LEASED: worker SETNX lease
    LEASED --> RUNNING: side-effects start
    RUNNING --> RUNNING: heartbeat (TTL extend)
    RUNNING --> SUCCEEDED: ack + status write
    RUNNING --> FAILED: error + retries left
    RUNNING --> TIMED_OUT: lease expired
    FAILED --> QUEUED: backoff (attempt+1)
    TIMED_OUT --> QUEUED: orphan reclaim
    FAILED --> DEAD_LETTER: retries exhausted
    SUCCEEDED --> [*]
    DEAD_LETTER --> [*]
```

**Airflow vs Argo vs Temporal — three different idempotent step models.** The orchestrator zoo collapses into three camps with very different opinions about where state lives. *Airflow* (Python DAGs, central scheduler + Celery/Kubernetes executors) keeps DAG state in a relational DB; tasks are idempotent at the DAG-run level keyed by `(dag_id, execution_date)`. Operator/sensor abstractions encourage external-system idempotency (S3 sensors, idempotent SQL). Strength: rich operator library, mature ecosystem; weakness: scheduler is a singleton bottleneck, sub-minute scheduling is awkward. *Argo Workflows* (Kubernetes CRDs, container-per-step) stores workflow state in etcd via Kubernetes — every step is a pod, idempotency is just "container exited 0 with the same input artefact"; retries restart pods. Strength: cloud-native, perfect for ML pipelines that already containerise; weakness: etcd object size limits cap workflow complexity (~1MB per CR). *Temporal* (formerly Cadence — Uber's invention) flips the model: workflow code is *durable* — the runtime captures every effect (activity call, timer, signal) into an event-sourced history, and replays history on recovery to reconstruct local variables. Activities are the unit of side-effect, retried with exponential backoff and idempotency keys. Strength: write workflows as ordinary code with no DAG DSL, sub-second scheduling, decade-long workflows survive arbitrary process restarts; weakness: replay-based determinism is subtle (no `time.now()` outside activities, no random without seeding). *[Source: Temporal docs, Argo Workflows GH, Airflow docs]*

**Idempotent step model.** All three orchestrators converge on the same contract: a step receives `(workflow_id, step_id, attempt)` and is expected to be safe to invoke multiple times. Standard patterns: (a) external idempotency keys passed to the side-effect (Stripe, SendGrid, Snowflake `MERGE`); (b) write-your-own ledger row in the step's own DB consulted before mutating state; (c) content-addressed outputs (Argo artefacts) so a re-run produces the same hash and downstream tasks short-circuit on cache hit. The orchestrator never promises exactly-once side-effects — it promises *at-least-once with deterministic step identity*, and pushes idempotency to the step author.

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: At-least-once vs exactly-once — how do you decide?**
Default to at-least-once and require jobs to be idempotent — exactly-once across queue + worker + side-effect needs distributed transactions and is rarely worth it. *If pushed:* exactly-once is achievable for self-contained jobs (transactional outbox + idempotency key in the worker's own DB); for jobs that hit external APIs, you can't guarantee it without their cooperation.

**Q: 10x scale — millions of jobs scheduled per minute. What breaks first?**
The single scheduler's `SELECT WHERE next_run_at <= NOW()` query — it scans more rows than it can fire. Shard jobs by `hash(job_id)` across N schedulers, each with its own slice. *If pushed:* move from polling to a delay-queue (Redis ZSET keyed on next_run_at) so the scheduler is event-driven instead of polling.

**Q: A worker crashes 30 min into a 1-hour job — does it run twice?**
Yes, once the lease TTL expires another worker grabs the tuple. That's why jobs must be idempotent. *If pushed:* expose a checkpoint API so long jobs can resume mid-run; if the work isn't naturally idempotent, wrap it in a per-`(job_id, scheduled_at)` ledger row that the job consults before doing side-effects.

**Q: Cron expression fires "every minute" but the previous run is still going — what's the policy?**
Per-job concurrency setting: `allow_overlap=false` (default) skips the new run; `allow_overlap=true` lets them stack. Encode this in the lease check. *If pushed:* a queue-with-cap mode (max 3 in flight) for jobs that benefit from parallelism but shouldn't fan out unbounded.

**Q: How do you handle DST transitions for cron schedules?**
Store schedules in the user's timezone for display, but compute next-fire by converting to UTC at evaluation time using a current tz database. On spring-forward "0 2 * * *" has no 02:00 local — most schedulers skip (or fire at 03:00 if configured to catch up missed runs). On fall-back 02:00 occurs twice in wall-clock; naive cron will double-fire unless you dedupe on `(job_id, scheduled_at_utc)`. *If pushed:* per-job policy flag — `dst_skip` (default, suppress the duplicate fall-back hour) vs `dst_double` for jobs that genuinely want both wall-clock occurrences.

**Q: How do you monitor a scheduler that fires millions of jobs?**
Per-job: success/fail/duration/lease-acquire-latency, all tagged. System-level: scheduler tick lag (now − last successful tick) is the SLO; alert if > 2× tick interval. *If pushed:* heartbeat in reverse — every job declares an expected next-run, page if it doesn't fire within a grace window.

**Q: Airflow vs Argo vs Temporal — which would you actually pick?**
Airflow for batch ETL with mature operators and minute-granularity (data team default). Argo for ML/CI pipelines that already containerise (every step is a pod with content-addressed inputs/outputs). Temporal for long-running stateful workflows (e.g. order saga that spans days, includes human approval, survives any restart) — the durable-execution model is uniquely powerful when control flow is complex and long-lived. *If pushed:* the orchestrators don't compose well across the same workload — pick by the dominant axis of the problem (data freshness vs container artifacts vs control-flow complexity).

**Q: How does Temporal achieve "exactly-once" workflow execution if exactly-once is impossible?**
It doesn't promise exactly-once *side-effects* — it promises deterministic *workflow logic replay*. Workflow code is captured as event-sourced history (`activity_started`, `activity_completed`, `timer_fired`); on worker restart the runtime replays history to reconstruct local variables, then resumes from the next undecided point. Activities (the side-effect units) are still at-least-once, retried with exponential backoff, and the activity author is on the hook for idempotency keys. *If pushed:* the determinism contract forbids non-deterministic primitives in workflow code (no `time.now()`, no random) — these must move to activities.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Scheduler is bottleneck** — single leader's `WHERE next_run_at <= NOW()` query saturates the jobs DB once it has to scan and update millions of rows per tick. Shard the jobs store by `hash(job_id)` and elect one leader per shard so each scheduler only sees a slice; for ultra-scale, switch to an event-driven delay-queue (Redis ZSET on `next_run_at`) so wake-ups are O(1) per fire.
- **Thundering herd at midnight** — every "0 0 * * *" job fires the same wall-clock second, overwhelming workers and downstream APIs. Apply jitter at schedule registration: spread within a configurable window (`±5min`), or accept the cron line as-is but jitter the actual fire time per job using a stable hash, so the user-visible schedule still reads "midnight."
- **Dead worker holding lease** — without expiry the run sits forever and dependent DAG tasks stall. Keep the heartbeat interval short relative to the lease TTL (heartbeat 30s, TTL 90s) so a crashed worker frees its lease in under 2 minutes; pair with `runs_in_progress` SLO alerting.
- **Failed job blocking dependents** — repeated failures wedge an entire DAG. Bound retries (`max_retries` with exponential backoff), then route to a DLQ for inspection; downstream tasks are marked SKIPPED with a parent_failed reason rather than queued indefinitely; on-call gets one alert, not one per dependent.
- **Time zone bugs** — DST transitions either skip a fire (spring-forward 02:00 doesn't exist) or double-fire (fall-back 02:00 happens twice). Store schedules in UTC plus original tz; compute fires in the user tz at evaluation time using a current tz database; dedupe on `(job_id, scheduled_at_utc)` so wall-clock duplicates collapse to one run.
- **Replay storm on Temporal-style restart** — a worker fleet restart triggers history replay for all in-flight workflows simultaneously, hammering the history store. Stagger worker restarts (rolling 10% at a time), cap concurrent histories per worker; for very long histories, use continue-as-new to truncate after N events into a fresh workflow with carry-over state.
- **Etcd object size limit (Argo)** — workflow CR exceeds the 1MB etcd value limit on long-running pipelines. Move large step outputs to artefact stores (S3, MinIO) and reference by URL; checkpoint long workflows into multiple sub-workflows; archive completed workflow CRs out of etcd to an external store within minutes of completion.


#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Scheduler leader | Leader pod dies mid-tick; standby has not yet acquired the lease | `time_since_last_tick` exceeds 2× tick interval; coordination-service watch on the lease key | Short lease TTL (15-30s) so failover happens within one tick; standbys poll the lease and acquire on expiry; on takeover, reconcile from `next_run_at` to skip already-emitted runs |
| Durable queue | Broker partition unavailable, ack stalls and producers block | Producer-side enqueue latency p99 alert; broker ISR-shrink alerts | Multi-AZ broker cluster with `min.insync.replicas≥2`; scheduler buffers a small in-memory backlog and replays once the broker recovers; consumer-lag SLO triggers worker autoscale |
| Lease cache | Redis primary fails before the lease is set, or split-brain produces two holders | Redis sentinel/cluster failover events; duplicate-execution alarm from the job-side ledger | Redis-with-Sentinel + 2s SETNX timeout; jobs are idempotent (per-`(job_id, scheduled_at)` ledger row in the job's own DB) so a duplicate is absorbed; dead-letter on persistent contention |
| Worker | Worker process killed mid-execution (OOM, pod evicted) | Heartbeat stops; lease TTL expires within 90s | Heartbeat 30s/TTL 90s so reclaim is bounded; workers checkpoint progress so retries resume; bounded `max_retries` then DLQ |
| Jobs DB | Transactional store unavailable for `next_run_at` updates | Connection-pool saturation; query-timeout alarms | Read-replica fallback for `WHERE next_run_at <= NOW()`; queue scheduler ticks until the primary recovers (catch-up policy decides whether to fire missed runs) |
| DAG reconciler | Crash leaves child tasks unscheduled despite the parent reaching COMPLETED | DAG-stuck SLO: any DAG with no progress for 2× p95 task duration | Reconciler is stateless — restart re-reads `task_completed` events from a durable topic and is idempotent on `(dag_run_id, task_id)` |
| Cron tz library | Stale tz database miscomputes the DST boundary | Drift between `expected_fire_at` and actual fires across the fleet at DST cutover | Auto-update tz data weekly via a package mirror; fleet-wide canary on DST weekends; dedupe on `(job_id, scheduled_at_utc)` to absorb wall-clock duplicates |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Scheduler tick lag** (`now − last_successful_tick`) — SLO p99 < 2× tick interval; page if > 5×. This is the clearest leading indicator of scheduler health.
- **Job fire delay** (`actual_fire_at − scheduled_at`) — SLO p99 < 60s for tick-driven, < 1s for delay-queue-driven; per-job breakdown to catch single-tenant regressions.
- **Run-state distribution** (counts of `QUEUED / LEASED / RUNNING / SUCCEEDED / FAILED / DEAD_LETTER` per minute) — sudden DLQ growth means a downstream API broke.
- **Lease-acquire success ratio** — SLO > 99.9%; sustained dips signal Redis contention or duplicate-pull from queue redelivery.
- **Worker concurrency utilisation** (in-flight runs ÷ worker capacity) — SLO < 80% during steady state, with autoscale headroom for top-of-hour bursts.
- **DAG end-to-end latency** (DAG-run start → terminal task) — SLO per DAG class; alert on p99 regression versus a 7-day baseline.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** active-passive per region. Each region has its own scheduler-leader, worker pool, and Redis lease cache; the jobs DB and `job_runs` log replicate cross-region (synchronous within a metro AZ-pair, async across continents) with one canonical write region per shard.
- **RTO:** ~5 minutes for scheduler-leader failover within a region (lease expiry + standby takeover + state warm-up). Cross-region failover is operator-triggered: ~30 minutes including DNS cutover and warm-pool stand-up.
- **RPO:** ~0s for scheduler decisions in the same region (sync-replicated jobs DB). For cross-region failover, up to ~5s of in-flight `job_runs` records (async replication lag); replay is idempotent so duplicate runs are caught by the lease layer.
- **Failover cadence:** automatic for scheduler-leader within a region (continuous via lease election). Manual game-days quarterly for cross-region failover; chaos drills monthly for worker-pool drain.
- **Cross-region cost:** mainly egress on `job_runs` log streaming (~75GB/day raw, ~10GB compressed) and snapshot replication of jobs metadata (~30GB nightly delta). Worker fleet is duplicated standby in the passive region — typically run at 20% capacity, scaled out on cutover.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **"Cron implies exactly-once" — wrong.** A naive interpretation of cron is "the job runs once at the scheduled time," but the practical guarantee a distributed scheduler delivers is *at-least-once with idempotent steps*. Conflating the two leads candidates to under-design retries, omit lease-based dedup, or skip the idempotency-key conversation. State the at-least-once contract explicitly and design jobs around it.
- **"Single-leader scheduler" without HA leader election.** Just running one scheduler pod is not HA — when it dies, no jobs fire until someone notices. The leader must be elected via a coordination service (etcd/ZooKeeper/Consul) with a short-lived lease so a standby can take over within one tick. Candidates often hand-wave "we'll have failover" without naming the lease/lock primitive that enforces single-writer.
- **Polling for `next_run_at <= NOW()` at all scales.** Polling works fine to ~100k jobs but degrades sharply once the scan saturates the jobs DB. The right answer at scale is a delay-queue (Redis ZSET keyed on `next_run_at` woken by the imminent fire) so wake-ups are O(1) per fire — mention this as the natural 10× evolution.
- **Treating DAG failure handling as out-of-scope.** Real DAGs fail in the middle constantly; without an explicit policy (`fail_dependents` / `retry_with_backoff` / `manual_override`) downstream tasks queue indefinitely and on-call gets one alert per dependent. Make the policy a per-DAG attribute and route dead-lettered runs to a clear DLQ.
- **Ignoring DST and tz handling.** Cron expressions evaluated naively against local time double-fire on fall-back and miss spring-forward. The fix is to store schedules with the original tz, compute `scheduled_at_utc` with a current tz database, and dedupe runs on the UTC tuple.
- **No backpressure between scheduler and queue.** When workers stall, naive schedulers keep enqueuing, blowing past queue retention and losing the head of the backlog. The scheduler should observe queue depth and pause emission past a threshold rather than overrun.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Hot scheduler tick → leader election with a short lease** is the load-bearing HA story. The scheduler is logically a singleton (only one tick at a time, otherwise duplicate enqueues at every cron boundary), and the standby acquires a coordination-service lease (etcd/ZooKeeper) with a 15-30s TTL — short enough that recovery is bounded, long enough to absorb GC pauses without thrashing leadership.
- **At-least-once + idempotent steps is the consistency contract.** Exactly-once across queue + worker + side-effect is impractical at scale; idempotency keys and per-`(job_id, scheduled_at)` ledger rows let retries be safe, and lease-based dedup keeps the steady-state honest.
- **`(job_id, scheduled_at)` is the dedup key end-to-end.** Mention it explicitly — it tells the interviewer you've thought about how queue redelivery, scheduler restart, and worker crash all collapse to one logical run.
- **Decoupling scheduler from execution unlocks independent scaling.** The scheduler is CPU-light (in-memory `next_run_at` heap, single-leader-per-shard), the worker pool scales on job-CPU/memory; the durable queue between them is the bulkhead.
- **Jitter and exposure caps tame midnight thundering herds.** Stable per-job hash jitter within a window keeps the user-visible cron line ("0 0 * * *") while spreading actual fires over minutes — the difference between a melting downstream API and a healthy one.
- **Trade-off you'd revisit at 10× scale:** moving from polling tick to event-driven delay-queue (Redis ZSET on `next_run_at`) so wake-ups are O(1) per fire instead of O(rows-scanned). Worth the operational complexity once the jobs table crosses ~10M active rows.
- **DAG semantics belong to a reconciler, not the scheduler.** Keep the scheduler dumb (emit due jobs); a separate reconciler subscribes to `task_completed` events and decides which downstream tasks become eligible — this keeps the scheduler tick fast and DAG failure policy explicit.

### 37. Design Google Docs (Real-Time Collaborative Editor)
#### Problem
Allow many users to edit the same document simultaneously, see each other's edits in real time, with eventual convergence and offline support.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a shared whiteboard in a meeting room, but instead of everyone erasing and redrawing the whole board every time someone makes a mark, each person announces a small instruction — "I wrote 'hello' starting at position 10" or "I erased 3 characters starting at position 5." A coordinator standing at the board receives these instructions, figures out how they interact when two people wrote at the same spot, and announces the resolved instructions to everyone so all whiteboards end up identical. The coordinator also takes a photograph of the board every 1,000 marks, so new people joining the meeting can catch up from the photo instead of replaying every mark since the document was created.

**The single-request walkthrough:** Alice and Bob are both editing a document at revision 42 (each edit increments a global revision counter). The current text is "hello world". Alice types "X" at position 5 (between "hello" and " world"). Bob simultaneously deletes the first 5 characters ("hello"). Both clients send their edits to the collaboration server with `base_rev=42`. Bob's delete arrives first and lands as revision 43 — the document is now " world". Then Alice's insert `insert("X", 5)` arrives, but the server is already at rev 43. Using OT (Operational Transformation — a set of rules for adjusting an edit against edits it didn't see), the server transforms Alice's operation: `delete(0, 5)` removed 5 characters before position 5, so Alice's insert position shifts from 5 to 0. The server applies `insert("X", 0)` as rev 44 — the document is now "X world". Both Alice's and Bob's clients receive the other's transformed operation and end up with "X world." This is convergence: different orders of arrival produce the same final state.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Collaboration server (per-doc actor)** — a single-threaded process pinned to one document, receiving all edit operations for it in sequence. By funneling everything through one process, the server turns a hard distributed-concurrency problem into a simple ordered queue. One actor per hot document; the coordination service assigns each document to one server.
- **OT (Operational Transformation)** — the math that makes concurrent edits converge. When two clients edit the same document from the same base revision, the server transforms each operation against the ones it did not see. The transform function `T(opA, opB)` produces a new opA that, applied after opB, yields the same result as applying opB's transform against opA first. Google Docs uses OT.
- **CRDT (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Type)** — an alternative to OT. Instead of transforming operations, every character gets a globally unique ID. Concurrent inserts are ordered by ID, concurrent deletes become "tombstones" (a marker that a character is deleted without removing its ID from the list). Merging two divergent histories is a mathematical set-union — always produces the same result regardless of order. Yjs, Automerge, and Notion use CRDT-based approaches. The cost: each character carries 16-32 bytes of ID metadata, bloating a 100KB document to 2-3MB in memory.
- **Op log (append-only per document)** — every edit is persisted to a log before being broadcast. This is the durability guarantee: if the collaboration server crashes after accepting an edit, the log contains the edit and the replacement server can replay from it.
- **Snapshots (object store)** — a periodic full copy of the document, taken every N operations (e.g., every 1,000 edits) or every M minutes. When a user opens a document, the system fetches the latest snapshot plus any operations after it, instead of replaying years of history. Without snapshots, opening a 5-year-old active document could mean replaying 800,000 operations.
- **Presence channel (ephemeral)** — cursor positions and user avatars. Sent over the same WebSocket but never written to the op log — they are ephemeral. Throttled to ~10 updates per second per client so a user dragging a selection doesn't flood the broadcast.

**The thing that makes it hard:** Alice is working on a document offline on a 4-hour flight. She accumulates 200 local edit operations. Her collaborator, Bob, makes 1,500 edits while she's in the air. When Alice reconnects, the collaboration server must transform all 200 of Alice's offline edits against all 1,500 of Bob's edits — a potentially enormous amount of computation. With OT this is a long sequence of pairwise transforms (each of Alice's 200 ops is transformed against each of the 1,500 ops it missed, in order). With CRDTs it is simpler — set-union of all operations — but the resulting document state may contain re-appeared deleted paragraphs and shifted text that looks jarring even if it is mathematically correct. Either way, very long offline gaps create merge results that may surprise the user.

**Why the standard solution works:** the single pinned actor per document converts the hardest part of the problem — concurrent distributed state — into a local problem. The server is the canonical arbiter of revision order. Clients apply edits optimistically to their local view (so typing feels instant), then the server's response tells them the canonical revision and any transforms they need to apply. The combination of a fast optimistic local apply plus a server-side canonical ordering is why Google Docs feels as responsive as a local editor. Snapshots plus an op log make document loading practical at any history depth.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- WebSocket connection from every editing client to the collaboration server for the document's assigned region. One per-doc actor (Erlang/Go goroutine or lightweight thread), coordination service (etcd) tracks which server owns which doc.
- Op log persisted to a replicated append-only store (Kafka or a Cassandra wide-column table partitioned by doc_id) before any broadcast. Snapshots written to S3 every 1,000 ops.
- Core server receive loop:
  ```
  on receive(client_op, base_rev):
    gap_ops = op_log.read(base_rev, current_rev)
    transformed_op = ot.transform(client_op, gap_ops)
    op_log.append(transformed_op)          # durable first
    current_rev += 1
    broadcast(transformed_op, current_rev) # then broadcast
    return ack(current_rev)
  ```
- Presence: forward cursor updates to all other clients without persisting. Reject presence frames that exceed 10/second per client.
#### Clarifying Questions
- Plain text or rich text?
- Conflict resolution model?
- Offline editing?
- Comments + suggestions?
- Max concurrent editors per doc?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** real-time collaborative edit, presence (cursors), comments, version history, offline edit + sync
- **NFR:** sub-200ms keystroke propagation, eventual convergence, conflict-free under concurrent edits

#### Scale Estimate
- **Doc creation rate:** Google Workspace ~3B users (public, 2023). Assume ~3% create a doc per day (consumers >> creators) → ~100M docs created/day. Active corpus = creation × retention curve. Workspace launched 2006 but most active docs are recent → ~10B docs in active hot corpus, older tier to cold.
- **Concurrent editors per doc:** observed Google Docs distribution — p50 = 1 (solo editing dominant), p99 ~20 (typical team meeting / shared notes), hot ceiling ~50 enforced as broadcast-fanout cap. 1k+ editors only on viral shared docs (throttled).
- **Ops/s on hot docs:** avg keystroke rate per active editor ~3 keystrokes/s (typing prose). 50 editors × 3 = ~150 ops/s baseline; simultaneous bullet-point editing bursts reach ~10k/s on the hottest doc, all collapsed to one server thread.
- **Doc size:** ~2k words × ~5 chars + ~30% formatting/structure overhead ≈ ~10KB serialised. Long-tail: design docs / research papers ~50-500KB; legal contracts / outlier specs up to ~5MB.
- **Storage:** 10B docs × 10KB avg = ~100TB raw snapshots; RF=3 → ~300TB. p95 ~100KB and p99 ~1MB skew real footprint ~10x → ~3PB realistic; templates/boilerplate dedupe recovers ~20%.
- **Op log volume:** ~10M docs/day enter active editing (~10% of created docs see >1 editing session). Avg session ~500 ops (10 min × ~0.83 keystrokes/s with idle gaps). Op size ~200B (op type 4B + path ~32B + offset 4B + content ~100B + author 16B + rev 8B + ts 8B + padding ~28B). 10M × 500 × 200B = ~1TB/day raw; post-snapshot truncation keeps hot tail ~7d → ~7TB live.
- **OT/CRDT log retention:** Google Docs surfaces ~1yr revision history. Strategy: snapshot every N=1000 ops + retain deltas. ~1TB/day × 5x columnar compression = ~200GB/day → ~70TB/yr.
- **Edit ops/sec sustained fleet-wide:** assume 100M MAU concurrently active in any 5-min window; ~5% are *editing* (vs reading / in-meeting) at any given moment = 5M concurrent editors. 5M × ~3 keystrokes/s = ~15M ops/s peak fleet-wide; partitioned by doc_id across thousands of collab servers → per-shard load = 15M ÷ N_shards.

#### API Contract
```
WebSocket per editing session:
  → { type: op, doc_id, ops: [...], rev: int }
  ← { type: ack, server_rev: int }
  ← { type: remote_op, ops, by, rev }
  ← { type: presence, user, cursor_pos }

REST:
  GET /doc/{id}                → snapshot + version
  GET /doc/{id}/history?cursor → revisions
```

#### High-Level Design
```
   Client A                     Client B
      │                            │
      │ ops + rev                  │ ops + rev
      ↓                            ↓
   ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
   │    Collaboration Server          │
   │                                  │
   │   - Per-doc actor / single thread│
   │   - Receives ops                 │
   │   - Transforms (OT) or merges    │
   │     (CRDT)                       │
   │   - Broadcasts to all clients    │
   │   - Persists to storage          │
   └─────────────┬────────────────────┘
                 ↓
        Persistent Document Store
        (snapshots + op log)
                 ↓
        Snapshot Service
        (periodic compaction)
```

**How to read the diagram:** clients do not send whole documents back and forth. They send small edit operations, and a collaboration service orders or transforms those operations before broadcasting them back out.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** the document is shared, but every user is editing from a slightly different local state. The architecture exists to turn those diverging local edits back into one coherent shared sequence without making the editor feel laggy.

**What this layout buys you:** low-bandwidth collaboration and a plausible path to convergence under concurrency. The tradeoff is that operational semantics, not storage, become the hardest part of the system.

#### Data Model
- **documents** (KV store): `(doc_id, snapshot_blob, current_rev, last_modified)`
- **operations** (append-only log per doc): `(doc_id, rev, client_id, ops_json, ts)`
- **snapshots** (object store): periodic full-document snapshots for fast load

#### Detailed Design
**Two main approaches:**

**1. Operational Transformation (OT) — Google Docs uses this**
```
Client edits at rev R, sends ops + R
Server already at rev R+5

Server transforms client's ops against the 5 ops the client missed,
producing equivalent ops applied at rev R+5.

Then broadcasts transformed ops to all clients.
```

Pros: works on plain text trees; simple per-op
Cons: transformation function is hard to get right; Google has decades of bug fixes

**2. CRDTs (Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types)**
```
Each character has a unique global ID (e.g. (lamport_clock, client_id))
Operations include character IDs

Insert: new char ID derived from neighbors
Delete: tombstone the ID

Merging two divergent histories = set union; mathematical convergence guarantee.
```

Pros: provably correct under concurrent edits; no central authority needed
Cons: metadata overhead per character; trickier rich text

**Why operational transformation works.** Concurrent edits diverge because two clients act on the same base revision in parallel: A inserts "x" at position 10 in rev R while B deletes 5 chars at position 5 in rev R. If both ops apply naively at rev R+2, the document state depends on order — non-convergent. OT solves this by transforming each op against the ops it didn't see: when A's op arrives at the server (already at rev R+1 because B's delete landed first), the server transforms `insert("x", 10)` against `delete(5, 5)`, producing `insert("x", 5)` — same *user intent*, adjusted positions. The transform function `T(opA, opB) → opA'` is designed so applying `opA` then `T(opB, opA)` yields the same state as applying `opB` then `T(opA, opB)` (the TP1/TP2 transformation properties). This convergence is what makes "what I see equals what you see" hold across the fleet despite concurrent typing.

**Why CRDTs work differently.** CRDTs sidestep transformation by giving every character a globally unique, ordered ID (e.g. `(client_id, lamport_timestamp)` plus a fractional position derived from neighbors). An insert is "new char with ID I, between IDs L and R"; a delete is "tombstone ID I." Merging two divergent histories is then a set-union: take every insert from both, every tombstone from both, sort by ID — same result regardless of merge order, mathematically. No central server needed for correctness (though one helps for storage and presence). The cost is per-character metadata: each character carries 16-32 bytes of ID, so a 100KB doc becomes 2-3MB on the wire and in memory unless aggressively compressed.

**Operation flow.** Local edit applies optimistically to the client's view (sub-frame latency, no perceived lag), then sends `{ops, base_rev}` over WebSocket. Server validates `base_rev`, transforms ops against the gap between `base_rev` and `current_rev`, applies, increments `current_rev`, persists to op log (sync write), broadcasts the transformed ops to every other connected client. Each client applies remote ops on top of its local view, transforming any pending unsent local ops against the incoming remote op. Concrete example: rev=42, A types "x" at position 10, server already at rev=44 because B inserted 2 chars earlier in the doc; server transforms A's op to position 12, persists at rev=45, broadcasts.

**Plain text vs rich text.** We support rich text (paragraphs, bold/italic, lists, headings, embedded images and tables), not just plain text. The document is a tree of styled nodes, not a flat character buffer; ops include `insert_text(path, offset, text)`, `delete_range(path_start, path_end)`, `apply_mark(range, mark, value)`, and structural ops like `split_paragraph` / `wrap_in(list, range)`. Rich-text OT transforms are strictly harder than plain-text OT (mark conflicts: A bolds a range while B italicises an overlapping range — both should compose, so marks are stored as independent attribute sets, not interleaved tokens). For the CRDT alternative, embedded objects (images, tables) are referenced by ID and stored as separate sub-CRDTs so concurrent edits inside a table cell don't entangle with concurrent edits to the surrounding paragraph. Plain-text-only would simplify the OT/CRDT layer materially, but it doesn't match the actual product surface.

**Presence (cursors / selections).** Cursor positions are sent on the same WebSocket but tagged ephemeral — server forwards to other clients, never persists. Anchor cursor positions to character IDs (CRDT) or revision-relative offsets (OT) so a remote cursor stays glued to its character even as text shifts around it. Throttle to ~10Hz per client to avoid flooding the broadcast on rapid mouse drags. Concrete example: B's cursor sits before character ID `xyz`; A inserts text before `xyz`; B's cursor still appears in the right *visual* place because it's anchored to the ID, not absolute position.

**Offline editing.** Client buffers local ops and bumps a local rev counter starting from `last_known_server_rev`. On reconnect, the client sends the entire buffer; server transforms each op in the buffer sequentially against everything that happened while the client was disconnected (potentially thousands of ops). With CRDTs this is a clean set-union; with OT it's a long sequence of pairwise transforms — the server caps this work, falling back to "your edits couldn't auto-merge, please review" if the gap is too large. Concrete example: a user edits offline on a flight for 4 hours, accumulating 200 ops; on reconnect they're transformed against 1500 ops from collaborators; final merged state is broadcast back to all parties.

**Snapshots.** A separate compaction service reads the op log and writes a full document snapshot to object store every N ops (e.g. 1000) or every M minutes. Doc load = fetch latest snapshot + tail any ops since `snapshot_rev`. Without snapshots, opening a 5-year-old doc would require replaying millions of ops. Op log GC runs after snapshot is durable: keep `last_snapshot_rev - safety_buffer` of op history online, archive older to cold storage for version history. Concrete example: a 50KB design doc with 800k historic ops loads in ~200ms by fetching one 50KB snapshot blob plus the ~50 ops since the last snapshot, instead of replaying 800k ops at 100k ops/sec = 8s.

**OT operation transformation across two concurrent edits.** The classic worked example: clients A and B both have rev=R = "hello world". A inserts "X" at position 5 (between "hello" and " world"); B simultaneously deletes 5 chars at position 0 ("hello"). Without transformation, applying A then B yields "X world" but applying B then A yields " worldX" — divergent. OT transforms each op against the gap it didn't see: when B's delete arrives first and lands as rev R+1, the server transforms A's `insert("X", 5)` against `delete(0, 5)` to get `insert("X", 0)`, applies as rev R+2 = "X world". The transform function `T` must satisfy TP1 (`apply(opA, T(opB, opA)) == apply(opB, T(opA, opB))`) for two-party convergence; TP2 is the harder property required for arbitrary N-way concurrent edits and is famously where OT implementations break. *[Source: Wave/Etherpad OT papers]*

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Client A
    participant S as Server (rev=R)
    participant B as Client B
    A->>S: insert("X", 5) at base=R
    B->>S: delete(0, 5) at base=R
    Note over S: B arrives first
    S->>S: apply delete → rev=R+1 ("hello "→" ")
    S-->>B: ack rev=R+1
    S-->>A: remote_op delete(0,5)
    Note over S: A's op arrives at R+1<br/>transform insert("X",5)<br/>against delete(0,5)<br/>→ insert("X",0)
    S->>S: apply transformed → rev=R+2 ("X world")
    S-->>A: ack rev=R+2 (transformed)
    S-->>B: remote_op insert("X",0)
    Note over A,B: both converge to "X world"
```

**OT vs CRDT vs hybrid (Figma).** Three real-world strategies, three different trade-off curves. *OT (Wave / Etherpad / Google Docs)* keeps a clean linear character buffer — no per-character metadata — but requires a central server to assign canonical ordering and runs a transform function whose correctness is notoriously hard at N>2 concurrent edits. *CRDT (Yjs / Automerge / Riak text)* gives every character a globally unique ID derived from neighbours (RGA, LSEQ, fractional indexing); merge is set-union, no central server required, peer-to-peer works. The cost is per-character metadata: a 100KB doc carries 2-3MB of IDs unless GC'd via tombstone collapse. *Figma's hybrid* models the doc as `Map<ObjectID, Map<Property, Value>>` — properties are last-writer-wins (server picks a canonical order), tree structure uses *fractional indexing* on parent-link properties so reordering is conflict-free, and cycles in concurrent reparents (A→B and B→A) are server-rejected. Critically, Figma does *not* merge concurrent edits to the same property (e.g. simultaneous text edits on a label) — last writer wins, the loser gets overwritten. This works because Figma's doc is mostly structured shapes, not free text. *[Source: Figma multiplayer blog, Yjs docs]*

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OT["OT (Google Docs)"]
        OT1[Linear char buffer]
        OT2[Server transforms<br/>concurrent ops]
        OT3[No per-char metadata]
        OT4[Hard to get right<br/>at N>2 editors]
    end
    subgraph CRDT["CRDT (Yjs/Automerge)"]
        CR1[Per-char unique ID]
        CR2[Merge = set union]
        CR3[Provably convergent]
        CR4[Metadata bloat<br/>2-3x doc size]
    end
    subgraph FIG["Figma Hybrid"]
        FG1["Map ObjectID -> Map Property -> Value"]
        FG2[LWW per property]
        FG3[Fractional indexing<br/>for tree position]
        FG4[Server rejects cycles]
    end
```

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: OT vs CRDTs — which would you actually pick today?**
CRDTs for a greenfield collaborative editor: provably convergent, no central authority, easier offline. Google stuck with OT because its codebase predates production-ready CRDT libraries. *If pushed:* OT still wins on per-character metadata overhead and for rich-text trees; large docs with millions of characters can balloon under naive CRDT representations (use RGA/Yjs-style tombstone GC to reclaim).

**Q: 1000 concurrent editors on one doc — what happens?**
Server becomes the broadcast bottleneck and ops queue grows faster than clients can apply. Cap concurrent editors (Google caps at ~50 active) and downgrade extras to view-only or suggestion mode. *If pushed:* shard the doc by section (each section a sub-CRDT) so editors on different sections don't fan out to each other; or move to a peer-to-peer gossip topology where the server is just durable storage.

**Q: A user edits offline for a week, then reconnects — what could go wrong?**
With CRDTs the merge is mathematically defined but the UX may be jarring (deleted paragraphs reappear, ordering shifts). With OT a week of transformation may be intractable. *If pushed:* show a "rebase preview" UI letting the user review their offline changes against current state before committing; cap offline buffer size and force a manual sync past the threshold.

**Q: How do you load a 10MB doc with 5M operations of history fast?**
Periodic snapshots to S3 (every N ops or every M minutes) + GC the op log behind the snapshot. Client loads snapshot, then tails ops since snapshot version. *If pushed:* incremental snapshots (delta from previous snapshot) so storage cost is bounded; lazy-load history on demand for "see who edited" features rather than streaming the full op log.

**Q: How do you handle authorisation revocation mid-edit?**
On revoke, the access service publishes to the doc's WS channel; server closes that user's connection and rejects their next op submission. Client buffer is discarded. *If pushed:* server-side ACL check on every op (not just on connect) so a stale token can't sneak ops in; row-level audit log of "user X edited at rev Y" for compliance.

**Q: Comments and suggestions — same channel as edits or separate?**
Separate. Comments are anchored to character ranges (CRDT IDs) but stored independently — this lets you edit a paragraph without losing the comment thread on it. *If pushed:* anchor migration is the hard part — when a comment's range is deleted, you need a policy (orphan it, attach to nearest surviving char, or resolve automatically); each has trade-offs that should be configurable per workspace.

**Q: Why does Figma get away without OT or CRDT for text?**
Figma's doc isn't free-form prose — it's a tree of structured objects with discrete properties (position, fill, stroke, text-content). Concurrent edits to *different* properties on the same object never conflict (independent slots). Concurrent edits to the *same* property are last-writer-wins, accepting that simultaneous typing on a label loses one user's input. This trade is fine for design files because simultaneous text editing on the same label is rare; for prose docs (Google Docs, Notion blocks) it would feel broken. *If pushed:* Figma uses fractional indexing for sibling order in arrays, which is a CRDT-flavoured technique; the system is "CRDT-inspired but not pure CRDT" — server is still the canonical ordering authority.

**Q: Yjs vs Automerge — both are CRDTs, what's the difference?**
Yjs uses a doubly-linked-list CRDT (YATA) optimised for text; very fast incremental updates, smaller wire format, lazy GC. Automerge uses an immutable doc-as-history model — every state is reachable, easy to fork/merge git-style — but slower for high-frequency text edits. *If pushed:* Yjs for live collaborative text (Notion uses Yjs), Automerge for offline-first apps where merging weeks of divergence is the dominant operation; the two libraries are converging in capability but the data-structure choice still matters for performance.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Hot doc** — a single doc with 1000s of editors saturates the pinned actor's CPU and the per-client WebSocket fan-out scales as N². Cap concurrent editors (Google caps ~50 active), downgrade extras to suggestion or view mode; for legitimate multi-hundred sessions, shard the doc by section (each section a sub-CRDT/sub-actor) so editors in different sections don't fan out to each other.
- **Op log size** — every keystroke is a row in the op log; a long-lived doc accumulates millions, slowing loads and burning storage. Periodic snapshot + GC keeps online op log bounded to last_snapshot_rev minus safety buffer; older ops archived columnar for version-history queries on demand.
- **Latency on large doc** — a 5MB doc with deep history takes seconds to deserialize. Load latest snapshot only; lazy-load history (revision list, blame) on user request; stream the snapshot in chunks if rich-text tree is large so the editor renders as soon as the visible viewport is in.
- **Conflicting edits with strong intent** — two users overwrite the same paragraph with different content; OT/CRDT will produce a merged sequence that includes both (interleaved or duplicated) which can read as garbage. Surface a conflict marker UI so the user sees both versions and chooses; for suggestions/track-changes, store as separate change-objects rather than collapsing into the doc state.
- **Server crash mid-broadcast** — some clients received the latest op, others didn't, so client-side revs diverge. On reconnect, clients send their `last_received_rev`; server replays missing ops from the op log (which was persisted before broadcast) so all clients converge to the same state; brief read-only window during failover.
- **CRDT metadata bloat** — naive RGA/LSEQ representations carry 16-32 bytes of ID per character, so a 100KB doc balloons to 2-3MB on the wire and in client RAM. Aggressive tombstone GC (Yjs runs a mark-and-sweep over deleted IDs once consensus on deletion is reached); structural compression on the wire (run-length encoding of sequential IDs from the same client); fall back to OT for purely text-heavy surfaces if client RAM is the constraint.
- **Fractional-index exhaustion (Figma-style)** — repeatedly inserting between two siblings doubles the precision needed for the fractional position; after enough inserts the float underflows. Use rational-fraction representations or rebalance the array periodically (assign fresh evenly-spaced indices in a quiet window); detect via index-precision-bits SLO and trigger background rebalance.


#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per-doc actor | Pinned actor crashes mid-broadcast; some clients have ops, others don't | Heartbeat from actor to coordination service; client revs diverge on reconnect | Actor restart re-reads op log up to `current_rev`, reasserts pin; clients send `last_received_rev` and server replays missing ops; brief read-only window (~seconds) during failover |
| WebSocket | Client connection drops mid-edit; buffered local ops haven't reached server | Heartbeat ping/pong timeout; client side socket close | Client buffers locally with `last_known_server_rev`, retries connection with exponential backoff, replays buffer transformed against gap on reconnect |
| Op log store | Sync write fails (disk full, replica lag) — op acked client-side but not durable | Write latency p99 alert; replication-lag SLO; ack-without-fsync detector | Synchronous write to majority quorum before broadcast; on failure, reject the op and surface "saving..." UI; never broadcast an op that hasn't been durably persisted |
| Snapshot service | Snapshot writer crashes mid-write — partial blob in object store | Snapshot manifest checks SHA before promoting `latest`; orphan-blob cleaner | Write snapshot to a temporary key, verify hash, atomically rename to `latest`; old snapshots retained for one cycle so a corrupt new snapshot can be rolled back |
| Auth / ACL | Token revoked while user has an open session | Auth-service revocation event published to doc's WS channel | Server-side ACL check on every op (not just on connect) so a stale token can't sneak ops in; close offending socket; row-level audit of "user X edited at rev Y" |
| Presence channel | Cursor flood from a malicious or buggy client | Per-client message-rate alert | Throttle presence frames to ~10Hz per client; drop excess at the WS edge before they hit the actor |
| Rich-text op transform | OT TP2 violation on N>2 concurrent rich-text edits — divergence between clients | Periodic checksum: server hashes its rev-R state, broadcasts; clients flag mismatch | Catch via the checksum, force the offending client to reload from latest snapshot; long-term fix is a CRDT for hard cases (Yjs-style RGA) |
| CRDT metadata | Tombstone-collapse GC fails to converge across replicas; metadata grows unbounded | RAM-per-doc SLO breach; wire-size growth | Mark-and-sweep over deleted IDs only after consensus on deletion is reached; structural compression (RLE on sequential same-client IDs); cap doc-RAM and force snapshot+truncate |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Op apply latency** (client-receives-ack − client-sent-op) — SLO p99 < 200ms; this is the felt-latency users notice as keystroke lag.
- **Server-rev gap** (current_rev − client's last_known_rev for active editors) — SLO p99 < 50 ops; gap growth means broadcast is back-pressured.
- **Active editors per doc** — distribution and p99; cap enforcement metric, also the input to "downgrade extras to view-only" logic for hot docs.
- **Op log write latency** (sync-fsync time on the durable log) — SLO p99 < 20ms; a primary signal for storage health since every op blocks on this.
- **Snapshot freshness** (current_rev − last_snapshot_rev) — SLO < N ops or < M minutes; if snapshots fall behind, doc-load time spikes.
- **Convergence checksum mismatches per hour** — hard alert; any drift is an OT bug or a corrupted client and must be investigated.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** doc actors are pinned to one home region; clients in remote regions connect through a regional WS edge that proxies to the home region (low overhead — WebSocket frames forwarded). Op log + snapshots replicate sync within a metro AZ-pair, async to remote regions for read-only standby.
- **RTO:** ~10-30 seconds for actor failover within a region (op-log replay from last snapshot + reassert pin). Cross-region failover is an operator decision: ~5-10 minutes including DNS/TLS cutover.
- **RPO:** ~0s within a region (sync majority write before broadcast). Cross-region: up to a few seconds of unreplicated ops (async lag); a forced cross-region failover may surface as "your last few keystrokes were lost — please review" because we won't broadcast acks ahead of durability.
- **Failover cadence:** automatic actor restart on host loss; cross-region game-days quarterly. Snapshot stores are replicated to standby regions continuously so cold-start in a new region is bounded by op-log delta replay.
- **Cross-region cost:** the dominant egress is op-log streaming (~7TB/day live tail) plus snapshot replication (~200GB/day delta). WS proxy traffic is small compared to the op stream.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **"OT and CRDT are interchangeable" — wrong.** They have different consistency stories: OT requires a central server to assign canonical ordering and runs a transform function (TP1/TP2) whose correctness is notoriously hard at N>2 concurrent edits; CRDTs give every character a globally unique ID so merge is set-union and works peer-to-peer, at the cost of per-character metadata. Pick one based on the surface (free-form prose vs structured shapes) and live with its tradeoffs — don't claim either gives you the other's properties.
- **"Merge always converges, so we don't need conflict UX" — wrong.** OT/CRDT guarantee mathematical convergence to *some* state, not the state the user wanted. Concurrent overwrite of the same paragraph produces an interleaved or deduped sequence that often reads as garbage; you still need a conflict marker UI for hard cases (or a track-changes/suggestion mode) so the user can resolve.
- **No throttling on presence frames.** A user dragging selection across a doc easily produces hundreds of cursor updates per second; broadcasting all of them to all peers melts the actor's CPU on hot docs. Throttle to ~10Hz per client at the WS edge.
- **Loading the full op log on doc open.** Without periodic snapshots + GC, opening a 5-year-old doc requires replaying millions of ops. Snapshot every N ops (or M minutes) to object store; doc load = latest snapshot + delta tail.
- **Server-side ACL only on connect.** Token revocation must take effect mid-session — check ACL on every op, not just at handshake, otherwise a stale token continues to write after revocation.
