- **Treating offline edits as just "another stream of ops."** Replaying a week of offline ops against a week of remote ops can be intractable for OT and confusing for CRDT (deleted paragraphs reappear). Need a "rebase preview" UI past a configurable gap threshold so the user can review and confirm.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **"Send operations, not document blobs"** is the first sentence to say. Interviewers want to hear op-based replication immediately because that is the conceptual shift from a normal CRUD editor.
- **Pick OT vs CRDT deliberately.** OT is still the cleanest story for Google Docs-style centrally coordinated text editing; CRDT is the stronger offline / peer-to-peer story. State why you picked one and what trade you are accepting.
- **A single pinned actor per hot doc keeps the happy path simple.** It serialises incoming ops, assigns the canonical revision, and turns a hard distributed-consistency problem into a local ordered-log problem for that document.
- **Persistence order matters:** write the op durably before broadcast. That shows you have thought about failover and replay, not just the live WebSocket path.
- **Presence is ephemeral, content is durable.** Cursors, selections, and typing indicators should never be in the same durability path as document edits.
- **Snapshots are what make old docs load fast.** Mention "latest snapshot + op-log tail" explicitly; otherwise the design sounds fine until the doc has years of history.

### 38. Design Tinder (Dating / Match)
#### Problem
Show users a feed of potential matches, capture swipes, create matches when both swipe right, enable chat afterward.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a speed-dating event where you rotate through hundreds of people in a few minutes, but a very smart organizer pre-screens who you see based on your location, preferences, and behavioral patterns — so you only meet people who plausibly match your criteria. When you hold up a "yes" paddle and the other person has also held up a "yes" paddle for you (even at different times), the organizer connects you and opens a private conversation room. The organizer never tells anyone who flashed a "yes" before a match is confirmed — privacy of unreciprocated interest is a core rule.

**The single-request walkthrough:** a user in London opens the Tinder app. The Feed Service receives a `GET /feed` request tagged with the user's last-known GPS coordinates. Stage 1 — geo query: the service queries the geo index (a Redis GEORADIUS command or S2 spatial cells — a way of dividing the globe into a hierarchy of cells, each identifiable by a compact ID) for all users within 10km, returning ~10,000 candidate user IDs. Stage 2 — preference filter: age band, gender, online-recency — trims to ~3,000. Stage 3 — exclusion via Bloom filter: a Bloom filter is a compact data structure (think: a tightly packed bit-array) that can quickly answer "have I probably seen/swiped this person before?" with a tiny false-positive rate. It says "no" for ~1,500 unswiped candidates; it occasionally says "yes" for someone not actually swiped (false positive, ~1% rate) — a harmless miss. Stage 4 — ML ranker: the remaining 1,500 candidates are scored by a model that predicts mutual interest (based on photo-aesthetic similarity, shared interest tags, behavioral patterns). Top 20 are returned, the whole pipeline taking ~250ms.

When the user right-swipes on candidate B: the Swipe Service records `(user_A, user_B, right, timestamp)` in a wide-column store (partitioned by swiper_id for write speed). It then performs a reciprocal lookup: "has user B previously right-swiped user A?" — this is a read against the reverse-lookup index (same swipes table, but also indexed by `target_id`). If yes: create a match record, notify both users via push, unlock the chat room. If no: just record the swipe silently. User B never knows User A swiped right unless B also swipes right.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Geo index (Redis + GEORADIUS or S2 spatial cells)** — stores each user's location as a coordinate that supports radius queries. When a user moves significantly, their location updates in the index. Tinder uses country-level sharding (a Redis cluster per country) so a query for "users within 10km of this London coordinate" never touches the US shard.
- **Bloom filter (per user, cached at edge)** — a compact bit array that answers "has user A swiped on user B before?" without storing every swipe pair in hot memory. A 10MB filter can track ~1 million swipes with 1% false-positive rate. Loaded into the feed server's memory at session start so exclusion checks are local, not a database round-trip.
- **Swipes table (wide-column store, e.g. Cassandra)** — records every swipe: `(swiper_id, target_id, direction, timestamp)`. Also has a reverse-lookup index by `target_id` for the match-check query. Partitioned by swiper_id so writes are fast (Cassandra excels at write-heavy, append-only workloads).
- **ML ranker (two-tower model)** — scores each of the ~1,500 candidates for compatibility. A two-tower model precomputes a 128-dimensional "embedding" vector (a mathematical fingerprint of preferences) for every candidate offline and stores it. At request time, it computes only the current user's embedding and does a fast dot-product score against each of the 1,500 candidates. This is orders of magnitude faster than running a full neural network for every (user, candidate) pair.
- **Match Service** — when a reciprocal right-swipe is detected, creates a match record keyed on the canonical pair `(min(user_id_A, user_id_B), max(user_id_A, user_id_B))`. Two simultaneous right-swipes are handled by idempotent insert — the second write is a no-op on the same row, so only one match is created.

**The thing that makes it hard:** a celebrity or very attractive profile gets right-swiped by millions of users. Every right-swipe writes a row to the swipes table's reverse-lookup index — keyed by `target_id`. One enormously popular user turns their `target_id` row into a write hotspot with thousands of writes per second hitting the same partition. The database node holding that partition saturates its write throughput. Meanwhile, that user's profile appears in millions of feeds simultaneously — flooding their experience with matches they cannot meaningfully act on.

**Why the standard solution works:** two mitigations in tandem. First, sub-shard the reverse-lookup index by `(target_id, hash(swiper_id) % N)` — spreading the writes for one hot user across N database partitions instead of one. Second, impose an exposure budget per profile per day: once a celebrity's profile has been shown in, say, 50,000 feeds today, the ranker's score multiplier for that profile drops to near zero. This stops the celebrity's inbox from being a firehose and prevents a single hot profile from distorting the feed for entire cities. Both solutions accept a small trade: the sub-shard adds a fan-out on the reciprocal lookup (now you check N shards instead of 1), and the exposure cap means some interested users never see the celebrity — but a match they cannot respond to is no match at all.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Redis Cluster (sharded by country) for the geo index. Cassandra for swipes (high write throughput, append-friendly). PostgreSQL for users, matches metadata. Bloom filters in Redis or as in-process LRU per session.
- Feed hot path pseudocode:
  ```
  candidates = geo_index.radius(user.lat, user.lng, radius_km, limit=10000)
  candidates = filter(candidates, user.prefs)
  candidates = [c for c in candidates if not bloom.possibly_swiped(user_id, c)]
  scores = ranker.dot_product(user_embedding, candidate_embeddings[candidates])
  return top_k(candidates, scores, k=20)
  ```
- Swipe hot path: write to Cassandra (partition by swiper_id) → read reverse-index for target_id → if reciprocal, `INSERT INTO matches (...) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` → publish match event.
#### Clarifying Questions
- Distance / age / preference filters?
- "Super-likes", boosts, premium features?
- Image-only or also video?
- Who-liked-you visibility (premium)?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** profile, swipe (left/right), match (mutual right), chat after match, filters, recommendations
- **NFR:** fast feed (<300ms), accurate matching, privacy (don't leak who swiped on you)

#### Scale Estimate
- **MAU:** Tinder reports ~75M MAU (Match Group earnings); use ~50M as conservative active-cohort default. 1.6B swipes/day is Tinder's public stat. Swipes/s = 1.6B ÷ 86400 ≈ ~18.5k/s steady → round to 20k/s. Evening peak (US prime + EU evening overlap, ~3x daily avg in a 4hr window): 20k × 3 ≈ ~60k/s.
- **Per-user swipe rate:** 1.6B ÷ 50M = ~32 swipes/user/day (matches reported ~30-50 swipes/session, ~1 session/day for active users).
- **Match rate:** ~5% of right-swipes mutual (Tinder public). Right-swipes ≈ ~50% of all swipes → 1.6B × 0.5 × 0.05 = 40M; round up to ~80M/day to account for super-likes inflating reciprocal rate.
- **Profile size:** ~50KB metadata (bio ~1KB + 50 attribute fields × ~100B + activity blob ~40KB for Spotify/Insta integration). 6 photos × ~200KB (multi-res ladder: 300px ~30KB + 600px ~80KB + 1200px ~300KB averaged ~200KB) → ~1.2MB/user. 50M × 1.2MB = ~60TB media; RF=3 → ~180TB.
- **Profile records:** ~5KB (user_id 16B + demographics ~200B + prefs JSON ~1KB + last_active 8B + geo ~32B + verification flags ~100B + premium state ~200B + indexed-field padding ~3KB) → 50M × 5KB = ~250GB; RF=3 → ~750GB.
- **Swipe events:** ~80B/swipe (swiper_id 16B + target_id 16B + direction 1B + ts 8B + session_id 16B + geo-at-swipe ~16B + padding ~7B). 1.6B/day × 80B = ~130GB/day raw; RF=3 → ~390GB/day.
- **Hot retention** (12mo — bloom + reciprocal-match window; older swipes can't produce a match anyway since the other party drifted away): 1.6B × 365 × 80B = ~46TB. Older tombstoned; bloom resets on dormant users.
- **Bloom filter per user:** ceiling ~500k lifetime swipes (32/day × 365 × ~5y heavy-user lifecycle = ~60k typical; 500k is power-user outlier). At 1% FPR → ~9.6 bits/element → 500k × 9.6 / 8 ≈ ~600KB per power-user. Most users far smaller (~50KB typical). 50M × ~10% concurrently active ≈ 5M concurrent × avg ~600KB = ~3TB hot bloom set at edge; cold blooms in main cache.
- **Match records:** ~100B (match_id 16B + user_a 16B + user_b 16B + created_at 8B + state 4B + last_msg_at 8B + flags 4B + padding ~28B). 80M/day × 100B = ~8GB/day; cumulative ~3TB/yr.
- **Chat backlog:** see chat-system pattern (#9). 80M matches × ~10% actively chatting × ~20 msgs/day × ~300B/msg ≈ ~50GB/day messages.

#### API Contract
```
GET  /feed             → [{user_id, photos[], bio, distance, ...}]
POST /swipe            body: { target_id, direction }    → { matched: bool, match_id? }
GET  /matches          → list
WS   /chat/{match_id}  ← messages (like #9)
```

#### High-Level Design
```
                   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
       Client ────→│  Feed Service               │
                   │                             │
                   │  1. Geo query (within Xkm)  │
                   │  2. Filter by preferences   │
                   │     (age, gender, etc.)     │
                   │  3. Exclude already-swiped  │
                   │     (Bloom filter)          │
                   │  4. ML rank (compatibility) │
                   │  5. Return ~20 candidates   │
                   └──────────┬──────────────────┘
                              ↓
                              ↓ swipe
                   ┌─────────────────────────────┐
                   │  Swipe Service              │
                   │  - record swipe             │
                   │  - check if reciprocal      │
                   │     (target swiped this user)│
                   │  - if yes → create match    │
                   │  - notify both              │
                   └──────────┬──────────────────┘
                              ↓
                       Match Service ─→ Chat Service (#9)
```

**How to read the diagram:** one path builds the candidate feed and another path records swipes and detects mutual interest. The feed path narrows and ranks possible matches, while the swipe path performs the actual did-this-become-a-match decision.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** recommendation and matching are related but not identical problems. Showing good candidates requires geo filtering and ranking; creating a match requires cheap reciprocal lookup and privacy-preserving state changes.

**What this layout buys you:** a faster swipe experience and cheaper match detection. The tradeoff is that quality depends heavily on the feed pipeline, not just on the correctness of swipe storage.

#### Data Model
- **users** (transactional store): `(user_id, age, gender, prefs, lat, lng, last_active, ...)`
- **swipes** (wide-column store, partition by swiper):
  `(swiper_id, target_id, direction, ts)` — also a reverse-lookup index for match check
- **matches** (wide-column store): `(match_id, user_a, user_b, created_at, last_msg_ts)`
- **swiped_set** (in-memory cache, bloom filter per user): exclude already-swiped from feed
- **photos** (object store + CDN, multiple resolutions)

#### Detailed Design
**Feed generation:**
```
On feed request:
  1. Geo query: GEORADIUS users:{country} lng lat max_km
  2. Filter: age, gender, online recently
  3. Exclude swiped (Bloom filter check)
  4. Score with ML model (interests, photo features, behavioral signals)
  5. Return diversified top N
```

**Swipe consistency:**
```
On right swipe by A on B:
  - Record swipe (A, B, right)
  - Check: did B swipe right on A? (lookup swipes by B with target A)
    - Yes → atomic: create match (A, B); notify both
    - No → just record; B will see A in feed (potentially boosted)
```

**Privacy.** Who-liked-you is never exposed to free users — surfacing it would invert the entire game and is monetised as a premium feature in many apps. Exact lat/lng never leaves the server; distance is bucketed and rounded ("3 km away," not "2.7km") to defeat trilateration attacks where someone moves around to triangulate a target's home. Block list filters bidirectionally: blocker and blocked never appear in each other's feed, and any open match between them is severed. Concrete example: a stalker can't use a fake account to find their victim because moving location around still gets them a rounded distance band, not a precise GPS fix.

**Matching ML.** Features are mostly behavioral: shared interest tags, mutual-friend overlap (when known via social graph), photo-aesthetic embeddings (CNN on profile pics — clusters the user prefers), response patterns (how quickly do they message after match), age/distance/activity-recency. Trained on positive labels = (right-swipe → match → chat with ≥5 messages exchanged) and negative labels = (left-swipe, or match with no chat). Two-tower architecture is common (user-tower, candidate-tower, dot-product score) because it allows offline-precompute of candidate embeddings; the request path is just a few hundred dot products. Concrete example: model learns user A consistently right-swipes profiles with hiking photos; CNN embedding clusters those, and similar-clustered candidates are surfaced more often.

**Boost / Super-likes.** Boost: boosted user's profile gets a 10x multiplier in the ranker's score for 30 minutes — they appear higher in more feeds in their geo. Super-like: target gets an immediate push notification ("X super-liked you") and the target's next feed surfaces X with a star/halo annotation; conversion-to-match rates run 2-3x higher than passive surfacing. Both are rate-limited by user tier (free users get 1 super-like/day, paid get more) to prevent inflation that would devalue the signal. Concrete example: at 7pm Friday in NYC, a user activates Boost; for the next 30min their profile is in the top-5 of every nearby feed query; they typically see 5-10x usual right-swipes back.

**Photos and short video on profiles.** We support both: 6 photos plus up to 2 short videos per profile (max 10s, looped, no audio by default — same product surface as Tinder Loops / Hinge Reels). Photos are pre-encoded into multiple resolutions (thumbnail 150px, feed 600px, full 1200px) at upload time and pushed to the CDN; videos are transcoded into short MP4 (H.264) and WebM at two bitrates each (~1Mbps cellular, 3Mbps wifi), also CDN-fronted. Feed responses carry asset URLs only — never blobs — so a feed page is ~5KB JSON regardless of media count. Per-asset CV (the "real face" classifier and known-scammer reverse hash) runs on photo upload synchronously and on video keyframes asynchronously; videos that fail moderation get the user a soft-warning and are blocked from feed appearance until re-uploaded. Storage budget per user grows from ~1.2MB (photos only) to ~5MB with two short videos.

**Candidate retrieval pipeline (visual).** The four narrowing stages: each stage trades cardinality reduction for compute cost — the expensive ML pass runs on hundreds, not millions. Approximate stage cardinalities at p50: geo-radius returns ~10k candidates → preference filter trims to ~3k → bloom-exclude leaves ~1.5k unswiped → ML ranker scores all 1.5k → diversification + freshness boost picks top 20. Total p99 latency budget ~300ms; geo and bloom each ~10-30ms, ranker ~100-150ms (the dominant cost), the rest in network/serialisation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User opens app] --> B[Geo radius query<br/>GEORADIUS instr-id<br/>~10k candidates]
    B --> C[Preference filter<br/>age, gender,<br/>activity recency<br/>~3k]
    C --> D[Bloom-filter exclude<br/>already-swiped<br/>~1.5k]
    D --> E[ML ranker<br/>two-tower dot product<br/>user x candidate emb<br/>~1.5k scored]
    E --> F[Diversification<br/>+ freshness boost<br/>+ exposure cap]
    F --> G[Top 20 returned<br/>p99 < 300ms]
```

**ELO-style attractiveness score (deprecated but informative).** Older Tinder used an ELO-derived "desirability score" — every right-swipe on user U raised U's score by a delta proportional to the swiper's score, and every left-swipe lowered it. High-scored users were preferentially shown to other high-scored users (and vice versa), preserving "matching markets" between similar-attractiveness pools. Tinder publicly deprecated pure ELO in 2019 in favour of behavioural ML, but the underlying signal — "how often do people swipe right on this profile" — is still a feature in modern rankers, just one of many. The lesson: a single attractiveness scalar is brittle (depressing for low-scored users, monoculture for high-scored), but the *signal* of "right-swipe rate per impression" remains a strong predictor of match success and is fine to use as one feature among many. *[Source: Tinder engineering blog, 2019 announcement]*

**Anti-spam and fake-account defence in depth.** Five layers, ordered cheap → expensive: (1) *Phone + email verification* at signup blocks the laziest bot farms; (2) *Photo CV* — a CNN classifier checks profile photos for "is this a real face, not a stock image / not a watermark / not a known scammer's photo via reverse image hash"; flags ~3% of new accounts for review; (3) *Behavioural anomaly detection* — features include swipe rate variance (real users have bursty patterns, bots are uniform), session duration (bots run sessions that are too long or too short), typing cadence (bots type at metronome speed); (4) *Device fingerprint + IP cluster* — if 200 accounts share a device fingerprint or originate from the same /24 within an hour, they're an account farm; (5) *Shadow-ban* — confirmed-bad accounts continue to function from the user's perspective (they can swipe, see matches) but their swipes don't propagate to real targets. Shadow-banning specifically prevents account-farm operators from iterating on detection signals — they don't get a clean "blocked" signal to A/B against. *[Source: Bumble/Tinder engineering posts on trust & safety]*

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: Match fairness — how do you stop attractive users from monopolising the feed?**
Cap appearances per day per user and add diversity penalties in the ranker (don't show the same hot profile to a million people the same hour). *If pushed:* explicit "exposure budget" per profile per day — once exhausted, the profile drops in rank; gives long-tail users their fair share of feed slots.

**Q: A celebrity joins and gets 50k right-swipes in an hour — what fails?**
The swipes table's reverse-lookup index keyed by `target_id` becomes a write hotspot (the primary copy is partitioned by swiper, so it's fine — but the index used for match-check funnels every right-swipe to one row). *If pushed:* sub-shard the reverse index by `(target_id, hash(swiper_id) % N)` so writes fan across N rows; the match-check lookup then queries all N sub-shards. For hot targets, also throttle inbound swipes (silent rate limit) and queue them; the user doesn't notice and their inbox doesn't melt.

**Q: How do you stop bots and fake accounts?**
Phone verification at signup, photo CV (real face, single person, not a stock image), behavioural anomaly (uniform swipe rate, no idle time, perfect typing cadence). *If pushed:* device fingerprint + IP reputation cluster catches account farms; shadow-ban suspect accounts (their swipes don't propagate) so operators can't iterate on detection signals.

**Q: Geo edge case — a user in rural Wyoming sees the same 20 profiles every day.**
Expanding-radius search: start at 10km, expand until N candidates found or cap at 500km. Mix in "passport mode" (paid feature) for true long-distance browsing. *If pushed:* relax other filters before expanding radius (age band ±2yr → ±5yr) since users in sparse areas already trade-off distance; track cohort retention to validate the trade is right.

**Q: Two users right-swipe each other simultaneously — who creates the match?**
Idempotent match creation keyed on the canonical pair `(min(a,b), max(a,b))` — both writes target the same row, second is a no-op or upsert. Both users get notified. *If pushed:* if you need to know "who liked first" for analytics, store both swipes' timestamps in the match row; the match itself is the canonical entity, the swipes are the audit log.

**Q: How do you keep the swipe-history exclusion fast at 1B users × 500 swipes each?**
Bloom filter per user (2-3MB for 500k swipes at 1% FPR) cached at edge; on FP, a secondary check against the swipes table on write. *If pushed:* time-bound the exclusion (12 months) so the bloom doesn't grow unbounded; users genuinely re-encounter old profiles after the window, which the data shows is fine.

**Q: Two-tower architecture for the ranker — why not a single deep network?**
Single-network scoring `f(user, candidate)` requires re-running the model for every (user, candidate) pair on every request — at 1.5k candidates per feed × 60k feeds/s peak = 90M model calls/s, infeasible. Two-tower factorises into `user_emb = f_u(user)` and `candidate_emb = f_c(candidate)` with score = dot product. Candidate embeddings are precomputed offline (one per profile, refreshed nightly); request path computes only the user embedding once and dot-products against the candidate set — orders of magnitude cheaper. *If pushed:* the cost is *expressivity* — interactions between user and candidate features can only flow through the dot product. For high-value finalists you can run a richer cross-encoder model on the top-50, accepting the latency hit on a small set.

**Q: How does Tinder handle the "right-swipe inflation" problem where over time everyone gets more matches?**
By rebalancing exposure budgets, not match thresholds. As right-swipe rates drift up (users become less selective due to fatigue or paid features), the cap on per-profile-per-day appearances tightens to maintain target match rates. Diversification penalties also tighten so the same hot profile shows to fewer people. *If pushed:* monitor the match-rate distribution per cohort; if the upper tail compresses (everyone's getting matches with everyone) it's a sign the signal has degraded — adjust ranker temperature to inject more variance.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Hot user** — a celebrity becomes a write hotspot on the swipes table's *reverse-lookup index* (every right-swipe writes a row keyed by `target_id` for the match-check; the swiper-partitioned primary copy is fine) and a feed-distortion problem (they appear in millions of feeds). Cap appearances/day (exposure budget exhausted → drop in rank); shard the reverse index by `(target_id, hash(swiper_id) % N)` so writes fan across N sub-shards instead of one row; rate-limit inbound swipes silently — over the threshold, swipes are accepted at the API but not propagated to match-check, sparing the user a melting inbox.
- **Match check race** — A and B right-swipe each other within milliseconds; without ordering, two match rows could be created. Mitigate by keying match rows on the canonical pair `(min(a,b), max(a,b))` and using upsert/idempotent insert — second writer is a no-op or merges; both users get the match notification.
- **Bloom filter false positive** — bloom says "already swiped" when the user actually hasn't, removing a valid candidate from the feed; at 1% FPR this is rare but visible. Accept it for feed (occasional missed surface, not user-facing); on swipe write, do a secondary exact check against the swipes table to catch FPs that affect duplicate-write detection.
- **Geo skew** — NYC has thousands of candidates per km², Wyoming has dozens per 100km, so a fixed radius produces either flooded or empty feeds. Expanding-radius search starts at 10km and doubles until N candidates found or hitting a 500km cap; for sparse regions, also relax other filters (age ±2yr → ±5yr) before expanding distance further.
- **Bot / fake accounts** — fake profiles are commercially motivated (escort services, scams, data harvest). Layered defense: phone verification at signup, photo CV (real face, single person, not a stock image search hit), behavioral anomaly detection (uniform swipe rate, no idle time, suspiciously fast typing), device fingerprint + IP reputation clusters; suspect accounts shadow-banned (their swipes don't propagate) so operators can't iterate on detection signals.
- **Stale candidate embeddings** — candidate embeddings refreshed nightly miss intra-day signal (user changes bio, adds new photos, becomes more active). Refresh embeddings incrementally on profile-edit events via a streaming pipeline (event → re-embed → upsert vector index) so the ranker sees recent state within minutes. Trade-off: more compute on the embedding service vs fresher relevance.
- **Cohort collapse on small markets** — in a town with 5k users, the ML ranker eventually shows everyone everyone, and matches stop growing. Inject exploration slots (10-20% of feed) showing geographically-near-but-outside-radius candidates; slowly relax filters as the user nears exhaustion of the local pool; track "candidates remaining" SLO per geo cohort.


#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geo index | Geo-shard primary fails; queries return empty for users in that region | Per-shard query-rate dropoff alert; replica health check | Multi-AZ replica per shard with automatic promotion; client retries with widened-radius fallback; degrade gracefully to popularity-default feeds when geo unavailable |
| Bloom filter cache | Edge cache cold or evicted — feed includes already-swiped users | Swipe-write secondary check counts FP-corrections per minute | Warm bloom on session start (background fetch from cold store); accept transient bad UX rather than block feed; reconstruct bloom from `swipes` table on demand |
| Swipes table reverse index | Hot target (celebrity) saturates the reverse-lookup row | Per-target write QPS alert; row-level p99 latency on target_id reads | Sub-shard reverse index by `(target_id, hash(swiper_id) % N)`; rate-limit inbound right-swipes silently past a threshold; queue match-checks for hot targets and process with backpressure |
| Match-creation race | Simultaneous reciprocal right-swipes within ms create two match rows | Match-row uniqueness violation | Idempotent insert keyed on canonical pair `(min(a,b), max(a,b))`; second writer is a no-op; both users notified once |
| Photo CDN | Edge eviction causes thumbnail load misses on feed | Image fetch p99 latency; CDN error-rate spike | Multi-CDN with health-checked failover; pre-warm popular profile thumbnails on signup-publish; client retries on next-best resolution |
| ML ranker | Model server fails; feed falls back to non-personalised | Ranker-call p99/error-rate alert; feed-quality cohort watch | Cached "default rank" per geo as fallback; degrade to popularity-by-recency rather than empty feed; circuit-break the ranker for short windows |
| Fake-account pipeline | Photo CV mis-classifies and a wave of bot accounts slip in | Reports-per-1k-new-accounts alert; cohort signup spike from one ASN | Behavioral anomaly catch on first session (uniform swipe rate, no idle); shadow-ban suspect cohort; manual triage queue for trust-and-safety |
| Chat handoff | Match created, but chat service unavailable to bind to it | Match-without-chat-binding count | Chat binding is async — match row exists immediately; chat retries binding with backoff; clients display match in inbox even if first message attempt has to retry |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Feed latency** (request → 20 candidates returned) — SLO p99 < 300ms; ranker call dominates and is monitored independently.
- **Swipe write latency** (client tap → match decision returned) — SLO p99 < 200ms; includes the reverse-lookup match check.
- **Match conversion rate** (right-swipes-leading-to-match ÷ all right-swipes) — health metric; sudden drops mean ranker drift or hot-user exposure cap is misfiring.
- **Bloom FP rate at swipe-write** — SLO < 1.5% (sized as 1% target with margin); higher means bloom sizing is wrong or filter is over-saturated.
- **Hot-key per-target QPS distribution** — alert when a single `target_id` exceeds 1000 right-swipes/minute; auto-engages the sub-shard / silent-throttle policy.
- **Per-cohort match-rate fairness** — long-tail users vs top decile; if the top 1% absorb >30% of right-swipes the exposure cap needs tightening.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** active-active per region for users + swipes (each user homed to a region; geo queries served locally). Profile media on multi-region object store + global CDN. ML embeddings precomputed centrally and pushed to regional caches.
- **RTO:** ~5 minutes for in-region replica promotion. Cross-region failover (e.g. eu-west-1 down) routes traffic to the next-nearest region within DNS TTL — typically ~5-10 minutes for full convergence.
- **RPO:** ~seconds for swipes (async cross-region replication); a forced failover may "lose" the very latest right-swipes, mitigated by client-side retry on reconnect (idempotent swipe key on `(swiper_id, target_id)`).
- **Failover cadence:** automatic in-region; cross-region game-days quarterly; ML embedding refresh is region-local with a global central source-of-truth re-pushed nightly.
- **Cross-region cost:** swipe events (~390GB/day raw RF=3) replicated to a global archive; profile media has standard CDN egress; embeddings are small (~1KB × 50M = 50GB, refreshed nightly) — not a meaningful egress driver.

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **Storing exact user lat/lng in profile records.** Trilateration attacks become trivial — an attacker moves around and reads the exact distance. Bucket distances at the API tier (e.g. nearest km), keep precise lat/lng server-side only, and never expose it via any endpoint.
- **Match check via "scan target's swipes table".** A celebrity's swipe history has hundreds of thousands of rows; scanning is unbounded. Use the reverse-lookup index keyed by target with a single-row read; sub-shard the index for hot targets.
- **Treating bloom-filter false positives as fatal.** A 1% FPR on a feed-exclusion bloom is fine — you occasionally drop a valid candidate, but the user has thousands more. The FP only matters at swipe-write where an exact secondary check resolves it. Don't over-engineer the bloom sizing.
- **Allowing the ranker to run on the global candidate set.** Running ML scoring against millions of users per request is infeasible at peak. Always narrow first (geo → preferences → bloom) before the ranker; mention the cardinality reduction at each stage.
- **No exposure cap on hot users.** Without a per-day appearance cap, a single attractive user appears in millions of feeds — they get an unmanageable inbox, and other users see the same profiles. Cap appearances and add diversity penalties in the ranker.
- **Skipping the simultaneous-swipe race.** Two users right-swiping each other within milliseconds is common at scale. Without canonical-pair idempotency, two match rows may exist. Always key matches on `(min(a,b), max(a,b))`.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Feed generation is a narrowing pipeline, not one giant model call.** Geo query → preference filter → swipe exclusion → ML rerank is the structure to say out loud.
- **Mutual match detection is one write plus one reverse lookup.** That is the key scale property; if you need a scan, the design is already wrong.
- **Privacy is product-critical.** Rounded distances, bidirectional block-list enforcement, and no leakage of "who liked you" outside paid surfaces are not side issues here.
- **Hot users are both a systems problem and a fairness problem.** Exposure budgets and diversity penalties belong in the core design, not as an afterthought.
- **Media stays off the feed path.** The JSON feed returns URLs only; photos and videos are pre-encoded and CDN-served.
- **Trust and safety needs to be in the first answer.** Phone verification, CV moderation, behavioural anomaly detection, and shadow-banning show you understand the real operating environment.

### 39. Design GitHub (Source Code Hosting)
#### Problem
Host millions of git repositories with web UI, pull requests, issues, code search, CI integration.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** a bank vault that also has a library card catalog and a postal sorting room. The vault stores every version of every file you have ever committed — nothing is ever overwritten, just appended to (git is content-addressed: each file version is stored by its SHA-256 hash). The library card catalog tracks pull requests, issues, code reviews, and CI check results — small structured records that reference items in the vault. The postal sorting room receives "push" events and routes them to CI pipelines, webhook subscribers, and search indexers. These three things look like one website to the user but have completely different storage needs and scaling strategies.

**The single-request walkthrough:** a developer runs `git push origin main` on a 50KB commit to `kubernetes/kubernetes`. The push hits GitHub's git proxy fleet — a pool of stateless L7 servers that terminate TLS and SSH, authenticate the developer's token against the auth service, parse the URL into a repo name, and look up `repo_id → primary_shard_id` from a metadata cache (Redis, backed by the metadata database). The proxy then forwards the git smart-HTTP stream (a binary protocol for sending packfiles — compressed collections of git objects) to the primary file server, a system called Spokes. The Spokes primary receives the packfile, validates all SHA hashes, writes it to disk with fsync, and simultaneously replicates to two other file servers in different data centers. Only after 2 of the 3 file servers confirm they have fsynced the data does the Spokes primary send an ack up to the proxy, which returns HTTP 200 to the developer's git client. The push took ~1 second. Then asynchronously: Spokes publishes a `push_event` to a durable queue, which triggers CI workflows (GitHub Actions), delivers webhooks to Slack/Jira/etc., and updates the search index.

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Spokes (the git file server system)** — GitHub's name for the 3-replica git storage layer. Every repository lives on 3 file servers intentionally spread across data centers ("we never place a majority of repository replicas in any single datacenter"). A push is only acked to the client after 2 of 3 replicas have fsynced the packfile. If a replica is damaged (bit rot, partial write), it is rebuilt from a healthy peer using `git clone` semantics plus SHA verification — not a raw filesystem copy — so corruption is detected rather than propagated.
- **Git proxy fleet** — stateless L7 servers that translate user-facing `github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes` URLs into internal `(repo_id, shard_id)` coordinates and then proxy the bidirectional git byte stream to the right Spokes primary. Stateless means any proxy instance can handle any request; the fleet scales horizontally behind an L4 load balancer.
- **Metadata database (transactional, relational)** — stores small structured records: pull requests, issues, comments, code reviews, check runs, labels. These are ordinary database rows — nothing git-specific. A PR is just `(pr_id, repo_id, head_sha, base_sha, state, author_id, ...)`. The actual diff is computed on demand by asking the git server `git diff base_sha..head_sha` and cached by `(repo_id, base_sha, head_sha)`.
- **Trigram search index** — code search works by indexing every 3-character substring ("trigram") of every source file. The query `getUserById` is rewritten as the intersection of posting lists for `get`, `etU`, `tUs`, etc. Files containing all required trigrams are candidate matches; a fast regex pass verifies them. Sharded by `repo_id` so ACL enforcement at query time is straightforward — users only query shards for repos they have access to.
- **Webhook/CI queue (Kafka)** — decouples pushes from their downstream effects. A push acks the developer immediately; the queue durably holds the `push_event` for up to 24 hours. Webhook workers fan out deliveries to registered URLs with exponential backoff on failure. Actions scheduler reads `.github/workflows/` files from the Spokes replica at the pushed SHA and queues CI jobs.

**The thing that makes it hard:** a large-scale open-source project like `kubernetes/kubernetes` (chromium, llvm, the Linux kernel) is used by thousands of developers globally, each running `git clone` or `git fetch` constantly. A full clone of such a repo downloads gigabytes of data. The naive approach — serve every clone request from the primary Spokes node — quickly saturates the file server's network and disk I/O. But the more subtle problem is the history walk: to prepare a clone packfile, git must traverse the entire commit graph, which for a repo with 1 million commits takes significant CPU. For very large repos, this traversal was previously the bottleneck that made clones time out.

**Why the standard solution works:** GitHub's geometric repacking + multi-pack bitmaps is the canonical fix. Normal git stores all objects in one giant packfile; for a large repo this packfile can be multi-gigabyte and requires a full re-sort every time the repo changes. Geometric repacking instead maintains a set of packfiles where each is at least 2x as large as the next — the total number of packfiles grows logarithmically with object count, and they are never all merged at once. Multi-pack bitmaps are precomputed reachability indexes across all packfiles, so a clone no longer needs to traverse the commit graph at all — the bitmap directly answers "which objects are reachable from this commit?" Reported result: average repack time dropped from 60+ minutes to ~15 seconds; fetch CPU dropped 35% across all repos. For client-side, Protocol v2 + `--filter=blob:none` (partial clone) skips downloading file contents entirely until checkout — a developer can clone a 30GB monorepo to a working checkout in minutes by fetching only commit metadata first.

**If you were building it tomorrow:**
- Spokes: 3 bare-git file servers per repo, cross-DC. Consensus layer (etcd) tracks `repo_id → (primary, replica_list, generation)`. Quorum: ack client after 2-of-3 fsync.
- Git proxy: stateless Go service, Redis-cached `repo_id → shard` lookups (30s TTL). Rate-limit per user and per IP at the proxy.
- Core push flow pseudocode:
  ```
  shard_id = redis.get(f"repo:{repo_id}:shard") or db.lookup(repo_id)
  primary = spokes.get_primary(shard_id)
  stream = proxy.forward(client_stream, primary)
  primary.replicate(replica_1, replica_2)
  await quorum(2_of_3)       # wait for fsync confirmation
  ack_client(200)
  kafka.publish("push_event", { repo_id, sha, pusher_id })
  ```
- For hot repos: add 5-10 read replicas, load-balance clone/fetch traffic across them. Cache common `git-upload-pack` responses by ref+commit SHA.
#### Clarifying Questions
- Public + private repos?
- Code search across all public?
- Actions / CI in scope?
- Large file storage (LFS)?
- Multi-region?

#### Requirements
- **FR:** git push/pull/clone over HTTPS+SSH; web UI; PR; issues; reviews; org/team mgmt; search
- **NFR:** durable repos (no data loss), git operations performant (clone of large repos), 99.99% availability

#### Scale Estimate
- **Users / repos:** GitHub reports ~100M users (2023 public stat) and 400M+ repositories (2024 Octoverse). Use 100M repos as conservative active-corpus default (drops dormant forks / spam repos that aren't in the working set). Mix: ~70% private (default since 2019 free private repos), ~30% public — derived from public Octoverse activity ratios.
- **Git ops/s:** assume ~10% of users active per day → ~10M DAU × ~10 git ops (push/pull/clone) per active user/day = ~100M ops/day → 100M ÷ 86400 ≈ ~1.2k/s pure user ops; CI fetches dominate (each push triggers N CI clones from runners) → ~5-10x amplification → ~10k/s sustained. Peak (US working hours overlap with EU): 5x avg → ~50k/s. PR opens: ~1M PRs/day public ÷ 86400 ≈ ~12/s avg → 1k/min peak working-hours. CI runner-jobs: each push fans out to ~5 workflows → 10k pushes/s × 5 = ~50k workflow-jobs/s peak; smoothed via runner queue to 10k+ runner-jobs/min sustained.
- **Repo size distribution:** measured GitHub stats — p50 ~5MB (typical side-project), p90 ~200MB (mature OSS lib), p99 ~2GB (large monorepo), long tail ~30GB+ (chromium, llvm, microsoft/winscope-class).
- **Aggregate repo storage:** 100M repos × ~50MB avg (the long tail dominates the mean — even with p50 ~5MB, the chromium-class outliers pull avg ~50MB). 100M × 50MB = ~5PB raw bare-repo data; Spokes 3-replica → ~15PB across the file-server fleet.
- **LFS blobs:** GitHub Octoverse — ~10% of repos use LFS for binary assets (designs, ML models, datasets). Avg blob volume ~500MB/repo (10 large blobs × 50MB or ~50 medium × 10MB). 100M × 0.1 × 500MB = ~5PB object-store binaries. Content-addressed cross-repo dedupe (forks share blobs) ~30% saving → ~3.5PB net.
- **Issues/PRs records:** ~1M new issues/PRs/day (Octoverse public stat). ~5KB each (id 16B + repo_id 16B + state 4B + author 16B + assignees ~64B + labels ~256B + body ~4KB + indexed metadata ~600B). 1M × 5KB = ~5GB/day; cumulative over a year × 365 ≈ ~1.8TB/yr; multiple years on hot store → ~10TB; RF=3 → ~30TB.
- **Comments/reviews:** ~10M/day (10x issues/PRs since each gets multiple comments). ~2KB each (body ~1KB + metadata 1KB). 10M × 2KB = ~20GB/day → ~7TB/yr.
- **Code search index:** trigram inverted index sized ~3-5x source-code volume (each char appears in ~3 trigrams + posting-list overhead). Indexed public corpus: 100M × 0.3 public × ~50MB ÷ ~10x non-code files (binaries, generated, vendored excluded) ≈ ~150TB code source; with overhead ~500TB indexable content; × 4x trigram multiplier → ~2PB on search-index cluster.
- **Webhook event volume:** 50k git ops/s peak × ~3 webhook subscribers avg per repo (CI + Slack + ops integrations) = ~150k webhook deliveries/s peak. Retention 7d for retries (24h primary + 6d for forensics) × avg ~100k/s × ~1KB payload = ~85TB → ~90TB on the durable queue.
- **Audit log:** 10k git ops/s sustained × ~500B audit row (actor 16B + repo_id 16B + action enum 4B + ts 8B + ip 16B + user-agent ~200B + diff ref ~100B + padding ~140B) = 400GB/day raw. Columnar Parquet on object store, dictionary encoding on `actor`/`action`/`repo_id` → 5x compression → ~80GB/day compressed. 7yr regulatory retention × 365d × 80GB ≈ ~200TB.

#### API Contract
```
git protocol over HTTPS + SSH                  (push/pull/clone)
REST + GraphQL APIs:
  GET  /repos/{owner}/{repo}
  POST /repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls
  GET  /search/code?q=...                      → results
WebHooks                                       (push, PR, etc.)
```

#### High-Level Design
```
                   ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
                   │   Frontend (web UI + REST + GQL)    │
                   └──────────────────┬──────────────────┘
                                      │
        ┌──────────────────────┬──────┴──────┬──────────────────────┐
        ↓                      ↓             ↓                      ↓
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────────┐  ┌────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│  Auth /      │    │  Git Storage     │  │ Search     │    │  PR / Issues │
│  Permissions │    │  (sharded git    │  │ (trigram   │    │  Service     │
│              │    │   on many file   │  │  index)    │    │              │
└──────────────┘    │   servers, repls)│  └────────────┘    └──────────────┘
                    └──────────────────┘
                                      │
                            ┌──────────────────┐
                            │ Git Proxy        │  (routes git ops to right shard)
                            └──────────────────┘
                                      ↓
                       ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                       │ CI / Actions Runner Pool     │
                       │ (containerized; per-job VMs) │
                       └──────────────────────────────┘
```

**How to read the diagram:** repository storage, pull-request metadata, code search, and CI are separate subsystems connected by repo identity and git refs. A clone or push mostly cares about repo shards; a PR page mostly cares about metadata and indexes.

**Why the flow is shaped this way:** the product surface looks unified, but the workloads are not. Git object storage, search, issue tracking, and CI all want different storage and scaling strategies, so separating them keeps any one model from distorting the others.

**What this layout buys you:** a platform that can scale repo traffic, collaboration features, and automation independently. The tradeoff is more cross-system coordination whenever one user action triggers several downstream effects.

#### Data Model
- **repos** (transactional store): `(repo_id, owner_id, name, visibility, default_branch, primary_shard)`
- **users / orgs / teams / permissions** (transactional store)
- **issues / prs** (transactional store): `(id, repo_id, state, author, assignees[], labels[], body)`
- **comments** (transactional store or sharded)
- **git data** (filesystem on shard): bare git repos, replicated across N file servers
- **LFS objects** (object store + content-addressed)
- **search index** (trigram inverted index): tokenized code (handle camelCase, snake_case, identifiers)

#### Detailed Design
**Repo sharding and routing.** Each repo is assigned a *primary shard* (one file server) plus N read replicas on other file servers in different racks/AZs. The metadata store keeps `repo_id → primary_shard_id` plus the replica list — a tiny row, indexed for fast lookup. When a user does `git push https://github.com/foo/bar`, the request hits the git proxy fleet, which auths, parses the URL into `(owner, name)`, resolves to `repo_id` in the metadata DB, looks up the primary shard, and forwards the smart-HTTP stream to that file server. Reads (`git clone`, `git fetch`) can route to any replica, balanced by load. Replication is either log-shipping (push the packfile + ref-update to replicas asynchronously after the primary commits) or filesystem-level (DRBD/LVM mirror), depending on durability requirements — log shipping is cheaper but has a small lag window where a primary failure could lose seconds of pushes. Concrete example: `git push` of a 50KB commit hits proxy → metadata lookup (~1ms) → forwards to file server #237 → primary applies ref update, fsyncs, returns success → log-shipper streams the packfile to replicas in the background within seconds.

**Git proxy routing.** The proxy is a stateless L7 router specialized for the git protocol. For HTTPS, it terminates TLS, runs auth (token validation against the auth service), inspects the URL path to identify the repo, looks up the shard via the metadata cache (Redis-backed, TTL'd), and proxies the bidirectional byte stream to the chosen file server. For SSH, it terminates the SSH connection (validating the user's public key against the auth service), parses the requested git command (`git-upload-pack` for fetch, `git-receive-pack` for push), then opens an SSH or local-protocol connection to the shard and bridges the streams. The proxy applies per-user/per-repo rate limits, abuse detection (suspicious clone patterns), and routes writes only to primaries. Because the proxy is stateless, you can scale it horizontally behind a standard L4 load balancer; failure of any proxy instance just retries on another. Concrete example: a clone of `kubernetes/kubernetes` from CI in Frankfurt → proxy in eu-west does auth + lookup, picks the eu-west replica of the repo, streams ~3GB packfile to the client over HTTPS.

**Pull requests.** A PR is a small relational entity: `(pr_id, repo_id, head_sha, base_sha, head_branch, base_branch, state, author_id, …)` plus rows in `comments`, `reviews`, `checks`. The "code" of the PR is just two git refs — the diff is computed on demand by asking the git server `git diff base_sha..head_sha`, which is a fast walk over the commit graph. Diff results are cached by `(repo_id, base_sha, head_sha)` so the same PR view is served from cache after the first viewer. Merging runs `git merge` (or `rebase`/`squash`, depending on settings) inside the bare repo on the file server, producing a merge commit, fast-forwarding the base branch ref. Concrete example: PR #1234 on `foo/bar` is opened — one row in `pull_requests`, no git mutation; reviewer requests changes, comment rows added; author pushes new commits to the head branch (git mutation, `head_sha` updated on the PR row); merge button computes diff fresh, runs `git merge`, advances `main`.

**Code search (trigram index).** Regular tokenizers (whitespace + punctuation split) fail on code: searching `getUserById` won't find `getuserbyid` or `get_user_by_id` or partial-substring matches, and regex queries are out of reach. The fix is *trigram indexing*: every 3-char shingle in every source file becomes a posting list entry. The string `getUserById` produces trigrams `get`, `etU`, `tUs`, `Use`, `ser`, `erB`, `rBy`, `ByI`, `yId`. A query for substring `serBy` is rewritten as the intersection of the posting lists for `ser`, `erB`, `rBy` — files containing all three are candidate matches; a final regex/substring verification step runs against just the candidates. Regex queries are rewritten into a set of trigrams that any matching file must contain, then verified. This is the architecture Google's codesearch and Sourcegraph use. The index is sharded by repo (so private-repo ACLs are easy: filter shards by access at query time), then sub-sharded by trigram for very large public-repo corpora. Concrete example: search `regex:Handle.*Request` across 10M public Go files — query rewriter extracts mandatory trigrams `Han`, `dle`, `equ`, `ues`, `est`; intersection narrows to ~50k candidate files; regex runs on each; top-K returned in a few hundred ms.

**Webhooks / CI.** A successful push commits the ref-update on the primary shard, then publishes a `push_event` to the durable queue. Webhook workers consume the event, look up subscribers (orgs/repos with webhook URLs configured), and POST the payload (with HMAC signature for verification) to each, retrying with exponential backoff on 5xx/timeout up to ~24h. CI/Actions consumes the same event: the workflow scheduler reads the repo's `.github/workflows/*.yml` from the git server at the pushed SHA, computes which workflows match the event, queues each as a job, and the runner pool spins up an ephemeral VM (or container) per job — clones the repo at the event SHA, runs the steps, reports check results back to the PR/issues service. Concrete example: a push to `main` in a Node.js repo triggers `ci.yml` (test) and `deploy.yml` (deploy) — two workflow jobs queued, two VMs spun up, each runs ~3 minutes, posts check status; the PR view shows "all checks passed" via the checks rows on the relevant PR.

**Large File Storage (LFS).** Git's delta compression is built for line-based text and balloons on binary blobs — committing a 100MB design file directly into git makes every clone download every version of it forever. LFS replaces the binary content in git with a tiny pointer file (`oid sha256:...`, size, version), and stores the actual blob in object storage (S3) keyed by content hash. On push, the git client uploads the blob to the LFS server (which writes to S3) before pushing the pointer-bearing commit. On clone/checkout, the git client sees the pointer, downloads the blob from LFS via a separate authenticated HTTP request, materializes the file. Cross-repo dedup is automatic via content addressing — the same 100MB file forked across 1000 repos costs one S3 blob. Concrete example: a designer commits `mockup.psd` (200MB); LFS uploads it to S3 keyed by SHA-256; the repo on disk grows by ~150 bytes (the pointer); future clones download the pointer instantly and pull the blob lazily on checkout.

**Spokes (GitHub's git fileserver) — three-replica majority.** Spokes is the system that replaces the original single-primary-with-replicas model with a three-way replicated git store. Every repo lives on three file servers, intentionally split across data-centres: GitHub's stated invariant is *"we never place a majority of repository replicas in any single datacenter."* This means a full-DC outage cannot lose write quorum on any repo — at least one replica in another DC has every push. Writes go to the primary; the primary streams the packfile to the other two replicas; a push acks the client only after a quorum (2 of 3) has fsynced. Repair: a damaged replica is rebuilt from a peer via `git clone` semantics rather than filesystem copy, so corruption (bit rot, partial write) is detected via SHA-1 mismatch on object verification. Routing decisions during failover rely on a small consensus layer that tracks `repo_id → (primary, replicas[], generation)` — when the primary is lost, a new primary is elected from the remaining replicas and the generation bumps. *[Source: GitHub engineering blog, "Scaling monorepo maintenance" 2021]*

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Client
    participant P as Git Proxy
    participant M as Metadata DB
    participant S1 as Spokes Primary (DC-A)
    participant S2 as Replica (DC-B)
    participant S3 as Replica (DC-C)
    participant Q as Webhook Queue
    participant CI as CI Runner
    C->>P: git push HTTPS over auth
    P->>M: lookup repo_id and primary
    M-->>P: primary=S1, generation=42
    P->>S1: forward smart-HTTP receive-pack
    S1->>S1: validate refs and write packfile
    S1->>S2: replicate packfile + ref-update
    S1->>S3: replicate packfile + ref-update
    S2-->>S1: fsynced
    S3-->>S1: fsynced
    Note over S1: quorum 2 of 3 reached
    S1-->>P: ack push
    P-->>C: 200 OK
    S1->>Q: publish push_event (async)
    Q->>CI: trigger workflow at SHA
    CI->>S2: git clone replica
    CI->>CI: run tests, post check status
```

**Geometric repacking and multi-pack bitmaps.** Pre-2021 GitHub maintenance ran `git repack` on each repo to compress every object into a single packfile — for huge repos this ran in 60+ minutes and burned massive CPU. The fix is *geometric repacking*: maintain a sorted set of packs where each pack contains at least 2× as many objects as the next, so total pack count grows logarithmically with object count rather than linearly. Combined with *multi-pack bitmaps* (an index over reachability across packs, not within a single pack), serving a fetch no longer requires that all objects live in one giant pack. Reported wins: average repack time 1 minute → 15 seconds, fetch CPU down 35% across all repos and up to 80% on individual large ones. *[Source: GitHub engineering blog]*

**Multi-region operation.** Yes — write-primary in one region (Spokes' three replicas straddle DCs within that primary region's metro area for cross-DC majority on writes) with async-replicated read-only mirrors in other regions (EU, APAC). The git proxy in each region prefers a local replica for reads and forwards writes back to the primary region — clones from Frankfurt hit the eu-west replica, but a push from Frankfurt pays one trans-Atlantic RTT to commit. Metadata (repo→shard, ACLs, PR/issue rows) replicates via the transactional store's native multi-region setup with bounded staleness; PR comments may take seconds to appear in a remote region, but git ref state is strongly consistent because every push routes to the primary. CI runner pools are regional, scheduled near the data they clone. Trade-off: writes pay cross-region latency, and a primary-region failure means demoting reads to remote regions until recovery — explicitly chosen over multi-write because git's content-addressable durability is hard to reconcile across async cross-region writes without operator visibility into conflicts.

**Monorepo support — partial clone, sparse checkout, GVFS.** Microsoft Windows source (~270GB, 3.5M files, 8M commits) was the canonical "git can't do this" repo until GVFS (now Scalar). Three layers of support compose: (1) *Protocol v2 + partial clone* (`git clone --filter=blob:none`) skips downloading any blob until checkout — the initial clone fetches only commit graph + trees, blobs lazy-load on `git checkout`; (2) *Sparse checkout* limits the working tree to declared paths, so a developer working in one team's directory doesn't materialise the rest; (3) *Reachability bitmaps* server-side make the ref-enumeration phase O(1) rather than O(commits), so even a "clone everything" against a 10M-commit repo doesn't time out; (4) *GVFS / Scalar* virtualises the filesystem itself — `ls` on a non-materialised directory makes a synchronous fetch from the server. Combined, a 270GB repo clones to a developer machine in ~5 minutes with ~5GB of disk used. *[Source: Microsoft GVFS / Scalar docs]*

#### Potential Follow-Up Questions
**Q: Git LFS or just raise the repo size limit?**
LFS for binary assets that change often (designs, datasets, ML models) — git's delta compression is built for text and balloons on binaries. *If pushed:* the cost is LFS adds an extra round-trip per blob (clone is two protocols, not one); for repos that just have a few large checked-in binaries, partial clone + sparse checkout is simpler than LFS.

**Q: How do you do code search across millions of repos?**
Trigram index per repo (3-char shingles → posting list); query rewrites substring/regex into trigram intersection. ACL filter applied at query time after candidate set returned. *If pushed:* global search is the hard part — index size scales with code volume, not repo count; shard by repo, fan out queries, merge top-K; precompute popular query results for "trending searches".

**Q: A push triggers 1000 CI workflows — how do you stop the runner pool collapsing?**
Per-org concurrency limits, queue with priorities (paid orgs ahead of free), autoscale runners with a hard ceiling. *If pushed:* workflow-level dedup — if the same SHA is already running, queue subsequent triggers as a single "rebuild on completion" rather than launching N parallel duplicates; saves 30%+ on hot repos.

**Q: A 30GB monorepo (chromium-style) — what's the clone strategy?**
Protocol v2 + partial clone (`--filter=blob:none`) + sparse checkout — fetch only the directories the dev cares about. *If pushed:* server-side, store packfiles with reachability bitmaps so clone enumeration is O(1) instead of O(commits); for the genuinely largest repos (Microsoft Windows source) you need GVFS-style virtual filesystem with on-demand fetch.

**Q: How do you handle a hot repo (`kubernetes/kubernetes`) hammered by clones?**
Read replicas of the bare repo on multiple file servers; loadbalance reads, route writes to primary; aggressive HTTP caching for `git-upload-pack` responses on common ref states. *If pushed:* edge-cache the most recent packfile snapshots at CDN; clones from cold geos hit the snapshot first then incrementally fetch from the primary.

**Q: How do you migrate a repo to a new shard without downtime?**
Replicate to the new shard, dual-write for a window, flip the metadata pointer, verify, then drop the old replica. Pushes during the flip get a brief 503 (seconds). *If pushed:* git's content-addressable model makes this safer than typical DB migrations — you can verify by SHA equivalence on every object before flipping; rollback is just flipping the pointer back.

**Q: How do you protect against a malicious push that exfiltrates secrets via webhook?**
Webhook URLs validated against allowlist, secrets scrubbed from logs, signed payloads (HMAC). *If pushed:* sandbox webhook delivery in a separate egress network so a malicious receiver can't pivot into your infra; rate-limit per-receiver to limit blast radius if a target is compromised.

**Q: Why does Spokes use 3 replicas with cross-DC majority instead of 5 with single-DC majority?**
Three-with-cross-DC trades higher latency on quorum writes (must hit a remote DC every push) for *survival of any single DC outage* — losing a DC never costs you the majority. Five-with-single-DC would be cheaper to write but a DC fire is a data-loss event. GitHub's invariant is durability over write latency. *If pushed:* the cost is real — every push pays a cross-DC RTT; mitigated by colocating CI runners and the primary in the same region so the developer-perceived push latency is dominated by upload, not replication.

**Q: How does code search handle private-repo ACLs without scanning every result?**
Trigram index sharded by `repo_id`. At query time the search service first computes the user's accessible-repos set (from the auth service, cached per session), restricts the trigram intersection to those repo shards, and never reads from inaccessible shards. The user can't even time-attack on private-repo existence because the shard list is opaque. *If pushed:* large orgs with millions of accessible repos hit a problem — the access set itself is huge; precompute org-level access roll-ups and filter at the org granularity first, then per-repo within.

**Q: How does the geometric repacking handle a force-push that orphans 1M objects?**
Force-push doesn't trigger immediate repack — the orphaned objects stay reachable via reflog for the configured grace period (typically 90 days). Geometric repack runs periodically (cron-ish) and only includes reachable objects in new packs; orphaned objects fall into a "loose" pool that's GC'd after the grace window. *If pushed:* concurrent force-pushes can briefly inflate disk usage with redundant packs; cap the rate of force-pushes per repo, and run urgent repack if disk pressure crosses threshold.

#### Bottlenecks & Mitigations
- **Hot repo** — `kubernetes/kubernetes` and similar OSS megaprojects get hammered by clone/fetch traffic, saturating the primary shard's disk IO and network. Add multiple read replicas (5-10 for the top repos), load-balance fetches across them, aggressive HTTP caching for `git-upload-pack` responses on common ref states (most clones request the same default-branch HEAD); for cold-region clones, edge-cache recent packfile snapshots so fetch hits the CDN before the origin shard.
- **Massive repo** (chromium ~30GB) — full clone is gigabytes of network and disk IO, and history walk for clone enumeration is O(commits). Protocol v2 + partial clone (`--filter=blob:none`) skips blob download until checkout; sparse checkout limits materialized files to declared paths; reachability bitmaps server-side make ref-enumeration O(1); for the truly largest (Microsoft Windows-class), GVFS-style virtual filesystem with on-demand fetch is the only option.
- **Webhook fan-out** — a single push can have 1000+ webhook subscribers; synchronous delivery would block the push response for seconds. Decouple via durable queue: ack the push immediately, fan out delivery via worker pool with retry+backoff (24h retention); per-receiver rate limits prevent one slow subscriber from starving others; signed payloads (HMAC) so receivers can verify authenticity.
- **CI fan-out** — a push to a monorepo can trigger 1000s of CI workflows, blowing through runner capacity. Per-org concurrency limits cap parallelism; queue with priorities (paid orgs ahead of free); workflow-level dedup — if the same SHA is already running, queue subsequent triggers as a single "rebuild on completion" rather than launching N parallel duplicates.
- **DDoS on git endpoints** — anonymous clones are unauthenticated and bandwidth-heavy, making git endpoints a juicy attack target. Rate limits per IP and per authenticated user (separate buckets); abuse detection on suspicious patterns (rapid clones from new IPs, clones immediately followed by repo creation suggesting scraping); aggressive temporary blocks for known-bad IP ranges.
- **Spokes replica skew** — a replica that lags far behind primary becomes useless for reads; if too many lag, write quorum is at risk. Health-check replicas continuously (every 30s); if a replica's lag exceeds threshold, drain reads from it and trigger an automated re-replication from a healthy peer; alert if 2-of-3 are unhealthy because the next failure loses quorum.
- **Repack pressure on huge repos** — geometric repacking still consumes IO at scale; on a fileserver hosting 50k repos, simultaneous repacks across many repos saturate disk. Schedule repacks per-fileserver with a global concurrency cap; prioritise by repo activity (active repos first, dormant ones can wait days); piggyback opportunistic repacks on quiet periods detected via per-repo IO metrics.
- **Force-push reflog exhaustion** — heavy force-push usage (some teams use force-push as workflow) inflates loose object pool faster than the 90d GC can keep up. Configurable per-org reflog retention; emergency GC trigger when loose-object disk usage crosses threshold; warn users on excessive force-push patterns.


#### Failure Modes
| Layer | Failure | Detection | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spokes primary | Primary file server fails mid-push; client connection dies | Replica heartbeat to coordination layer; missing primary-ack within timeout | Elect new primary from remaining replicas; bump generation; client retries push (idempotent — git protocol carries pack hash, server detects already-applied refs) |
| Spokes replica skew | One replica falls behind; a second failure would lose write quorum | Per-replica lag SLO (max-lag > N seconds = unhealthy) | Drain reads from lagging replica; trigger automated re-replication from a healthy peer; alert if 2-of-3 unhealthy because the next failure loses quorum |
| Git proxy | Stateless proxy crashes; in-flight git stream interrupted | Health checks at L4 LB; per-connection error rate | Client retries against another proxy instance; smart-HTTP and SSH protocols are idempotent at the request level; proxy fleet sized for N+2 redundancy per region |
| Metadata DB | `repo_id → primary_shard` lookup unavailable | Lookup p99 latency; transactional store error rate | Cache lookup at proxy with ~30s TTL; serve stale on lookup error and probe in background; degraded: route to last-known primary even if metadata is unreachable |
| Webhook delivery | Receiver returns 5xx or times out repeatedly | Per-receiver delivery success rate; queue depth per webhook target | Exponential backoff up to 24h retention; circuit-break consistently-failing receivers (block delivery, surface to repo admin); HMAC-signed payloads so receiver can dedupe on retry |
| CI runner pool | Runner VM fails to start, or throughput collapses under fan-out | Job-queue depth per org; runner-launch p99 | Per-org concurrency caps; queue priorities (paid > free); workflow-level dedup (same SHA already running → "rebuild on completion"); ephemeral runner pool with hard ceiling and graceful queue |
| Code search index | Trigram shard hot or stale | Query p99 per shard; index-build lag | Replicas per shard, fan out reads; rebuild stale shards from canonical source; degrade to "search temporarily unavailable" rather than wrong results |
| LFS pointer↔blob mismatch | Pointer in git but blob missing in object store (corruption / orphaned upload) | LFS fetch 404 on checkout | Validate pointer→blob existence on push; re-upload from another replica (LFS is content-addressed so any source with the SHA suffices); audit-log mismatches for forensic review |

#### Observability — Key Metrics & SLOs
- **Git op latency** (push and clone p50/p99 by repo size class) — SLO p99 push < 5s for repos < 1GB; clone latency tracked per size bucket because the long tail (chromium-class) skews any global metric.
- **Spokes write quorum success ratio** — SLO > 99.99%; quorum failures are visible as push errors and are the most user-visible reliability signal.
- **Replica lag distribution** — SLO p99 < 5 seconds; growth indicates network or disk pressure on one DC and is a leading indicator of quorum risk.
- **Webhook delivery success / lag per repo** — SLO > 99% delivered within 60s; per-receiver breakdown to catch slow integrators starving others.
- **CI runner queue depth and time-to-start** — SLO p99 time-to-start < 30s for paid tiers; queue depth is the leading indicator of runner-pool exhaustion during fan-out spikes.
- **Code search query latency** — SLO p99 < 1s for ACL-filtered global queries; trigram intersection cost dominates and is monitored per shard.

#### Multi-Region & DR
- **Replication mode:** Spokes triple-replica with cross-DC majority within the home region; async-replicated read-only mirrors in other regions (EU, APAC). Writes always route to the home-region primary; reads prefer local replica.
- **RTO:** ~30 seconds for in-region primary failover (Spokes generation bump). Cross-region failover (lose home region entirely) is operator-triggered: ~30 minutes including DNS cutover, promotion of a remote async replica, acceptance that recent pushes may need to be re-pushed.
- **RPO:** ~0s for in-region failover (sync 2-of-3 quorum). Cross-region: up to ~seconds of unreplicated pushes (async lag) — mitigated by git's content-addressable durability (replaying a missing push is just re-pushing the same packfile). Metadata (PRs, issues) bounded-staleness: typically seconds.
- **Failover cadence:** automatic for Spokes in-region; cross-region game-days quarterly; CI runner pools are regional, scaled on demand.
- **Cross-region cost:** dominant egress is git replication of pack data. Cold-start a new region cost = full mirror seed (~5PB raw bare-repo data) plus ongoing delta (~50-100GB/day live tail). LFS blobs replicated cross-region only on demand (first remote access pulls + caches at remote object store).

#### Common Mistakes / Anti-patterns
- **"Primary + read-replicas via log-shipping" — wrong for Spokes.** Casual descriptions of "GitHub git storage" reach for the standard primary-async-replica topology, but Spokes uses a 3-replica cross-DC majority quorum: writes ack only after 2-of-3 fsync, replicas are full peers (not log-shipped), and a damaged replica is rebuilt from a healthy peer via `git clone` semantics with SHA verification rather than filesystem copy. Saying "log-shipping" misses the durability invariant ("never place a majority of replicas in any single datacenter") that defines the design.
- **Treating a PR as git-native data.** A PR is relational metadata that *references* git refs (head_sha, base_sha); reviews, comments, labels, and checks live in the transactional store. Conflating the two leads to mismatched consistency models and overcomplicated PR creation flows.
- **Standard tokenizer for code search.** Whitespace+punctuation tokenizers fail on `getUserById` ↔ `get_user_by_id` ↔ partial-substring queries. Trigram indexing is the right primitive — name it explicitly and explain the regex-rewrite path.
- **Synchronous webhook delivery on push.** Blocking the push response on webhook fan-out means one slow integrator can stall every push to a repo. Always decouple via durable queue with retries; ack the push immediately.
- **Unbounded CI fan-out per push.** A monorepo push can trigger 1000s of workflow jobs; without per-org concurrency limits and SHA-level dedup the runner pool collapses. State the quotas and dedup policy explicitly.
- **Forgetting LFS for binary churn.** Committing binaries directly into git makes every clone download every version forever (delta compression is built for text). LFS replaces blobs with content-addressed pointers — for repos with binary assets this is a 100x win on clone size.

#### Talking Points for the Interview
- **Separate git storage from collaboration metadata immediately.** Repos are append-heavy content-addressed blobs; PRs, checks, comments, and issues are transactional relational data.
- **Spokes-style 3-way replication with 2-of-3 fsync quorum** is the durability story worth naming explicitly. That is what distinguishes this from a generic file server design.
- **Git protocol routing is long-lived stream proxying, not a normal REST request.** The proxy authenticates, resolves the shard, and then tunnels smart-HTTP or SSH to the primary.
- **Code search is its own system.** Trigram indexing, ACL filtering at query time, and separate serving infrastructure show you understand why grep-on-disk is not the answer.
- **CI fan-out is often the hidden dominant load.** Git operations are not the only scale driver; the push side effects matter just as much.
- **Large repos need special treatment.** Partial clone, sparse checkout, bitmaps, and sometimes dedicated shards are the right things to mention for chromium-class repos.

### 40. Design Spotify (Music Streaming + Recommendations)
#### Problem
Stream music globally with low latency, generate personalized playlists, support playlists/social, and pay royalties accurately.

#### Summary
**The picture in your head:** three separate businesses that share the same front door. Walk in and you see one music app, but behind the scenes: (1) a media distribution company that stores every song at multiple quality levels and serves them from a global network of servers close to listeners (a CDN — Content Delivery Network); (2) a recommendation engine that analyzes your listening history and computes your personalized playlist every week using machine learning; (3) a financial accounting firm that counts every second of every stream for every listener and calculates royalty payments to rights holders. These three have almost nothing in common architecturally. Playback needs to be fast. Recommendations need to be smart. Royalties need to be correct to the cent.

**The single-request walkthrough:** a user in Berlin presses Play on a track they have never heard before. The client calls the Catalog Service: `GET /track/{track_id}` returns metadata plus signed CDN URLs for each bitrate variant (96kbps, 160kbps, 320kbps, FLAC). The client examines its subscription tier (Premium → eligible for 320kbps) and measures current network throughput, then selects 320kbps. It fetches the audio manifest — a list of ~10-second chunk URLs — from the nearest CDN edge node in Frankfurt. The first chunk arrives in ~80ms, playback begins. While the user is listening, the client pre-fetches the next track's first chunk ~15 seconds before the current track ends, so the gap between tracks is imperceptible. Simultaneously, every 30 seconds the client sends a heartbeat play-event: `{ track_id, user_id, duration_played: 30, skip: false, country: "DE" }`. This event lands in a partitioned Kafka stream. Two consumers read it independently: the Royalty Pipeline (counts every play for payment calculation) and the Recommendation ML pipeline (uses plays, skips, and saves as training signal for future recommendations).

**The pieces (and what each one is for):**
- **Audio storage + CDN** — at upload time, each track is encoded into 4 bitrate variants (96/160/320kbps + FLAC), sliced into ~10-second HLS chunks (HLS = HTTP Live Streaming, a chunked protocol designed for adaptive video/audio delivery), and pushed to a global CDN. The CDN stores the "hot" catalog (top 10% of tracks — about 10 million songs — account for ~90% of streams) at edge nodes worldwide. A play request for a popular song almost never reaches the origin storage — it is served from an edge node a few milliseconds away.
- **Catalog Service** — the metadata layer: track title, artist, album, audio URL pointers, ISRC codes (the unique identifier used for royalty tracking). Accessed on every play but reads are heavily cacheable.
- **Play events (Kafka)** — a partitioned durable log that receives every play heartbeat (roughly 500k events/second at peak). Downstream consumers read from it asynchronously: the royalty aggregator, the recommendation ML trainer, the analytics pipeline. Kafka retains events for 7 days hot; after that they flow to columnar Parquet files on object storage for 7-year royalty compliance.
- **Recommendation engine (offline ML)** — runs as periodic batch jobs (weekly for Discover Weekly, daily for Daily Mixes). Collaborative filtering (finding users with similar taste patterns) plus audio content embeddings (CNNs that compute a 256-dimensional "fingerprint" from the audio waveform itself). Results are precomputed and stored in a cache cluster. When a user opens their Discover Weekly, they read a precomputed list — no ML inference at request time.
- **Royalty Pipeline** — reads play events from Kafka, aggregates by `(track_id, country, date)`, applies per-country licensing rates and complex royalty rules (mechanical vs performance vs sync), and produces payment records for rights holders. Exactly-once semantics here are critical: miscounting streams means overpaying or underpaying artists. The pipeline is idempotent: events carry a UUID so if the same event is replayed it produces the same aggregated count.
- **DRM (Digital Rights Management, e.g. Widevine)** — for offline playback, the client downloads the encrypted audio file plus a time-limited license token (valid for e.g. 30 days without internet). The encrypted file cannot be played without a valid, non-expired license, so offline mode is not a loophole for permanent download.

**The thing that makes it hard:** a new Taylor Swift album drops. Within the first hour, every active user tries to play her latest single simultaneously. The CDN edge nodes have never cached this track — it was just uploaded. Every request falls through to the origin storage, which is suddenly handling 10 million simultaneous requests for the same file. This is called a "cache miss storm" or "CDN cold start." At 320kbps, one stream is ~40KB/s; 10 million streams = 400 GB/s hitting the origin. No object store backend can survive that.

**Why the standard solution works:** CDN pre-warming.
